# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between self-regulating and non-self-regulating processes. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Self-Regulating | Non-Self-Regulating (Integrating) |
|---------|----------------|------------------------------------|
| **Definition** | A process whose output settles to a new finite steady state after a step change in input, due to internal self-correcting physics. | A process whose output continues to change indefinitely (typically a linear ramp) after a step change in input, with no inherent self-correction. |
| **Steady-state** | Reached asymptotically as $t \to \infty$ | Never reached; output grows without bound |
| **Pole location** | All poles in the open left half-plane (LHP) | One pole at the origin $s = 0$ |
| **Industrial example** | Outlet temperature of a heat exchanger | Liquid level in a tank with constant outflow (or boiler drum level) |

*Allocation: 1 mark definition of self-regulating + example; 1 mark definition of non-self-regulating + example; 1 mark for contrast (pole location or steady state).*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is dead time in a process? How does it affect closed-loop control performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Dead time $\theta$ (also called transport delay or time delay) is the time interval between the instant an input change is applied to a process and the instant the output begins to respond. It is caused by transport lag (e.g., material flowing in a pipe, measurement sampling) or pure computational delay. In transfer-function form, it appears as a multiplicative factor $e^{-\theta s}$.

**Effect on control (1.5 marks):**
- Dead time contributes a **pure negative phase shift** $\phi(\omega) = -\omega\theta$ to the loop transfer function, with **no amplitude attenuation**.
- This phase erosion **reduces phase and gain margins**, making the closed loop prone to **oscillation and instability** as $\theta$ increases.
- Empirically, the **maximum achievable controller gain $K_c$** is inversely related to $\theta$; rule of thumb: $\theta/\tau$ ratio dictates how aggressive the loop can be. A ratio above 1 indicates a difficult-to-control loop.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between the two full questions.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**A (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the classification of process behavior based on (i) self-regulating vs non-self-regulating nature and (ii) order of dynamics. Give a representative transfer function for each class. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** A temperature sensor used in a chemical reactor has a first-order dynamics with gain $K = 2.5\,^\circ$C / (% heater power), time constant $\tau = 60$ s, and pure dead time $\theta = 15$ s. The heater is given a step change of $\Delta u = 10\%$ from an initial steady-state temperature of $y_{ss,0} = 120\,^\circ$C. Determine:
  (i) The steady-state change in temperature $\Delta y_{ss}$.
  (ii) The temperature at $t = 75$ s after the step.
  (iii) The time at which the temperature reaches $125\,^\circ$C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — A (a) [7 marks]**

**Classification table (4 marks):**

| Class | Sub-class | Canonical Transfer Function |
|-------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **By steady-state** | Self-regulating | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ (or pure $K$) |
| | Non-self-regulating | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s}$ or $\dfrac{K\, e^{-\theta s}}{s}$ |
| **By dynamic order** | Zero-order | $G(s) = K$ |
| | First-order | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ |
| | First-order + dead time | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ |
| | Second-order (over-damped) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ |
| | Second-order (under-damped) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$, $\zeta < 1$ |
| | Integrating + dead time | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\, e^{-\theta s}}{s}$ |

**Explanation (2 marks):** Self-regulating processes settle to a finite steady state because the underlying physics (e.g., heat loss in a heat exchanger) creates a self-correcting internal feedback. Non-self-regulating processes lack this — every input increment accumulates without bound (pure integration). The order of dynamics counts the **number of independent energy-storage elements**, which determines the number of **poles** of the transfer function.

**Example (1 mark):**
- Self-regulating, first-order + dead time: heat-exchanger outlet temperature vs. steam valve.
- Non-self-regulating, integrating: liquid level in a tank with constant outflow pump.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $K = 2.5\,^\circ$C / %; $\tau = 60$ s; $\theta = 15$ s; $\Delta u = 10\%$; $y_{ss,0} = 120\,^\circ$C; FOPDT model.

**(i) Steady-state temperature change (2 marks):**

$$\Delta y_{ss} = K \cdot \Delta u = 2.5 \times 10 = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$$

New steady-state temperature: $y_{ss} = 120 + 25 = 145\,^\circ$C.

**(ii) Temperature at $t = 75$ s (3 marks):**

Since $t = 75$ s $> \theta = 15$ s, we use the FOPDT step response:

$$y(t) = y_{ss,0} + K \Delta u \left( 1 - e^{-(t-\theta)/\tau} \right)$$

Compute $(t - \theta)/\tau$:

$$\frac{t - \theta}{\tau} = \frac{75 - 15}{60} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0$$

Substitute:

$$y(75) = 120 + 2.5 \times 10 \times \left( 1 - e^{-1.0} \right)$$

$$y(75) = 120 + 25 \times \left( 1 - 0.3679 \right) = 120 + 25 \times 0.6321$$

$$y(75) = 120 + 15.80 = 135.80\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**(iii) Time to reach $125\,^\circ$C (2 marks):**

Set $y(t) = 125\,^\circ$C and solve for $t$ (note that $t > \theta$ is required):

$$125 = 120 + 25 \left( 1 - e^{-(t-15)/60} \right)$$

$$\frac{5}{25} = 1 - e^{-(t-15)/60} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 0.2 = 1 - e^{-(t-15)/60}$$

$$e^{-(t-15)/60} = 0.8 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -\frac{t - 15}{60} = \ln(0.8) = -0.2231$$

$$t - 15 = 60 \times 0.2231 = 13.39 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{t \approx 28.39\text{ s}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2024]**

**B (a)** Derive the step response of a first-order plus dead time (FOPDT) process with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$. Sketch the response and label the parameters $K$, $\tau$, and $\theta$ on it. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** For a process whose open-loop step response is approximated as a SOPDT with $K = 0.8$, $\tau_1 = 20$ s, $\tau_2 = 80$ s (over-damped), determine:
  (i) The transfer function.
  (ii) The final steady-state output for a unit step input.
  (iii) The time to reach $90\%$ of the final value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — B (a) [7 marks]**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1** — Output in Laplace domain for step input $U(s) = \Delta u / s$:

$$Y(s) = \frac{K e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{\Delta u}{s}$$

**Step 2** — Separate the dead-time factor:

$$Y(s) = e^{-\theta s} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{K \Delta u}{s(\tau s + 1)}}_{\text{call this } F(s)}$$

**Step 3** — Partial-fraction expand $F(s)$:

$$\frac{K \Delta u}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{K \Delta u}{s} - \frac{K \Delta u \tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 4** — Inverse Laplace (without dead time):

$$f(t) = K \Delta u \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 5** — Apply the time-shift property $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-\theta s}F(s)\} = f(t-\theta)\,u(t-\theta)$:

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = \begin{cases} y_{ss,0}, & 0 \le t < \theta \\[4pt] y_{ss,0} + K \Delta u \left( 1 - e^{-(t-\theta)/\tau} \right), & t \ge \theta \end{cases}\,}$$

**Sketch and labelling (2 marks):**

A correctly drawn curve must show:
- A flat region from $t = 0$ to $t = \theta$ (this is the **dead time**).
- A rising exponential from $t = \theta$ onwards.
- The total rise $\Delta y_{ss} = K \Delta u$ between $y_{ss,0}$ and the final asymptote.
- A point on the curve at $t = \theta + \tau$ where the response has reached $63.2\%$ of $\Delta y_{ss}$ (this is the **time constant $\tau$**).

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** SOPDT, $K = 0.8$, $\tau_1 = 20$ s, $\tau_2 = 80$ s, over-damped, unit step input.

**(i) Transfer function (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{K}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} = \frac{0.8}{(20 s + 1)(80 s + 1)}\,}$$

**(ii) Steady-state output for unit step (2 marks):**

By the Final Value Theorem:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot G(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \frac{0.8}{(1)(1)} = 0.8$$

**(iii) Time to reach 90 % of final value (3 marks):**

Over-damped SOPDT step response (sum of two exponentials):

$$y(t) = K \left[ 1 - \frac{\tau_1}{\tau_1 - \tau_2} e^{-t/\tau_1} + \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1 - \tau_2} e^{-t/\tau_2} \right]$$

With $\tau_1 - \tau_2 = 20 - 80 = -60$ s:

$$y(t) = 0.8 \left[ 1 - \frac{20}{-60} e^{-t/20} + \frac{80}{-60} e^{-t/80} \right] = 0.8 \left[ 1 + \tfrac{1}{3} e^{-t/20} - \tfrac{4}{3} e^{-t/80} \right]$$

Set $y(t) = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$:

$$\frac{0.72}{0.8} = 0.9 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{3} e^{-t/20} - \tfrac{4}{3} e^{-t/80}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{3} e^{-t/20} - \tfrac{4}{3} e^{-t/80} = -0.1$$

$$e^{-t/20} - 4 e^{-t/80} = -0.3$$

Solving numerically (iterative substitution or Newton's method) yields:

$$t \approx 39.4 \text{ s}$$

(Acceptable answer range: **38 s ≤ t ≤ 41 s**; full credit if the iterative method is shown.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Process-Behavior Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $y_{ss,0}$** — Students often write only the *deviation* form $K\Delta u(1-e^{-t/\tau})$ and lose **1–2 marks** because the absolute output is what the system actually shows.
> 2. **Mixing up $\tau$ with $4\tau$ or $5\tau$** — $\tau$ is the **63.2%** time, not the settling time. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Dead-time units** — Always quote $\theta$ in the **same time units** as $\tau$. Mixing seconds and minutes loses a mark.
> 4. **Integrating process step response** — A common mistake is to write $y(t) = K\Delta u$ (constant). The correct answer is a **linear ramp** $y(t) = y_0 + K\Delta u \cdot t$.
> 5. **Sign of phase lag from dead time** — $\phi(\omega) = -\omega\theta$ is **negative**, not positive. Mixing this up will invalidate any stability-margin argument.
> 6. **Pole classification** — A self-regulating process has all poles strictly in the LHP; "marginally stable" applies **only** to the pure integrator. Do not write "stable" for an integrator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **process** is a system with three variable classes: **MV** ($u$), **CV** ($y$), and **DV** ($d$).
- [x] **Self-regulating** processes reach a finite steady state; **non-self-regulating** (integrating) processes do not.
- [x] The **FOPDT model** $G(s) = \dfrac{K e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ describes ~90 % of industrial loops.
- [x] **Process gain $K$** = $\Delta y_{ss} / \Delta u$ (steady-state sensitivity).
- [x] **Time constant $\tau$** = time to reach **63.2 %** of $\Delta y_{ss}$ from the moment the response begins.
- [x] **Dead time $\theta$** = time before the output first responds; contributes pure **negative phase** $\phi = -\omega\theta$.
- [x] **Settling time** ≈ $4\tau$ (2 %) or $5\tau$ (0.5 %).
- [x] **Integrator step response** is a **linear ramp** $y(t) = y_0 + K\Delta u \cdot t$.
- [x] **% Overshoot** of an under-damped second-order system $= 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- [x] In **multivariable** systems, the process is a **transfer-function matrix** $\mathbf{G}(s)$; diagonal $G_{ii}$ = direct path, off-diagonal $G_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$) = interaction path.
- [x] **Padé approximation** of $e^{-\theta s} \approx (1 - \theta s/2)/(1 + \theta s/2)$ allows analytical controller design despite the irrational dead-time term.
- [x] The **step test** is the standard plant-side method to identify $K$, $\tau$, and $\theta$ from open-loop response data.
- [x] The ratio $\theta / \tau$ is a key **controllability index** — large $\theta / \tau$ means a difficult, sluggish loop.
- [x] **Linear** processes obey superposition and homogeneity; **non-linear** processes do not — non-linearity is the primary reason for **gain-scheduling** and **adaptive** control.
- [x] **FOPDT step response** with $y_{ss,0}$ included: $y(t) = y_{ss,0} + K\Delta u \left[ 1 - e^{-(t-\theta)/\tau} \right]$ for $t \ge \theta$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat, labelled step-response sketch** — examiners award up to **2 marks** for a clear, correctly-annotated figure even if the algebra has a minor error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Aims
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Control Aims # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Control Aims In the context of **Multivariable Control Systems** (MIMO – Multiple Input Multiple Output), **Control Aims** refer to the well-defined, mathematically tractable objectives that a control engineer seeks to achieve when designing a controller for a system possessing more than one input and more than one output. For a generic **LTI (Linear Time-Invariant) MIMO plant** described by the state-space model: $$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$ $$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$ where $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $u \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $y \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ (with $m, p > 1$), the control aims are the **engineering specifications** that dictate the choice of feedback law $u = -Kx + r$ (or a dynamic compensator) such that the closed-loop system satisfies stability, performance, and robustness criteria simultaneously. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > For Module 1, the university expects students to clearly **distinguish** between the aims of SISO and MIMO control, and to identify **why classical (loop-by-loop) control fails** for strongly coupled multivariable plants, justifying the need for centralized multivariable design. ## 1.2 The Three Primary Control Aims The control aims in a multivariable setting are conventionally classified into three hierarchical categories: 1. **Stability (AIM-1)** – The foremost, non-negotiable aim. The closed-loop system must exhibit **asymptotic stability** of its internal states and **bounded-input bounded-output (BIBO) stability** at every output channel. 2. **Performance (AIM-2)** – Quantitative time-domain and frequency-domain specifications such as rise time, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error, and disturbance rejection. 3. **Robustness (AIM-3)** – The ability of the closed-loop system to maintain Stability and Performance in the presence of **plant uncertainty** (parametric perturbations, unmodelled dynamics, or disturbances). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Piloting a Modern Fighter Jet vs. Driving a Bicycle** Imagine controlling a **bicycle** (a SISO-like system): one steering input controls one forward motion. The "aim" is simply to stay upright and reach a destination — a single objective. Now consider piloting a **modern fighter jet** (a true multivariable system): you simultaneously manipulate the **aileron, elevator, rudder, and throttle** (4 inputs) to control **roll, pitch, yaw, and forward speed** (4 outputs). A roll command, however, *inevitably couples* into yaw (adverse yaw). The control aims are no longer trivial: - **Stay flying** (Stability) – even under turbulence, - **Hit the target with precision** (Performance) – track aggressive maneuvers, - **Survive damage / icing / sensor failure** (Robustness) – keep flying despite the plant changing. Just as the pilot relies on a **fly-by-wire computer** to decouple, stabilize, and optimize these intertwined behaviors, the control engineer designs a **multivariable compensator** $K(s)$ to translate raw pilot commands into coordinated, decoupled actuator actions that meet all three aims simultaneously. ## 1.4 Fundamental Distinction: SISO vs. MIMO Control Aims | Aspect | SISO Control Aim | MIMO Control Aim | |---|---|---| | Number of objectives | One scalar tracking objective | Vector-valued tracking, plus **interaction management** | | Coupling concern | None (single loop) | **Loop interaction / directionality** is central | | Design tool | Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus | State-space, Polynomial matrix, $\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$, $\mathcal{H}_2$ | | Primary challenge | Achieving bandwidth / phase margin | Achieving bandwidth + **decoupling** + directionality | | Stability check | Characteristic polynomial of scalar TF | Eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ (matrix) + transmission zeros | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** In MIMO systems, the control aims are **not merely N copies of SISO aims stacked together**. The *interactions* (off-diagonal terms of the plant transfer matrix $G(s)$) introduce a fundamentally new aim: **Directionality / Decoupling Control**. ## 1.5 The Engineering Trade-off Triangle The three aims form a **fundamental trade-off triangle**, often called the **"Robust Performance Triangle"**: - Higher **performance** (faster response) → typically reduces **robustness** margins. - Higher **robustness** (conservative design) → typically reduces achievable **performance**. - Both extreme performance and extreme robustness can jeopardize **stability** if naively pursued. The art (and science) of multivariable control design is to find the **optimal point on the trade-off surface** dictated by the application. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Control Aims ### AIM-1: STABILITY (The "Must-Have") Stability is the **sine qua non** of any control system. In multivariable control, two distinct notions of stability must be guaranteed: **A. Internal Stability (State-Space Sense):** The closed-loop state matrix $A_{cl} = A - BK$ (for state feedback $u = -Kx$) must have all eigenvalues in the open left-half plane: $$\text{Re}\{\lambda_i(A_{cl})\} < 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ This is equivalent to $A_{cl}$ being a **Hurwitz matrix**. **B. Input-Output Stability (BIBO):** The closed-loop transfer function matrix $T(s)$ from exogenous inputs (references, disturbances) to regulated outputs must be **proper and stable** — meaning all poles of every element $T_{ij}(s)$ lie in $\text{Re}(s) < 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Internal Stability matters in MIMO:** For SISO, internal stability and I/O stability are equivalent. For **MIMO**, a system can be BIBO stable yet have **hidden unstable pole-zero cancellations**, leading to *internal instability* (states blow up even if outputs look bounded). The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this distinction. ### AIM-2: PERFORMANCE (The "How-Good") Performance is quantified by: **1. Time-Domain Specifications** (for a unit step at reference $r_i$): - **Rise time** $t_r$: time to go from 10% to 90% of final value. - **Settling time** $t_s$ (2% or 5% criterion): time to remain within a band of the final value. - **Overshoot** $M_p$: maximum percentage peak above the final value. - **Steady-state error** $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$. **2. Frequency-Domain Specifications:** - **Bandwidth** $\omega_B$: frequency at which the magnitude of the sensitivity function crosses $-3\,\text{dB}$ from below. - **Resonant peak** $M_r$: maximum of the closed-loop frequency response magnitude. - **Crossover frequency** $\omega_c$: open-loop gain-crossover frequency. **3. The Sensitivity Function Framework:** Define the **sensitivity function** $S(s)$ and **complementary sensitivity** $T(s)$: $$S(s) = [I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$ $$T(s) = G(s)K(s)[I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1} = I - S(s)$$ The fundamental identity (algebraic constraint): $$S(s) + T(s) = I$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** $|S(j\omega)|$ small ⇒ good disturbance rejection and small tracking error. $|T(j\omega)|$ small ⇒ good noise rejection. The constraint $S+T=I$ means you **cannot make both small at the same frequency** — the source of the performance–robustness trade-off. ### AIM-3: ROBUSTNESS (The "Survivability") Robustness is the **insensitivity** of stability and performance to **plant uncertainty**. Uncertainty is modelled either as: - **Additive:** $G_p(s) = G(s) + \Delta_a(s), \quad \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_a(j\omega)) \leq \ell_a(\omega)$ - **Multiplicative (output):** $G_p(s) = [I + \Delta_m(s)]G(s), \quad \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_m(j\omega)) \leq \ell_m(\omega)$ - **Inverse multiplicative (input):** $G_p(s) = G(s)[I + \Delta_i(s)]$ **Robust Stability Criterion (Small Gain Theorem for MIMO):** For a closed loop with multiplicative uncertainty $\Delta_m$, robust stability is guaranteed if: $$\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] \cdot \ell_m(\omega) < 1, \quad \forall \omega$$ where $\bar{\sigma}(\cdot)$ denotes the **maximum singular value**. **Nominal Performance Criterion:** $$\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)] < \vert W_P(j\omega) \vert^{-1}, \quad \forall \omega$$ where $W_P(s)$ is a performance weighting function chosen by the designer. **Robust Performance Criterion (Combined):** $$\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)]\,\vert W_P(j\omega) \vert + \bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)]\,\ell_m(\omega) < 1, \quad \forall \omega$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | State equation (MIMO LTI) | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | | 2 | Output equation | $y = Cx + Du$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ | | 3 | State-feedback law | $u = -Kx + r$ | $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | | 4 | Closed-loop $A$-matrix | $A_{cl} = A - BK$ | Hurwitz condition | | 5 | Stability condition | $\text{Re}\{\lambda_i(A_{cl})\} < 0$ | $\forall i$ | | 6 | Sensitivity function | $S = (I+GK)^{-1}$ | Matrix of dimension $p \times p$ | | 7 | Complementary sensitivity | $T = GK(I+GK)^{-1}$ | Matrix of dimension $p \times p$ | | 8 | Identity constraint | $S + T = I$ | Algebraic, $\forall s$ | | 9 | Nominal performance | $\bar{\sigma}(S(j\omega)) < \vert W_P^{-1}(j\omega) \vert$ | $\forall \omega$ | | 10 | Robust stability | $\bar{\sigma}(T(j\omega)) < \vert \ell_m^{-1}(j\omega) \vert$ | $\forall \omega$ | | 11 | Robust performance | $\bar{\sigma}(S)\vert W_P\vert + \bar{\sigma}(T)\ell_m < 1$ | $\forall \omega$ | | 12 | Singular value definition | $\bar{\sigma}(A) = \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(A^H A)}$ | Non-negative scalar | | 13 | Condition number | $\kappa(G) = \bar{\sigma}(G)/\underline{\sigma}(G) \geq 1$ | $\kappa = 1$ ⇒ isotropic | | 14 | RHP-zeros count | McMillan degree of $S$ | $N_S = N_G + N_K$ in closed RHP | | 15 | Decoupling aim | $G(s)K(s) = \text{diag}\{g_i(s)\}$ | Ideal decoupling | | 16 | Internal stability (MIMO) | No hidden unstable modes | $\nexists$ unstable cancellation | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Dominant Control Aim | Why? | |---|---|---| | Aircraft autopilot | Robustness + decoupling | Model uncertain, channels coupled | | Chemical process control (distillation column) | Performance + decoupling | Strong interaction between trays | | Robotic manipulator | Performance + tracking | Precise trajectory following needed | | Power system stabilizer | Stability + disturbance rejection | Generator oscillations must damp quickly | | Hard-disk-drive servo | Performance (high bandwidth) | Track density increases → faster seeking | | Automotive engine control | Robustness (operating range wide) | Plant varies enormously with RPM/load | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity Function $S(s)$ and the Identity $S + T = I$ We start from the classical **negative feedback interconnection** of a multivariable plant $G(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ and a compensator $K(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}(s)$. The closed-loop relationship from exogenous reference $r(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ to error $e(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is: $$e(s) = r(s) - y(s)$$ The controller output is: $$u(s) = K(s) \, e(s)$$ The plant output is: $$y(s) = G(s) \, u(s)$$ Substituting step-by-step: **Step 1:** Substitute $u$ into $y$: $$y(s) = G(s) K(s) \, e(s) = G(s) K(s) [r(s) - y(s)]$$ **Step 2:** Group the $y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side: $$y(s) + G(s) K(s) \, y(s) = G(s) K(s) \, r(s)$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $y(s)$ using the matrix identity (assuming $I + GK$ is invertible): $$[I_p + G(s) K(s)] \, y(s) = G(s) K(s) \, r(s)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $y(s)/r(s)$ (the closed-loop transfer function): $$T(s) \triangleq \frac{y(s)}{r(s)} = [I + GK]^{-1} \, GK$$ **Step 5:** Use the matrix identity $[I + GK]^{-1}GK = I - [I+GK]^{-1}$ (valid for any compatible square matrices — a consequence of the Woodbury-like manipulation). Derive this: $$I = [I+GK]^{-1}[I+GK] = [I+GK]^{-1} + [I+GK]^{-1}GK$$ $$\Rightarrow \quad [I+GK]^{-1}GK = I - [I+GK]^{-1}$$ **Step 6:** Define the sensitivity function $S(s)$ and complementary sensitivity $T(s)$: $$S(s) \triangleq [I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$ $$T(s) \triangleq G(s)K(s)[I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$ **Step 7:** Therefore, by direct addition: $$S(s) + T(s) = [I+GK]^{-1} + GK[I+GK]^{-1} = [I + GK][I+GK]^{-1} = I_p$$ $$\boxed{\,S(s) + T(s) = I_p\,}$$ This is the **fundamental multivariable identity** on which the entire $\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$ and $\mathcal{H}_2$ robust-control frameworks are built. ## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Stability via Characteristic Polynomial For a **square** MIMO plant $G(s) = N(s)D(s)^{-1}$ (right-coprime factorization), the closed-loop characteristic polynomial is: $$p_{cl}(s) = \det[D(s) + N(s)K(s)]$$ Closed-loop stability requires: $$\boxed{\,\det[D(s) + N(s)K(s)] \neq 0 \quad \forall s : \text{Re}(s) \geq 0\,}$$ Equivalently, for the state-space form, the **eigenvalues of $A - BK$** must all lie strictly in the open left half-plane. ## 3.3 Derivation: Robust Stability Margin under Multiplicative Uncertainty **Setup:** The true (uncertain) plant is $G_p(s) = [I + \Delta_m(s)] G(s)$, with $\bar{\sigma}[\Delta_m(j\omega)] \leq \ell_m(\omega)$. **Nyquist-based generalization:** In SISO, the Nyquist plot of $L(s) = GK$ must stay outside a disk of radius $\ell_m$ around $-1$. In MIMO, the analogous condition is that the **image of the Nyquist contour** under $L(j\omega)$ does not encircle $-I$ after "fattening" by the uncertainty disc. **Small-gain derivation:** The closed-loop matrix $[I + GK(I+\Delta_m)]^{-1}$ exists (closed loop stable) if and only if: $$\det[I + G_p K] \neq 0 \quad \forall \, \Delta_m \text{ with } \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_m) \leq \ell_m$$ Using the identity $I + G_pK = I + GK + GK\Delta_m$ and the matrix determinant lemma: $$\det[I + G_pK] = \det[I+GK] \cdot \det[I + (I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m]$$ The second factor vanishes (instability) iff $-1$ is an eigenvalue of $(I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m$. Hence: $$\rho[(I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m] \geq 1 \quad \text{(instability)}$$ Applying the spectral-radius-vs-singular-value bound $\rho(\cdot) \leq \bar{\sigma}(\cdot)$: $$\bar{\sigma}[(I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m] \leq \bar{\sigma}(T) \cdot \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_m) \leq \bar{\sigma}(T) \ell_m$$ For NO instability, we require this product be strictly less than 1 for all $\omega$: $$\boxed{\,\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] \cdot \ell_m(\omega) < 1, \quad \forall \omega \in [0, \infty)\,}$$ This is the **MIMO robust stability margin** condition. The KTU examiner loves testing this exact derivation. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Aims to Design Tools | Control Aim | Primary Design Variable | Frequency-Domain Quantity | State-Space Tool | |---|---|---|---| | Internal stability | Eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ | $N_S$ = RHP poles of $S$ | Lyapunov equation $A_{cl}^T P + P A_{cl} = -Q$ | | Nominal performance | Closed-loop bandwidth | $\bar{\sigma}(S) < \vert W_P^{-1}\vert$ | LQR / $\mathcal{H}_2$ synthesis | | Robust stability | Stability margin | $\bar{\sigma}(T) < \vert \ell_m^{-1}\vert$ | Riccati-equation based $\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$ | | Robust performance | Combined bound | $\bar{\sigma}(S)\vert W_P\vert + \bar{\sigma}(T)\ell_m < 1$ | Mixed $\mathcal{H}_2/\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$ | | Decoupling | Off-diagonal $GK$ | $\kappa(GK) \to 1$ | Inverse-based / pseudo-inverse design | | Disturbance rejection | $\bar{\sigma}(S)$ at low $\omega$ | $\bar{\sigma}(S) \to 0$ as $\omega \to 0$ | Integral action / LQI | | Noise rejection | $\bar{\sigma}(T)$ at high $\omega$ | $\bar{\sigma}(T) \to 0$ as $\omega \to \infty$ | Roll-off weighting in $K$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Hierarchy of Control Aims ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Multivariable Control Design Objective] --> A1[Aim 1: Stability] A0 --> A2[Aim 2: Performance] A0 --> A3[Aim 3: Robustness] A1 --> A1a[Internal Stability
Hurwitz Acl matrix] A1 --> A1b[BIBO Stability
No unstable pole-zero cancellation] A2 --> A2a[Time-Domain Specs
tr, ts, Mp, ess] A2 --> A2b[Frequency-Domain Specs
Bandwidth, Mr, crossover] A2 --> A2c[Decoupling
kappa GK approaches 1] A3 --> A3a[Robust Stability
sigma bar T less than bound] A3 --> A3b[Robust Performance
sigma bar S Wp plus sigma bar T lm less than 1] A3 --> A3c[Disturbance and Noise Rejection
Low frequency and High frequency design] A1a -. tradeoff .-> A3a A2a -. tradeoff .-> A3c A2b -. tradeoff .-> A3b ``` ## 4.2 MIMO Negative Feedback Interconnection ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference r of dimension p] --> SUM[ Summing Junction
e = r minus y ] SUM --> K[Compensator K of s
dimension m by p ] K --> U[Control signal u
dimension m ] U --> G[Plant G of s
dimension p by m ] G --> Y[Output y
dimension p ] Y --> SUM Y --> PERF[Performance
Evaluation Block] SUM --> SENS[Sensitivity S equals I plus GK inverse] G --> TCOMP[Complementary T equals GK I plus GK inverse] PERF --> AIM1[Aim 1: Stability] PERF --> AIM2[Aim 2: Performance] PERF --> AIM3[Aim 3: Robustness] ``` ## 4.3 The Robust Performance Trade-off Triangle ```mermaid flowchart TD STAB[Stability
lambda Acl in LHP] --> TRADE{Trade-off
Surface} PERF[Performance
bar sigma S small at low omega] --> TRADE ROB[Robustness
bar sigma T small at high omega] --> TRADE TRADE --> IDENTITY[Fundamental Constraint
S plus T equals I] IDENTITY --> NOTE[Cannot make both S and T small
at the same frequency] TRADE --> DESIGN[Optimal Compensator K of s
via H-infinity or H-2 synthesis] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Flow: From Specifications to Compensator ```mermaid flowchart TD SPEC[Engineering Specifications
from plant and application] --> DECOMP[Decompose into three aims] DECOMP --> STABGOAL[Stability Goals
eigenvalue placement
Hurwitz check] DECOMP --> PERFGOAL[Performance Goals
weight Wp of s selection] DECOMP --> ROBGOAL[Robustness Goals
uncertainty model and weight lm of s] STABGOAL --> MODEL[Unified Mathematical Model
G of s, Wp, lm, and K of s] PERFGOAL --> MODEL ROBGOAL --> MODEL MODEL --> SYNTH[Synthesis Method Selection
H-infinity, H-2, LQR, or LQG] SYNTH --> COMP[Realized Compensator K of s] COMP --> VERIFY[Verification via Simulation
and Frequency Sweep] VERIFY --> CHECK{Meets all three aims?} CHECK --> YES[Design Complete] CHECK -. no .-> TUNE[Reiterate Weight Selection
and go back to Synthesis] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **"List and briefly explain the three primary control aims in multivariable control system design."** **Model Answer:** The three primary control aims in multivariable control system design are: 1. **Stability:** The closed-loop system must be internally stable — all eigenvalues of the closed-loop state matrix $A_{cl} = A - BK$ must lie strictly in the open left half of the complex plane ($\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$). Additionally, there must be **no unstable pole-zero cancellations** between the plant and compensator (a critical MIMO-specific concern). 2. **Performance:** The closed-loop system must meet time-domain specifications (rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$, overshoot $M_p$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$) and frequency-domain specifications (bandwidth $\omega_B$, resonant peak $M_r$). In MIMO systems, this includes **decoupling** — minimizing interaction between input-output channels. 3. **Robustness:** The closed-loop system must maintain stability and performance in the presence of plant uncertainty (parametric variations, unmodelled dynamics). This is quantified by the **robust stability** condition $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] \cdot \ell_m(\omega) < 1$ and **robust performance** condition $\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)]\vert W_P \vert + \bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)]\ell_m < 1$. > [!WARNING] > **Common 3-Mark Loss:** Writing only two aims (e.g., omitting robustness) or failing to state the *mathematical condition* for each aim. Always back up the verbal definition with one formula or matrix inequality. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **"Define the sensitivity function $S(s)$ and the complementary sensitivity function $T(s)$ for a MIMO system, and state the algebraic identity relating them."** **Model Answer:** For a multivariable plant $G(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ in negative feedback with compensator $K(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}(s)$: - **Sensitivity function:** $S(s) \triangleq [I_p + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$ - **Complementary sensitivity function:** $T(s) \triangleq G(s)K(s)[I_p + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$ The fundamental algebraic identity is: $$S(s) + T(s) = I_p$$ This identity is the **mathematical root of the performance–robustness trade-off** in multivariable control: making $|S|$ small (for good tracking/disturbance rejection) forces $|T|$ to be close to $I$, which compromises robustness margins; conversely, making $|T|$ small (for noise rejection and robustness) forces $|S|$ close to $I$, worsening tracking. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style] **"With suitable block diagrams, explain the three primary control aims (stability, performance, robustness) in multivariable control system design. Derive the condition for robust stability under multiplicative output uncertainty and discuss how it links the sensitivity functions $S$ and $T$."** **Distribution:** Part (a) — 7 marks | Part (b) — 7 marks. #### Part (a) — Explanation with Block Diagram (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The three primary control aims in a MIMO control system are explained using the standard negative feedback structure (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2): **1. Stability (AIM-1):** The foremost aim. The closed-loop system matrix $A_{cl} = A - BK$ must be **Hurwitz**, i.e., all eigenvalues satisfy $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$. For the transfer-function viewpoint, the matrix $I_p + G(s)K(s)$ must be **non-singular in the closed right-half plane**, and there must be **no unstable pole-zero cancellations** between $G(s)$ and $K(s)$ (a *MIMO-specific* concern, since cancellations can be hidden in non-diagonal elements). **2. Performance (AIM-2):** Quantified by the **sensitivity function** $S(s) = [I+GK]^{-1}$. The smaller $\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)]$ in the low-frequency band, the better the tracking and disturbance rejection. Specifications include: - Time-domain: rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$, overshoot $M_p$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$. - Frequency-domain: closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_B$ (defined by $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega_B)] = 1/\sqrt{2}$). - MIMO-specific: **decoupling** of input-output channels (loop interaction suppression), quantified by the **condition number** $\kappa(GK) = \bar{\sigma}(GK)/\underline{\sigma}(GK)$. **3. Robustness (AIM-3):** Insensitivity to plant uncertainty, quantified via the **complementary sensitivity** $T(s) = GK[I+GK]^{-1}$. Smaller $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)]$ in the high-frequency band gives larger stability margins against unmodelled dynamics. **The Fundamental Identity (linking all three):** $$S(s) + T(s) = I_p \quad \forall s$$ This single algebraic constraint is the **mathematical expression** of why the three aims cannot be simultaneously maximized — they are coupled through this identity. #### Part (b) — Derivation of Robust Stability Condition (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Model the uncertain plant with multiplicative output uncertainty as: $$G_p(s) = [I_p + \Delta_m(s)]G(s)$$ where $\Delta_m(s)$ is an unknown stable perturbation matrix bounded in singular value by: $$\bar{\sigma}[\Delta_m(j\omega)] \leq \ell_m(\omega)$$ **Step 2:** The closed-loop with the uncertain plant becomes: $$y(s) = G_p(s)K(s)[I + G_p(s)K(s)]^{-1}r(s)$$ **Step 3:** The closed-loop is internally stable if and only if the matrix $I + G_p K$ is non-singular for all admissible $\Delta_m$. Using the matrix determinant lemma: $$\det[I + G_pK] = \det[I + (I+GK\Delta_m)GK] = \det[I + GK] \cdot \det[I + (I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m]$$ **Step 4:** The closed loop becomes unstable when the second determinant vanishes, i.e., when $-1$ is an eigenvalue of $(I+GK)^{-1}GK\Delta_m = T(s)\Delta_m(s)$. Applying the bound $\rho(AB) \leq \bar{\sigma}(A)\bar{\sigma}(B)$: $$\rho[T\Delta_m] \leq \bar{\sigma}(T)\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_m) \leq \bar{\sigma}(T)\ell_m(\omega)$$ **Step 5:** For robust stability, we require $\rho[T\Delta_m] < 1$ for all $\omega$: $$\boxed{\,\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] \cdot \ell_m(\omega) < 1, \quad \forall \omega \in [0, \infty)\,}$$ This is the **MIMO robust stability condition** — the direct generalization of the SISO condition $|T(j\omega)| < 1/\ell_m(\omega)$. **Step 6: Connection to $S$ and $T$:** Using the identity $T = I - S$, robustness can equivalently be phrased as a bound on $S$. The complete design goal — **robust performance** — combines both: $$\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)]\,\vert W_P(j\omega) \vert + \bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)]\,\ell_m(\omega) < 1, \quad \forall \omega$$ The first term enforces nominal performance; the second enforces robust stability; their sum < 1 guarantees **robust performance**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) **"Discuss the difference between internal stability and BIBO stability in multivariable systems. Why is internal stability a stronger requirement? With reference to the closed-loop transfer matrix, derive the condition under which hidden instability can occur in a $2 \times 2$ MIMO system, and explain how the McMillan degree of $S(s)$ captures this."** **Distribution:** Part (a) — 7 marks | Part (b) — 7 marks. #### Part (a) — Internal vs BIBO Stability (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **BIBO Stability (Input-Output Stability):** A system with transfer matrix $G(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input $u(t)$ produces a bounded output $y(t)$. Mathematically, this is equivalent to: **every element $G_{ij}(s)$ of the transfer matrix must have all its poles in the open left half-plane** ($\text{Re}(s) < 0$). **Internal Stability:** A feedback interconnection is **internally stable** if, starting from *any* initial state of every subsystem (plant, compensator, and any sensor/actuator dynamics), the resulting state trajectories remain bounded as $t \to \infty$. For state-space systems, this reduces to the **Hurwitz condition** on the closed-loop state matrix $A_{cl}$. **Why Internal is Stronger (MIMO-Specific Issue):** For SISO systems, the two notions are equivalent. For MIMO, they differ because of the possibility of **hidden unstable pole-zero cancellations**. A BIBO-stable transfer matrix can have **Right-Half-Plane zeros** that cancel with **RHP poles** of the compensator (or vice versa). The cancellation is *invisible* in $G(s)$ (since poles and zeros cancel in the scalar elements after matrix reduction), but the unstable mode persists in the *internal state*. When the input excites a direction aligned with the unstable mode, the internal state grows without bound even though the output remains bounded. **Example (classical):** The $2 \times 2$ plant: $$G(s) = \frac{1}{(s-1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s-1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ The plant has a RHP pole at $s=1$ that is *not visible* in the individual transfer-function elements after the matrix reduction (due to the RHP zero at $s=1$ in the first column). A naive compensator $K(s)$ that cancels this pole–zero pair would yield a BIBO-stable closed loop with **internally unstable** states. #### Part (b) — Derivation: McMillan Degree of $S(s)$ and Hidden Instability (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Recall the sensitivity function: $$S(s) = [I_p + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the determinant: $$\det S(s) = \frac{1}{\det[I + G(s)K(s)]}$$ **Step 3:** The poles of $S(s)$ are the **closed-loop poles** of the system. Express $G = N D^{-1}$ (right-coprime factorization). Then: $$S = [I + N D^{-1} K]^{-1} = [D + N K]^{-1} D$$ The poles of $S$ are the roots of $\det[D(s) + N(s)K(s)]$, i.e., the closed-loop characteristic polynomial. **Step 4 — Key MIMO Insight:** $S(s)$ can have *more* poles than the plant $G(s)$ alone, because of the **coprime factorization structure**. The number of closed-loop poles (i.e., the McMillan degree of $S$) is: $$N_S = N_G + N_K$$ where $N_G$ is the McMillan degree of $G$ and $N_K$ is the McMillan degree of $K$. If the plant has a RHP pole-zero cancellation hidden in the coprime factors (i.e., $N$ and $D$ share a RHP root that the designer has neglected), then $N_S$ will *not equal* the number of finite closed-loop eigenvalues observed in the eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ — and the missing modes are the hidden unstable ones. **Step 5: Test for Internal Stability:** The closed loop is **internally stable if and only if** $S(s)$ has **no poles in the closed right half-plane** (including the point at infinity, which enforces properness). Equivalently: $$\boxed{\,N_S^{\text{RHP}} = 0 \quad \text{(no RHP poles of } S(s)\text{)}\,}$$ If $N_S^{\text{RHP}} > 0$, the system is *not* internally stable, even if every individual transfer-function element of the closed loop looks stable. **Practical Takeaway (KTU 2024 Emphasis):** Always check internal stability by computing the **McMillan degree of $S(s)$** (i.e., the degree of $\det[I+GK]$ as a rational function) and confirming it equals the expected number of closed-loop poles from the state dimension of plant + compensator, **with none in the closed RHP**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do not** equate BIBO stability with internal stability for MIMO — this is the single most common conceptual error and costs 3–4 marks immediately. > 2. **Do not** forget to mention that the McMillan degree of $S$ is the *closed-loop pole count*, not the open-loop plant pole count. > 3. When asked to "derive," you **must** show the matrix determinant lemma step ($\det S = 1/\det[I+GK]$) — verbal descriptions alone get only 50% credit. > 4. For the trade-off question, always write the **identity $S+T=I$** explicitly; never just say "they are related." ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Three aims = Stability, Performance, Robustness** — in that order of priority; stability is non-negotiable. - [ ] **Sensitivity function** $S = (I+GK)^{-1}$ quantifies *performance* (low-frequency tracking + disturbance rejection). - [ ] **Complementary sensitivity** $T = GK(I+GK)^{-1}$ quantifies *robustness* and noise rejection. - [ ] **Fundamental identity** $S + T = I_p$ is the algebraic source of the **performance–robustness trade-off** — never violate it in design. - [ ] **Internal stability** in MIMO is **stronger** than BIBO stability — always check for **hidden unstable pole-zero cancellations** using the McMillan degree of $S(s)$. - [ ] **Hurwitz condition** for state feedback: $\text{Re}\{\lambda_i(A - BK)\} < 0$ for all $i$. - [ ] **Robust stability** under multiplicative output uncertainty: $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] \cdot \ell_m(\omega) < 1, \forall \omega$. - [ ] **Nominal performance**: $\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)] < \vert W_P(j\omega) \vert^{-1}, \forall \omega$. - [ ] **Robust performance** (the combined condition): $\bar{\sigma}(S)\vert W_P \vert + \bar{\sigma}(T)\ell_m < 1, \forall \omega$. - [ ] **Decoupling** is a *MIMO-specific* aim quantified by the **condition number** $\kappa(G) = \bar{\sigma}(G)/\underline{\sigma}(G) \geq 1$; perfect decoupling $\Leftrightarrow \kappa = 1$. - [ ] $\bar{\sigma}(\cdot)$ = **maximum singular value** = $\sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(A^H A)}$ — the MIMO generalization of magnitude. - [ ] In KTU exam answers, **always** pair a verbal definition with one **formula** and (where possible) a **block-diagram or mermaid** reference. - [ ] Remember the **two-stage internal-stability test**: (i) $I + GK$ non-singular on closed RHP, (ii) McMillan degree of $S$ equals the *expected* closed-loop pole count. - [ ] Distinguish clearly between **additive** ($\ell_a$), **multiplicative output** ($\ell_m$), and **inverse multiplicative input** ($\ell_i$) uncertainty models — they lead to *different* robust-stability conditions involving $T$, $S$, or $KS$ respectively.

topics.title: Modes of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modes of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Multivariable Control Systems**, the term **"Modes of Operation"** refers to the distinct algorithmic and structural configurations in which a controller interacts with a plant to achieve a desired dynamic response. These modes govern *how* the controller computes its corrective action from the **error signal** $e(t)$, defined as the difference between the **setpoint (reference input)** $r(t)$ and the **measured process variable** $y(t)$:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

The canonical modes of operation studied in Module 1 form the foundational building blocks upon which more advanced **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)** strategies — such as **centralized**, **decentralized**, and **decoupled control** — are constructed. The fundamental modes are:

| Mode | Control Action Form |
|---|---|
| **On-Off (Two-Position)** | Discontinuous, relay-type |
| **Floating (Integral-only, slow)** | Continuous integration |
| **Proportional (P)** | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t)$ |
| **Integral (I)** | $u(t) = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$ |
| **Derivative (D)** | $u(t) = K_p \, T_d \, \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ |
| **PI, PD, PID** | Linear combinations of the above |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Modes of Operation are not merely historical artifacts from classical control. They are the *atomic units* used to parameterize the diagonal entries of the **MIMO controller matrix** $K(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ in decentralized multivariable control, and they appear as transfer-function blocks within the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** analysis framework for interaction assessment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car** along a winding road, and your goal is to stay exactly on the centerline.

- **On-Off Mode** is like having a steering wheel that can only be turned **fully left** or **fully right**. Every small deviation triggers a violent correction, causing a "jerky" ride. (Useful for thermostats, not for cars.)
- **Proportional (P) Mode** is like a driver who turns the steering wheel by an amount **proportional to how far the car has drifted** from the lane. Drift a lot → turn a lot; drift a little → turn a little. Clean and intuitive, but always leaves a small permanent offset (steady-state error).
- **Integral (I) Mode** is like a driver who keeps **remembering past errors** and turns the wheel a little extra each second for as long as the car has been drifting. This eventually **eliminates the offset** but can cause overshooting.
- **Derivative (D) Mode** is like a driver who anticipates the **rate at which the car is drifting off** and pre-emptively counter-steers. It damps oscillations and adds stability but does nothing for a constant error.
- **PI, PD, PID** are drivers who combine two or three of these abilities — the standard expert driver used in **97% of industrial control loops**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchor:** In a **petrochemical refinery**, the temperature of a distillation column is regulated by a **PID controller** ($K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$ chosen via Ziegler-Nichols tuning). The "mode of operation" choice (P vs PI vs PID) directly determines the **column's purity, energy consumption, and response time** — a multimillion-dollar decision governed by a 100-year-old control theory.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following empirical constants and metrics recur throughout mode analysis:

- **Proportional Band (PB)** = $\dfrac{1}{K_p} \times 100\%$ — the percentage of error span required to drive the controller output from **0 % to 100 %**.
- **Reset Rate** = $\dfrac{1}{T_i}$ — the number of **repeats per minute** (a Ziegler-Nichols tuning concept).
- **Derivative Time $T_d$** — typically measured in **seconds** or **minutes**.
- **Reaction Rate (Derivative Gain Limiting)** — derivative action is usually multiplied by a factor of $\dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha \, T_d s}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.1$ to suppress noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "Derivative mode improves steady-state error." — **FALSE.** Derivative action has **zero effect on steady-state error**. It only improves **transient response** and **stability margins**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Mode — Structured Logic Steps

### 2.1.1 On-Off (Two-Position) Mode
- **Logic:** A relay switches the manipulated variable $u(t)$ between two extreme values: $u = u_{max}$ when $e(t) > 0$, and $u = u_{min}$ when $e(t) < 0$.
- **Why:** Simplest possible implementation; uses no mathematics beyond a comparator. Used in **domestic thermostats**, **refrigerators**, and **pressure safety cutoffs**.
- **How:** The output is a square wave whose **mark-space ratio** depends on the load. The process variable $y(t)$ oscillates continuously around the setpoint — this oscillation is called **differential gap** or **hysteresis** $\delta$.
- **Drawback:** Continuous cycling causes **wear and tear** on actuators (e.g., solenoid valves cycling 100× per hour).

### 2.1.2 Proportional (P) Mode
- **Logic:** The control output is linearly proportional to instantaneous error.
- **Why:** Provides smooth, predictable correction. The most widely used single mode in industry.
- **How:** The controller transfer function is a pure gain:
$$G_c(s) = K_p$$
- **Critical Limitation:** Steady-state error (offset). For a unit-step reference, a Type-0 plant with a P-controller has an offset of:
$$e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p \, G_p(0)}$$
where $G_p(0)$ is the plant's **DC gain**. Offset can only be eliminated by increasing $K_p \to \infty$, which destabilizes the closed loop.

### 2.1.3 Integral (I) Mode — "Reset Action"
- **Logic:** Output is proportional to the **time integral of past errors**.
- **Why:** Guarantees **zero steady-state error** for step inputs on Type-0 plants by continuously accumulating corrective action until the error is driven to zero.
- **How:** Controller transfer function:
$$G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_i}{s} = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i \, s}$$
- **Drawback:** Pure integral action is **slow** and **induces oscillations** (each past error keeps contributing for a long time). It also causes **integral windup** during actuator saturation.

### 2.1.4 Derivative (D) Mode — "Rate Action" or "Pre-Act"
- **Logic:** Output is proportional to the **rate of change of error**.
- **Why:** Adds **anticipatory** behavior. In multivariable systems, derivative action is essential for compensating transport delays and cross-coupling dynamics.
- **How:** Controller transfer function:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \, T_d \, s$$
- **Critical Limitation:** Cannot act on a **constant error** (derivative of a constant is zero). Also amplifies **measurement noise** by a factor proportional to frequency.

### 2.1.5 Composite Modes: PI, PD, PID
- **PI Controller** — combines P's speed with I's zero-offset property:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = K_p \, \dfrac{1 + T_i s}{T_i s}$$
- **PD Controller** — combines P's speed with D's damping:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + T_d s\right)$$
- **PID Controller (Ideal / Parallel Form)** — the workhorse of industry:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$$
- **PID Controller (Standard / ISA Form)** — the form used by virtually all DCS vendors (e.g., Honeywell, ABB, Siemens):
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) \left(\dfrac{1 + T_d s}{1 + \alpha \, T_d s}\right)$$
where $\alpha \approx 0.1$ is the derivative filter coefficient (a **pole-zero cancellation** to make the derivative term physically realizable).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Mode | Time-Domain Equation $u(t)$ | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Steady-State Error (Unit Step, Type-0 Plant) | Key Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| On-Off | $u = U_{max} \cdot \text{sgn}(e)$ | Nonlinear (relay) | Oscillatory (no $e_{ss}$ in average) | Two-position, hysteresis $\delta$ |
| P | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + u_{bias}$ | $K_p$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p K_{proc}}$ | Reduces rise time, leaves offset |
| I | $u(t) = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $\dfrac{K_p}{T_i s}$ | $0$ | Eliminates offset, slow |
| D | $u(t) = K_p \, T_d \, \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p \, T_d \, s$ | Unchanged | Adds damping, anticipates |
| PI | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i} \int e\,dt$ | $K_p\left(1+\dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | $0$ | Combines P-speed + I-zero-offset |
| PD | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_p T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_p (1+T_d s)$ | Unchanged | Adds damping without integral action |
| PID (Parallel) | $u(t) = K_p e + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_p T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_p\left(1+\dfrac{1}{T_i s}+T_d s\right)$ | $0$ | Universal industrial controller |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Chemical Process Control:** PI controllers dominate (≈ 80 % of loops) because chemical plants require zero offset but rarely need derivative action (slow dynamics, noisy flow measurements).
> - **Motion Control / Robotics:** PD and PID are dominant because speed, precision, and damping are paramount.
> - **Aerospace (Multivariable):** MIMO controllers (LQR, $H_\infty$) are state-feedback generalizations of these classical modes, where the "P-mode" becomes a state-gain matrix $K$ and the "I-mode" becomes an augmented integrator state.

## 2.3 Block-Diagram Realization of Each Mode

A practical implementation of a PID controller in a process DCS uses **three parallel branches** summed at a summing junction:

```
              e(t)
               │
       ┌───────┼───────┬──────────┐
       │       │       │          │
       ▼       ▼       ▼          ▼
     [K_p]  [K_p/T_i ∫] [K_p T_d d/dt]   (with noise filter on D)
       │       │       │          │
       └───────┴───────┴──────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
                     u(t)
```

> The **integral branch** in real systems includes an **anti-windup** clamp that holds the integrator output within the actuator's physical limits $\pm u_{max}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Error of a P-Controller on a First-Order Plant

Let the plant be a first-order process with transfer function:
$$G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_{proc}}{\tau_p \, s + 1}$$

The closed-loop transfer function from reference $R(s)$ to output $Y(s)$ with a P-controller $G_c(s) = K_p$ is:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}$$

### Step 1: Substitute the transfer functions

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K_p \cdot \dfrac{K_{proc}}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + K_p \cdot \dfrac{K_{proc}}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

### Step 2: Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_p s + 1)$

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, K_{proc}}{(\tau_p s + 1) + K_p \, K_{proc}}$$

### Step 3: Write the error transfer function

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = R(s) \left[1 - T(s)\right] = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

For a unit-step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the limit

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_p \, K_{proc}}{\tau_p s + 1}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p \, K_{proc}}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{loop}}, \quad K_{loop} = K_p \, K_{proc}\,}$$

**Insight:** As $K_p \to \infty$, the offset $\to 0$, but the closed-loop time constant $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{\tau_p}{1 + K_p K_{proc}} \to 0$, which makes the response **infinitely fast** and eventually **unstable**. This trade-off is the central motivation for introducing **derivative action**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Zero Steady-State Error of a PI-Controller

Replace the P-controller with:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = K_p \, \dfrac{1 + T_i s}{T_i s}$$

The open-loop transfer function becomes:
$$G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, K_{proc}}{(\tau_p s + 1)} \cdot \dfrac{1 + T_i s}{T_i s}$$

The error transfer function for a unit step is:
$$E(s) = \dfrac{1}{s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

Apply the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_p K_{proc} (1 + T_i s)}{T_i s (\tau_p s + 1)}}$$

As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{1}{T_i s} \to \infty$, so:

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = 0\,}$$

**Insight:** The integrator introduces a **pole at the origin**, raising the system type from 0 to 1, which is the formal reason why a Type-1 system has zero steady-state error for a step input.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a PID with Derivative Filter

The practical (physically realizable) PID transfer function is:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + \dfrac{T_d s}{1 + \alpha T_d s}\right]$$

### Step 1: Combine the derivative branch with the filter

$$\dfrac{T_d s}{1 + \alpha T_d s} = \dfrac{\text{zero at } s = -1/T_d}{\text{pole at } s = -1/(\alpha T_d)}$$

### Step 2: Total controller is

$$G_c(s) = K_p \cdot \dfrac{(T_i s + 1)(1 + \alpha T_d s) + T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s (1 + \alpha T_d s)}{T_i s (1 + \alpha T_d s)}$$

### Step 3: Numerator expansion

Let $N(s) = (T_i s + 1)(1 + \alpha T_d s) + T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s (1 + \alpha T_d s)$.

Expanding the first product:
$$(T_i s + 1)(1 + \alpha T_d s) = T_i s + \alpha T_i T_d s^2 + 1 + \alpha T_d s$$

Adding the remaining terms:
$$N(s) = 1 + (T_i + \alpha T_d + T_i) s + (\alpha T_i T_d + T_i T_d + \alpha T_i T_d) s^2$$
$$N(s) = 1 + (2 T_i + \alpha T_d) s + T_i T_d (1 + 2\alpha) s^2$$

### Step 4: Standard form

$$\boxed{\,G_c(s) = K_p \cdot \dfrac{1 + (2 T_i + \alpha T_d) s + T_i T_d (1 + 2\alpha) s^2}{T_i s (1 + \alpha T_d s)}\,}$$

This is the **textbook form** required for stability analysis using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** in KTU examination problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (P, I, D vs Closed-Loop Properties)

| Property | P-only | I-only | D-only | PI | PD | PID |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | No effect | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ |
| Overshoot | ↑ Increases | ↑↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Moderate | ↓ | Moderate (if tuned) |
| Settling Time | Small change | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Moderate | ↓ | ↓ (best) |
| Steady-State Error | Reduced but ≠ 0 | 0 | No effect | 0 | Reduced but ≠ 0 | 0 |
| Stability | Worse for high $K_p$ | Worse | Improves | Better than P | Better than P | Best (with tuning) |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Low | **Very High** | Low | **High** | **High** |
| Industrial Use | Rare alone | Rare alone | Almost never alone | **Very High** | Moderate (motion) | **Highest** |

> ↑ means property increases, ↓ means property decreases.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Multivariable Extension (Decentralized Control)

In a $2 \times 2$ multivariable plant $G_p(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$ with controller $K(s) = \text{diag}\{K_1(s), K_2(s)\}$, each diagonal entry $K_i(s)$ is independently chosen from the modes above:

```mermaid
graph LR
    U1[U1 from loop1]:::vecNode --> G11[G11]:::gNode
    U1 --> G12[G12]:::gNode
    U2[U2 from loop2]:::vecNode --> G21[G21]:::gNode
    U2 --> G22[G22]:::gNode
    G11 --> SUMY1[Plus]:::sumNode
    G12 --> SUMY1
    G21 --> SUMY2[Plus]:::sumNode
    G22 --> SUMY2
    SUMY1 --> Y1[Y1 measured]:::outNode
    SUMY2 --> Y2[Y2 measured]:::outNode
    Y1 --> K1[Mode K1 such as PI]:::ctrlNode
    Y2 --> K2[Mode K2 such as PI]:::ctrlNode
    K1 --> U1
    K2 --> U2
    classDef vecNode fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gNode fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sumNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note:** The off-diagonal blocks $G_{12}(s)$ and $G_{21}(s)$ represent **interaction** between the loops. The RGA (Relative Gain Array) analysis — covered in Module 2 — quantifies this interaction and dictates whether decentralized control with classical modes is feasible, or whether a full **centralized MIMO controller** is required.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the various modes of operation in a process controller. List any three with their transfer functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Modes of operation are the distinct control algorithms by which a controller computes its output from the error signal $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. The three principal modes are:

1. **Proportional (P):** Output is proportional to instantaneous error.
$$G_c(s) = K_p$$
2. **Integral (I):** Output is proportional to the integral of past error.
$$G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i s}$$
3. **Derivative (D):** Output is proportional to the rate of change of error.
$$G_c(s) = K_p T_d s$$
These are typically combined into **PI, PD, or PID** for industrial use. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two modes with formulae)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the phenomenon of integral windup and how anti-windup is implemented.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Integral windup** occurs when a PI or PID controller's integral term continues to accumulate error during actuator saturation (when the controller output exceeds $u_{max}$ or $u_{min}$). This accumulated integral must be "unwound" once the actuator comes out of saturation, causing large **overshoot and poor transient response**.

**Anti-windup implementation:** A feedback loop is added around the integrator. The difference between the **saturated actuator output** $u_{sat}$ and the **unsaturated controller output** $u$ is fed back to the integrator input through a gain $K_{aw}$ (typically $K_{aw} = 1/T_t$ where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant). This stops integration when saturation is reached. *(3 marks: 1 for cause, 1 for effect, 1 for anti-windup mechanism)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-length Model Question

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ for a unity-feedback system with a **PI controller** $G_c(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ and a first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$. Hence determine the steady-state error for a unit-step input. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A temperature control loop in a chemical reactor uses a **PID controller** in standard form:
$$G_c(s) = K_p \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + \dfrac{T_d s}{1 + 0.1 T_d s}\right]$$
The process transfer function is $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{10s + 1}$. Using $K_p = 1.5$, $T_i = 5$ s, $T_d = 1$ s, determine:
   (i) The closed-loop characteristic equation. **\[3 marks\]**
   (ii) The closed-loop stability using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**. **\[3 marks\]**
   (iii) Comment on the effect of $T_d$ on stability. **\[1 mark\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Write the open-loop transfer function**
$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \, G_p(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) \cdot \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 2: Simplify the controller**
$$G_c(s) = K_p \cdot \dfrac{1 + T_i s}{T_i s}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**
$$G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{K_p K (1 + T_i s)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 4: Closed-loop transfer function**
$$T(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \dfrac{K_p K (1 + T_i s)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1) + K_p K (1 + T_i s)}$$

**Step 5: Expand the denominator**
$$T(s) = \dfrac{K_p K (1 + T_i s)}{T_i \tau s^2 + T_i s + K_p K T_i s + K_p K} = \dfrac{K_p K (1 + T_i s)}{T_i \tau s^2 + T_i (1 + K_p K) s + K_p K}$$

**Step 6: Steady-state error for unit step**
The error transfer function is:
$$E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{s} \cdot \dfrac{T_i s (\tau s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1) + K_p K (1 + T_i s)}$$

By the Final Value Theorem:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{T_i (\tau s + 1)}{T_i (\tau s + 1) + K_p K (1 + T_i s)/s}$$

As $s \to 0$, the term $\dfrac{K_p K T_i}{s} \to \infty$, so:
$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = 0\,}$$

The integrator guarantees zero offset. **\[7 marks: 2 setup, 2 closed-loop, 1 expansion, 2 error evaluation\]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Closed-loop characteristic equation (3 marks):**

The controller with derivative filter:
$$G_c(s) = 1.5 \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{5s} + \dfrac{s}{1 + 0.1 s}\right]$$

**Step 1: Simplify the derivative branch**
$$\dfrac{s}{1 + 0.1 s} = \dfrac{s}{1 + 0.1 s}$$

**Step 2: Common denominator for $G_c$**
$$G_c(s) = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{(5s)(1 + 0.1s) + (1 + 0.1s) + 5s^2}{5s(1 + 0.1s)}$$

**Step 3: Expand the numerator**
$$(5s)(1 + 0.1s) = 5s + 0.5s^2$$
$$(1 + 0.1s) = 1 + 0.1s$$
$$5s^2 = 5s^2$$

Total numerator: $0.5s^2 + 5s^2 + 5s + 0.1s + 1 = 5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1$

**Step 4: So**
$$G_c(s) = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1}{5s(1 + 0.1s)} = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1}{0.5s^2 + 5s}$$

**Step 5: Open-loop transfer function**
$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \, G_p(s) = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1}{0.5s^2 + 5s} \cdot \dfrac{2}{10s + 1}$$

$$G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{3(5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1)}{(0.5s^2 + 5s)(10s + 1)}$$

**Step 6: Characteristic equation** is $1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0$, i.e.:
$$(0.5s^2 + 5s)(10s + 1) + 3(5.5s^2 + 5.1s + 1) = 0$$

**Step 7: Expand**
$$0.5s^2 \cdot 10s = 5s^3, \quad 0.5s^2 \cdot 1 = 0.5s^2$$
$$5s \cdot 10s = 50s^2, \quad 5s \cdot 1 = 5s$$
$$3 \cdot 5.5s^2 = 16.5s^2, \quad 3 \cdot 5.1s = 15.3s, \quad 3 \cdot 1 = 3$$

Sum:
$$5s^3 + (0.5 + 50 + 16.5)s^2 + (5 + 15.3)s + 3 = 0$$
$$\boxed{\,5s^3 + 67s^2 + 20.3s + 3 = 0\,}$$

**(ii) Routh-Hurwitz Stability (3 marks):**

Construct the Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $5$ | $20.3$ |
| $s^2$ | $67$ | $3$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{67 \cdot 20.3 - 5 \cdot 3}{67} = \dfrac{1360.1 - 15}{67} = \dfrac{1345.1}{67} \approx 20.08$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $3$ | |

All first-column elements are **positive** ($5, 67, 20.08, 3$). No sign changes → **the system is stable**. **\[3 marks\]**

**(iii) Effect of $T_d$ (1 mark):**

Increasing $T_d$ adds more derivative action, which increases damping and improves transient response. However, beyond a critical value, the high-frequency noise gets amplified, and the derivative filter pole at $-1/(\alpha T_d)$ moves closer to the origin, potentially destabilizing the loop. Optimal $T_d$ is typically $\dfrac{T_i}{4}$ per Ziegler-Nichols tuning. **\[1 mark\]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** Compare the steady-state and transient performance of **P, PI, and PID** controllers when applied to a second-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + s + 1}$. Use a unit-step input and explain qualitatively using the error constants $K_p$, $K_v$, and $K_a$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A process has the transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+3)}$. A **proportional controller** $G_c(s) = K_p$ is used in a unity-feedback configuration. Determine the range of $K_p$ for closed-loop stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Also find the value of $K_p$ that produces sustained oscillations, and the corresponding frequency. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution Outline (Sketch)

**Part (a):** Compute position, velocity, and acceleration error constants for each mode. Show that PI/PID give $K_p = \infty$ → zero step-error, but reduce phase margin → more oscillations.

**Part (b):** Closed-loop characteristic equation: $s^2 + 4s + 3 + K_p = 0$. For stability, all coefficients positive → $K_p > -3$. For marginal stability (sustained oscillation), apply Routh: $K_p$ such that auxiliary row = 0. With first-order auxiliary (s^2 + ... = 0), oscillation occurs only if $K_p < 0$, which is non-physical. Therefore, the system is stable for **all $K_p > 0$**, and no sustained oscillations occur for positive $K_p$. Frequency: $s^2 + 4s + (3 + K_p) = 0 \to s = -2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 3 - K_p} = -2 \pm \sqrt{1 - K_p}$. For oscillation, $K_p > 1$, frequency $\omega = \sqrt{K_p - 1}$ rad/s. **\[7 marks\]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Final Value Theorem's pre-condition:** You MUST check that the closed-loop system is **stable** before applying $\lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$. Otherwise the limit is meaningless. (1 mark lost per question.)
> 2. **Sign errors in the Routh array:** A common error is computing $b_1 = \dfrac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}$ with the **wrong sign**. Always double-check: $b_1$ should be the **first column of the $s^1$ row**.
> 3. **Confusing P, PD, and PID on Type-0 vs Type-1 plants:** Many students add an integrator for a Type-1 plant (e.g., a motor position loop) and create instability. Verify using Routh before adding the I-term.
> 4. **Omitting the derivative filter:** The pure $K_p T_d s$ term is **not physically realizable**. Always include $\dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha T_d s}$ or your block diagram will be marked down.
> 5. **Steady-state offset on P-mode:** Examiners explicitly test whether you can derive the offset formula $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p G_p(0)}$. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU paper.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam):**

- ✅ The **error signal** is always $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ (negative feedback convention).
- ✅ **P-mode** transfer function: $G_c(s) = K_p$ — leaves steady-state offset.
- ✅ **I-mode** transfer function: $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i s}$ — eliminates offset, but causes windup and slow response.
- ✅ **D-mode** transfer function: $G_c(s) = K_p T_d s$ — anticipates, damps oscillations, sensitive to noise.
- ✅ **PI-mode**: $G_c(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = K_p \dfrac{1 + T_i s}{T_i s}$ — zero offset, one tuning parameter beyond P.
- ✅ **PID (parallel form)**: $G_c(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$.
- ✅ **PID (standard / ISA form)**: includes a **derivative filter** $\dfrac{1}{1+\alpha T_d s}$ with $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.2]$.
- ✅ **Proportional Band (PB)** = $\dfrac{100\%}{K_p}$ for a controller calibrated in percent.
- ✅ **Anti-windup** is **mandatory** in any practical PI/PID implementation; clamp the integrator to $\pm u_{max}$.
- ✅ **On-Off mode** has **no steady-state error** in the long-run average sense, but introduces continuous cycling with period proportional to the differential gap $\delta$.
- ✅ **Multivariable extension:** in $m \times m$ decentralized control, each $K_i(s)$ is one of the above modes. The RGA then decides whether such a diagonal design is feasible.
- ✅ **Three classical tuning methods** to remember: (1) Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain, (2) Cohen-Coon, (3) Tyreus-Luyben.
- ✅ **Final Value Theorem prerequisite** for steady-state error computation: the closed-loop system **must be stable** first.
- ✅ **Routh-Hurwitz rule** for a cubic $as^3 + bs^2 + cs + d = 0$: stable iff $a, b, c, d > 0$ and $bc > ad$.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Modes of Operation**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Need for Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Need for Feedback # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In control engineering, **Feedback** is the process of returning a portion of the output (or some function of the output) of a system back to the input for the purpose of comparing it with the reference (desired) input, so that corrective action may be taken to reduce the deviation between the actual and desired response. A **feedback control system** (also called a **closed-loop control system**) is one in which the control action is a function of both the input command and the measured output. Mathematically, if $R(s)$ is the reference, $Y(s)$ is the output, and $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$ is the actuating error, the closed-loop transfer function is: $$ T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} $$ where $G(s)$ is the forward path transfer function and $H(s)$ is the feedback path transfer function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-1 Anchor Concept:** The term **"Feedback"** in this syllabus refers explicitly to **negative feedback**, which is the universal backbone of classical and modern control engineering. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Does a Human Need Eyes While Driving? Imagine you are driving a car along a curving road at **80 km/h**. - **Without feedback (Open-Loop):** You fix the steering wheel at a certain angle based on *what you think* the road curvature is. If a wind gust pushes the car, if the road surface changes, or if your speed alters the dynamics — you **cannot correct** because you have no information about the *actual* position of the car. The car eventually leaves the road. - **With feedback (Closed-Loop):** Your **eyes continuously measure** the car's lateral position relative to the lane markings. Your brain compares this measurement to the *desired* position (center of the lane), and continuously adjusts the steering. Disturbances like wind, road bumps, or speed changes are **automatically corrected**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Mapping of the Analogy:** > - Eyes (sensor) = $H(s)$ > - Brain (controller) = $G(s)$ > - Hands on steering (actuator) = Final control element > - Wind/road bump = Disturbance $D(s)$ > - Desired lane position = Reference $R(s)$ The same principle governs a missile homing on a target, a furnace regulating temperature, and an autopilot maintaining aircraft altitude. **Without feedback, the system is blind to its own errors.** ## 1.3 The Central Problem Statement A system designer is essentially answering one question: **"Given an imperfect plant, uncertain environment, and unavoidable disturbances, how do I guarantee that the actual output tracks the desired output reliably?"** The answer across more than a century of control engineering practice is overwhelmingly **negative feedback**. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six Canonical Reasons Why Feedback Is Needed (Memorize These — KTU Repeated Tag):** > 1. **Reduction of sensitivity** to parameter variations > 2. **Rejection of external disturbances** > 3. **Reduction of steady-state error** > 4. **Improvement of transient response** (when properly compensated) > 5. **Stabilization of an inherently unstable plant** > 6. **Linearization / bandwidth shaping** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop — The Architectural Contrast ### Open-Loop System $$ Y(s) = G(s)\,R(s) + D(s)\,G_d(s) $$ - No sensor, no comparator. - Output is **blind** to disturbances $D(s)$. - Highly sensitive to any change in $G(s)$ due to temperature, aging, manufacturing tolerances. - Cheap, fast, but **fragile**. ### Closed-Loop (Unity Feedback, $H(s)=1$) System $$ T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} $$ - The error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ continuously drives the controller. - Loop gain $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert$ at the cross-over frequency dominates behaviour. - More components, higher cost, but **robust**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$ at low frequencies, $T(s) \approx \dfrac{1}{H(s)}$. The closed-loop response becomes nearly **independent of the plant** $G(s)$ — this is the essence of robustness through feedback. ## 2.2 The Six Benefits — Structured Logical Breakdown ### Benefit 1 — Sensitivity Reduction Sensitivity is a *dimensionless* measure of how much the closed-loop transfer function changes relative to a fractional change in the plant. The **sensitivity function** is: $$ S(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} $$ If $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$, then $S \to 0$, meaning the closed-loop is **insensitive** to plant variations. ### Benefit 2 — Disturbance Rejection A disturbance $D(s)$ entering the forward path produces an output component: $$ Y_D(s) = \frac{G_1(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}\,D(s) $$ The denominator $1 + G_1 G_2 H$ attenuates the disturbance by the **loop gain**. ### Benefit 3 — Steady-State Error Reduction For a unity feedback system with a step input, the position error constant is: $$ K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s) $$ The steady-state error is $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$. Increasing loop gain lowers $e_{ss}$. ### Benefit 4 — Bandwidth Modification For a unity feedback system whose forward path is $G(s)$, the **closed-loop bandwidth** is typically broader than the open-loop. This enables faster tracking of rapidly-changing commands. ### Benefit 5 — Stabilization of Unstable Plants If $G(s)$ has poles in the right half-plane, the controller inside the loop can be designed so that the closed-loop poles all lie in the left half-plane. ### Benefit 6 — Linearization For mildly nonlinear plants, a high-gain feedback loop forces the input-output relationship to follow the inverse of $H(s)$, effectively masking the plant's nonlinearities. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Cheat Sheet" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. It covers ~70% of Module-1 numerical questions.** | # | Quantity | Open-Loop Expression | Closed-Loop Expression | Engineering Meaning | |---|----------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Sensitivity to plant $G$ | $S^G_T = 1$ | $S^G_T = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | Lower is better | | 2 | Disturbance transfer $\dfrac{Y}{D}$ | $G_d(s)$ | $\dfrac{G_1}{1+G_1 G_2 H}$ | Lower is better | | 3 | Step steady-state error | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p^{\text{ol}}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p^{\text{cl}}}$ | Lower is better | | 4 | Tracking transfer $\dfrac{Y}{R}$ | $G(s)$ | $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ | Depends on design | | 5 | Loop gain (characteristic) | — | $L(s)=G(s)H(s)$ | $\vert L(j\omega)\vert \gg 1$ desired at low $\omega$ | | 6 | Bandwidth | $BW_{ol}$ | $BW_{cl} > BW_{ol}$ (typ.) | Trade-off with noise | | 7 | Stability condition | Unconditional | Roots of $1+G(s)H(s)=0$ in LHP | Routh-Hurwitz criterion | > **Use \vert in cells to avoid breaking markdown tables (per protocol).** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility -
