**Automotive:** Cruise control, anti-lock braking (ABS), electronic stability program (ESP) — all are feedback loops running at $\geq 100\,\text{Hz}$. - **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire systems, attitude control of satellites, autopilot — multi-loop nested feedback. - **Process Industries:** Temperature, pressure, flow, level control in refineries — typically PID loops. - **Power Systems:** Automatic voltage regulators (AVR), load frequency control (LFC). - **Robotics:** Joint position and torque feedback for precise motion. - **Multivariable extension (this course):** MIMO systems use feedback not just to control multiple outputs but to **decouple** them and to handle **loop interaction** — which is the natural extension of the single-loop need for feedback. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Sensitivity Reduction by Feedback **Statement:** Feedback reduces the fractional change in the closed-loop transfer function $T$ with respect to a fractional change in the plant $G$ by the factor $\vert 1 + GH \vert$. **Starting Point:** Define the sensitivity function: $$ S^G_T \triangleq \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} $$ **Closed-Loop Transfer Function:** $$ T = \frac{G}{1 + GH} $$ **Step 1 — Compute $\partial T / \partial G$:** $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+GH) - G \cdot H}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} $$ **Step 2 — Form the ratio $\dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G}$:** $$ \frac{\partial T}{T} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot dG = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \, dG $$ Dividing by $T = \dfrac{G}{1+GH}$: $$ \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{T} = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G(1+GH)}{G} = \frac{1}{1+GH} $$ **Step 3 — Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\,S^G_T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** If $G$ doubles ($G \to 2G$), then $T$ changes by a factor of only $\dfrac{1}{1+GH}$. For example, with $\vert GH\vert = 100$, a $100\%$ change in $G$ produces only a $\approx 1\%$ change in $T$. **The plant is tamed.** > [!NOTE] > This is *the* canonical derivation. KTU examiners award full marks only when the partial derivative is shown explicitly. Do not skip Step 1. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Disturbance Rejection by Feedback **System setup:** Disturbance $D(s)$ enters between $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$. $$ E \longrightarrow \boxed{G_1} \longrightarrow \boxed{+} \longrightarrow \boxed{G_2} \longrightarrow Y $$ The summing junction adds $D$ at the input of $G_2$: $$ Y = G_2\bigl(G_1 E + D\bigr) $$ With $E = R - HY$: $$ Y = G_2\bigl[G_1(R - HY) + D\bigr] $$ $$ Y = G_1 G_2 R - G_1 G_2 H Y + G_2 D $$ $$ Y\bigl(1 + G_1 G_2 H\bigr) = G_1 G_2 R + G_2 D $$ **Disturbance-to-Output Transfer Function:** $$ \boxed{\,\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)}\,} $$ **Open-loop equivalent** (no feedback, $H=0$): $$ \frac{Y(s)}{D(s)}\bigg\vert_{OL} = G_2(s) $$ **Reduction Factor:** $$ \frac{\text{CL disturbance response}}{\text{OL disturbance response}} = \frac{1}{1 + G_1 G_2 H} $$ **Conclusion:** With $\vert G_1 G_2 H \vert \gg 1$, the disturbance effect is attenuated by the loop gain. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Steady-State Error Reduction (Unity Feedback) **Position error constant (Type-0 system):** $$ K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) $$ **Open-loop steady-state error for a step $R(s) = 1/s$:** $$ e_{ss}^{OL} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{OL}} $$ **Closed-loop steady-state error:** The closed-loop forward path becomes: $$ T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p^{CL} = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \frac{K_p}{1+K_p} $$ Therefore: $$ e_{ss}^{CL} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{CL}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{1+K_p}} = \frac{1+K_p}{1+2K_p} $$ **Comparison for $K_p \gg 1$:** $$ e_{ss}^{OL} \approx \frac{1}{K_p}, \qquad e_{ss}^{CL} \approx \frac{K_p}{2K_p} = \frac{1}{2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Caution for KTU students:** Closing the loop on a **Type-0** system alone does *not* drive step-error to zero. To achieve $e_{ss}=0$ for a step, you need a **Type-1** system (one integrator in $G(s)$), which gives infinite $K_p$ in CL. This is a classic examiner trap. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Sensitivity Calculation (KTU Style) **Problem:** A plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s+5}$ is placed in a unity feedback loop. If $G$ drifts to $G' = \dfrac{12}{s+5}$ (20% increase), find the open-loop and closed-loop sensitivities to this drift. **Solution:** **Open-loop:** $T_{OL} = G = \dfrac{10}{s+5}$, $T_{OL}' = \dfrac{12}{s+5}$. $$ \Delta T_{OL} / T_{OL} = \frac{12 - 10}{10} = 0.20 = 20\% $$ **Closed-loop:** $$ T_{CL} = \frac{G}{1+G} = \frac{\frac{10}{s+5}}{1+\frac{10}{s+5}} = \frac{10}{s+15} $$ $$ T_{CL}' = \frac{12}{s+17} $$ **At $s = 0$:** $$ T_{CL}(0) = \frac{10}{15} = 0.667, \qquad T_{CL}'(0) = \frac{12}{17} = 0.706 $$ $$ \frac{\Delta T_{CL}}{T_{CL}} = \frac{0.706 - 0.667}{0.667} = 0.058 = 5.8\% $$ **Sensitivity:** $$ S = \frac{5.8\%}{20\%} = 0.29 $$ **Verification via formula** at $s=0$: $$ S = \frac{1}{1+G(0)} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{10}{5}} = \frac{1}{1+2} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333 $$ > Small mismatch is due to the chosen evaluation point. **The formula gives the differential sensitivity**; the worked numbers are the finite-difference approximation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[R reference input] --> G1[G controller] G1 --> G2[P plant] G2 --> Y1[Y output] D1[D disturbance] -.-> G2 classDef ref fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px class R1 ref class Y1 out ``` > **Figure 4.1 — Open-Loop System.** Note the absence of any path from $Y$ back to $R$. Disturbance $D$ enters the plant directly and cannot be sensed or corrected. ```mermaid flowchart LR R2[R reference] --> SUM2[summing junction e] SUM2 --> C2[GC controller] C2 --> P2[P plant] P2 --> Y2[Y output] Y2 --> H2[H sensor] H2 --> SUM2 D2[D disturbance] -.-> P2 classDef ref fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef fb fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px class R2 ref class H2 fb class Y2 out ``` > **Figure 4.2 — Closed-Loop (Negative Feedback) System.** The feedback signal $H \cdot Y$ is *subtracted* from $R$ at the summing junction. The error $e = R - HY$ drives the controller continuously. ## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic of Why Feedback Is Chosen ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Design Engineer has a Plant G] --> B{Is the plant open-loop acceptable?} B -- Yes --> C[Use open-loop
e.g. cheap toaster, washing timer] B -- No --> D{What problem?} D --> E[Sensitivity too high] D --> F[Disturbances too large] D --> G[Steady-state error unacceptable] D --> H[Plant is unstable] E --> I[Apply negative feedback
S = 1/1+GH] F --> J[Apply negative feedback
Y/D = G1/1+G1G2H] G --> K[Add integrator in loop
Type 1 or higher] H --> L[Compensator inside loop
Pole placement] I --> M[Closed-loop design complete] J --> M K --> M L --> M classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px classDef solution fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px class E,F,G,H problem class I,J,K,L,M solution ``` > **Figure 4.3 — Decision Tree.** The branch the engineer takes determines the role of feedback. **Almost every industrial branch eventually leads to closing the loop.** ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Feedback's Six Benefits ```mermaid flowchart TB FB[Negative Feedback Applied] FB --> B1[Benefit 1 Sensitivity Reduction
S = 1/1+GH] FB --> B2[Benefit 2 Disturbance Rejection
Y/D attenuated by loop gain] FB --> B3[Benefit 3 Steady-State Error Reduction
Higher type via integrator] FB --> B4[Benefit 4 Bandwidth Modification
BW increased typical] FB --> B5[Benefit 5 Stabilization
Pole relocation] FB --> B6[Benefit 6 Linearization
High gain masks nonlinearity] B1 --> OUT[Robust, Accurate, Fast,
Stable, Linear System] B2 --> OUT B3 --> OUT B4 --> OUT B5 --> OUT B6 --> OUT classDef root fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef benefit fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px class FB root class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 benefit class OUT out ``` > **Figure 4.4 — Six Benefits Map.** All converge on a robust closed-loop system. This is the single most-tested conceptual diagram in KTU Module-1 viva voce. ## 4.4 Signal-Flow View — Where the Error Lives ```mermaid flowchart LR REFr[R reference] -->|+| EP[e error signal] EP --> FORW[Forward path G] FORW --> YO[Y output] YO --> FBK[Feedback path H] FBK --> EP REFr2[Disturbance D] -.-> FORW classDef err fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px classDef sig fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px class EP err class REFr,REFr2,YO,FORW,FBK sig ``` > **Figure 4.5 — The Error Signal $e = R - HY$ is the lifeblood of the feedback system.** The entire goal of feedback is to drive $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ for a step reference. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define the term "sensitivity" in a feedback control system. Write the expression for sensitivity of the closed-loop transfer function with respect to the forward path transfer function.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sensitivity is a measure of the effect of plant parameter variation on the closed-loop transfer function. It is defined as the ratio of fractional change in $T$ to the fractional change in $G$. $$ S^G_T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} $$ For a closed-loop system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback $H(s)$: $$ \boxed{S^G_T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}} $$ A small value of $\vert S\vert$ implies the closed-loop response is robust to plant variations. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **List any four reasons why feedback is employed in control systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Reduction of sensitivity** to plant parameter variations. 2. **Rejection of external disturbances.** 3. **Reduction of steady-state error.** 4. **Improvement of transient response** and **stabilization** of unstable plants. (Any four of the six canonical reasons listed in Section 1.3 are acceptable.) **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b). You answer either Question A or Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **(a)** Derive an expression for the sensitivity of the closed-loop transfer function with respect to the plant transfer function. Explain how feedback reduces sensitivity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine the value of $K$ for which the closed-loop system becomes marginally stable. Comment on the effect of feedback on steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation:** For a closed-loop system with forward path $G(s)$ and unity feedback $H(s)=1$: $$ T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} $$ Differentiating with respect to $G$: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+G) - G(1)}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+G)^2} $$ The sensitivity is: $$ S^G_T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{1}{T}\cdot\frac{\partial T}{\partial G}\cdot G = \frac{1+G}{G} \cdot \frac{G}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{1}{1+G} $$ **Interpretation:** If $\vert G(s) \vert \gg 1$ (high loop gain), $S^G_T \approx \dfrac{1}{G(s)}$, which is very small. Thus a fractional change in $G$ produces a much smaller fractional change in $T$. Feedback **desensitizes** the system. **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Routh Stability and Steady-State Error:** Characteristic equation: $$ 1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s(s+2)(s+5) + K = 0 $$ Expanding: $$ s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K = 0 $$ Routh array: $$ \begin{array}{c|cc} s^3 & 1 & 10 \\ s^2 & 7 & K \\ s^1 & \dfrac{70-K}{7} & 0 \\ s^0 & K & \\ \end{array} $$ For marginal stability, the $s^1$ row must vanish: $$ \frac{70 - K}{7} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{K = 70} $$ Auxiliary equation from $s^2$ row: $$ 7s^2 + K = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 7s^2 + 70 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2 = -10 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{10} $$ The system oscillates at $\omega = \sqrt{10}\;\text{rad/s}$. **Steady-state error for ramp input:** Velocity error constant: $$ K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,G(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{K}{10} = 7 $$ Steady-state error for unit ramp: $$ e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.143 $$ **Comment:** Without feedback (open-loop), the ramp error would be *infinite* because the plant has no integrator path. Closing the loop converts the Type-0 plant into a **Type-1 closed-loop system**, reducing the ramp error to a finite value. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]* **(a)** With the help of block diagrams, explain how feedback improves: (i) disturbance rejection, and (ii) bandwidth of a closed-loop system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{20}{s+4}$. The plant parameter changes so that $G(s)$ becomes $G'(s) = \dfrac{24}{s+4}$. Compute the open-loop and closed-loop percentage change in the DC gain. Also compute the sensitivity. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation:** **(i) Disturbance Rejection:** Open-loop disturbance enters the plant directly: $$ \frac{Y_{OL}(s)}{D(s)} = G_2(s) $$ With feedback and disturbance entering between $G_1$ and $G_2$: $$ \frac{Y_{CL}(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)} $$ The feedback attenuation factor is $1/(1 + G_1 G_2 H)$. For large loop gain, the disturbance effect is suppressed. **(3.5 marks)** **(ii) Bandwidth:** For $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \tau s}$, the open-loop bandwidth is $\omega_B^{OL} = 1/\tau$. Closing the loop with unity feedback gives: $$ T(s) = \frac{K/(1+\tau s)}{1 + K/(1+\tau s)} = \frac{K}{1 + K + \tau s} = \frac{K/(1+K)}{1 + \dfrac{\tau}{1+K}\, s} $$ The new bandwidth is $\omega_B^{CL} = (1+K)/\tau = (1+K)\cdot\omega_B^{OL}$. Thus feedback **increases bandwidth** by the factor $(1+K)$. **(3.5 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Sensitivity Computation:** **Open-loop DC gain (at $s=0$):** $$ G(0) = \frac{20}{4} = 5, \qquad G'(0) = \frac{24}{4} = 6 $$ $$ \Delta G / G = \frac{6-5}{5} = 20\% $$ $$ \text{Open-loop change} = \boxed{20\%} $$ **Closed-loop DC gain (unity feedback):** $$ T(0) = \frac{G(0)}{1+G(0)} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.8333 $$ $$ T'(0) = \frac{G'(0)}{1+G'(0)} = \frac{6}{7} = 0.8571 $$ $$ \Delta T / T = \frac{0.8571 - 0.8333}{0.8333} = 0.0286 = 2.86\% $$ $$ \text{Closed-loop change} = \boxed{2.86\%} $$ **Sensitivity:** $$ S = \frac{\Delta T / T}{\Delta G / G} = \frac{2.86\%}{20\%} = 0.143 $$ **Verification via formula at $s=0$:** $$ S = \frac{1}{1+G(0)} = \frac{1}{1+5} = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667 $$ The slight difference is because the formula gives the **differential (infinitesimal)** sensitivity, while our number is the **finite-difference** approximation. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. **Sign error at the summing junction** ($+HY$ instead of $-HY$): A single sign flip nullifies an entire derivation. Always write $e = R - HY$ explicitly. *(–2 to –4 marks per occurrence.)* > 2. **Forgetting to evaluate at the correct point** $s=0$ for sensitivity: The sensitivity function $S(s)$ is *frequency-dependent*. KTU usually expects the **DC value** $S(0)$ or the value at a specified $s$. > 3. **Confusing "open-loop" with "open-loop transfer function of the forward path"**: The open-loop system means $H=0$. The "loop transfer function" $L(s)=G(s)H(s)$ is the product of forward and feedback paths. Mixing these terms costs marks. > 4. **Skipping the partial derivative step**: Many students jump from $T=G/(1+GH)$ to $S=1/(1+GH)$ without showing $\partial T/\partial G$. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the derivation — **always show both steps.** > 5. **Ignoring the "Type" of the system** for steady-state error questions: A Type-0 closed-loop system has *finite* step error, *infinite* ramp error. A Type-1 has zero step error but finite ramp error. Memorize this ladder. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Need for Feedback"** - ☐ Feedback = returning a portion of the output to the input for comparison with the reference. - ☐ **Negative** feedback is the universal default (positive feedback causes instability). - ☐ **Six reasons for feedback:** sensitivity reduction, disturbance rejection, steady-state error reduction, bandwidth modification, stabilization, linearization. - ☐ **Sensitivity function:** $S^G_T = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$. Smaller $\vert S \vert$ = more robust. - ☐ **Disturbance transfer (with $D$ entering between $G_1$ and $G_2$):** $\dfrac{Y}{D} = \dfrac{G_2}{1+G_1 G_2 H}$. - ☐ **Closed-loop transfer function:** $T = \dfrac{G}{1+GH}$. - ☐ **Steady-state error ladder:** Step error $= \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$, Ramp error $= \dfrac{1}{K_v}$, Parabolic error $= \dfrac{1}{K_a}$. - ☐ **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Stability requires all roots to lie in the open left-half plane (LHP). - ☐ **Loop gain $\vert G(s)H(s)\vert$ at low frequencies** governs tracking and disturbance rejection; at **high frequencies** it governs noise rejection and robustness margins. - ☐ **Trade-off:** Higher loop gain = better tracking and disturbance rejection, but **poorer stability margins** and **higher noise sensitivity**. - ☐ **Multivariable preview:** When extending to MIMO systems, feedback additionally addresses **loop interaction** and **directionality** — concepts that will reappear in Modules 2 and 3 of PEICT746. > **One-line takeaway:** *"Feedback trades gain for robustness — by spending loop gain, the engineer buys insensitivity, disturbance rejection, and accuracy, but must carefully manage stability."*

topics.title: Model-Free vs. Model-Based Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Model-Free vs. Model-Based Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Multivariable Control Systems**, control strategies are broadly classified into two paradigms based on their dependency on a priori mathematical knowledge of the plant dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Model-Based Control (MBC)**
> A control methodology in which the controller is **synthesized using an explicit, identified mathematical representation** (transfer function matrix $G(s)$ or state-space model $\{A, B, C, D\}$) of the plant. The controller computes its action by solving an optimization or pole-placement problem that *relies on* the model to predict the plant's future response.
> *Examples:* Model Predictive Control (MPC), Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR), Internal Model Control (IMC), $H_\infty$ robust control, and pole-placement state feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Model-Free Control (MFC)**
> A control methodology in which the controller is **synthesized directly from input–output (I/O) measurements** of the plant, **without requiring an explicit parametric model** of the process. The control law is derived from heuristic rules, iterative tuning, or real-time data processing.
> *Examples:* Classical PID, Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC), Iterative Learning Control (ILC), Iterative Feedback Tuning (IFT), Extremum-Seeking Control (ESC), and modern Data-Driven / Reinforcement Learning controllers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving a Car" Intuition

Imagine you are learning to **drive a car** on an unfamiliar mountain road.

| Paradigm | The Analogy | What the Controller "Knows" |
|---|---|---|
| **Model-Based** | A professional rally driver who has studied a **detailed topographical map, vehicle dynamics equations (tire friction, engine torque curves), and GPS elevation data** before even starting the engine. | A complete dynamic model: $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ |
| **Model-Free** | A self-taught driver who simply **watches the road, observes the steering response, and adjusts the wheel through trial-and-error**, perhaps with simple rules like *"if the car drifts right, turn left a little."* | Only the current error $e(t)$ and its rate $\dot e(t)$ — no internal physics equations. |

> [!NOTE]
> The model-free driver is surprisingly robust (he does not care if the road is wet or dry) but is **slower to learn** and cannot perform aggressive optimal maneuvers. The model-based driver is **fast and optimal** when the map is correct, but **crashes spectacularly** if the actual road differs from the map (model–plant mismatch).

---

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in Multivariable Control

In a **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** loop, a well-tuned PID often suffices. However, in a **Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO)** plant, the situation changes dramatically:

- **Cross-coupling** appears (one actuator affects multiple sensors).
- **Directionality** and **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** issues arise.
- **Constraints** on actuators and states become critical.

This is precisely the regime where the *choice* between MBC and MFC dictates whether the closed-loop system meets its performance and robustness specifications.

> [!TIP] **Key KTU 2024 Highlight**
> The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *data-driven* and *learning-based* methods as a **bridge paradigm** — neither purely model-based nor purely model-free. Be ready to discuss this spectrum in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — A Side-by-Side Breakdown

### **A. Model-Based Control (MBC) Operational Pipeline**

1. **System Identification:** Apply PRBS or step signals → estimate $G(s)$ or $\{A,B,C,D\}$ via least-squares / subspace methods.
2. **Model Validation:** Cross-validate the identified model against fresh I/O data; check residuals.
3. **Controller Synthesis:** Solve an optimization problem (e.g., LQR cost, MPC horizon, $H_\infty$ norm bound).
4. **Closed-Loop Implementation:** Use the model *online* (e.g., MPC's internal simulator) to predict and optimize.
5. **Re-tuning (if needed):** If the plant drifts, re-identify and re-synthesize.

### **B. Model-Free Control (MFC) Operational Pipeline**

1. **Define a Control Structure:** Choose PID, fuzzy rule-base, or learning law — based on *qualitative* understanding only.
2. **Tune / Train:** Adjust parameters (e.g., $K_p, K_i, K_d$ for PID) by observing closed-loop response.
3. **Online Adaptation:** Update parameters in real time using gradient estimates of the I/O map.
4. **No Explicit Plant Equations:** The controller treats the plant as a **black box** or **gray box** at most.

---

## 2.2 The Trade-off Triangle: Performance vs. Robustness vs. Effort

```
                 PERFORMANCE
                      ▲
                     /│\
                    / │ \
                   /  │  \
                  /   │   \
                 /    │    \
                /  M  │  B  \      B = Model-Based
               /   B  │  C   \     M = Model-Free
              /       │   \   \
             /────────┼────────\
   EFFORT ──▶                   ROBUSTNESS TO
   (Design Time)                 UNCERTAINTY
```

- **Model-Based** wins on **performance** (optimization) and **multivariable decoupling** (RGA-aware pairing) but demands high design effort and accurate identification.
- **Model-Free** wins on **robustness** to unmodeled dynamics and on **low design effort**, but at the cost of sub-optimal performance and limited handling of constraints.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Aspect | Model-Based Control | Model-Free Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Required Plant Knowledge** | Full mathematical model $G(s)$ or $\{A,B,C,D\}$ | None (black-box) or qualitative (fuzzy rules) |
| 2 | **Control Law (generic form)** | $u = \kappa(x, \theta^*)$ where $\theta^*$ solves $\min J$ using the model | $u = f(e, \int e\, dt, \dot e)$ (PID) or $u = f_\text{NN}(e,y)$ |
| 3 | **Design Equation** | $\min_\theta J = \int_0^\infty (x^\top Q x + u^\top R u)\, dt$ (LQR) | Heuristic / iterative cost reduction: $J_{k+1} < J_k$ |
| 4 | **Stability Guarantee** | Lyapunov / Riccati-based (analytical) | Empirical / iterative proof |
| 5 | **Constraint Handling** | Explicit (MPC's inequality constraints) | Indirect (anti-windup for PID) |
| 6 | **MIMO Coupling Treatment** | Decoupler $\mathbf{D}(s)$ or centralized synthesis | Pairing via RGA + decentralized PID (approximate) |
| 7 | **Computational Load (Online)** | High (MPC solves QP at every step) | Very low (PID) to medium (fuzzy / NN inference) |
| 8 | **Robustness to Uncertainty** | Sensitive — needs robust variants ($H_\infty$, $\mu$-synthesis) | Inherently robust (closed-loop adaptation) |
| 9 | **Typical Industrial Examples** | MPC in refineries, LQR in aerospace | PID in 90% of industrial loops, FLC in washing machines |
| 10 | **Failure Mode** | Model-plant mismatch → instability | Poor tuning / lack of persistent excitation |

> [!WARNING]
> For KTU 2024, do **not** write that MFC is "always worse." Modern data-driven methods (e.g., **VRFT — Virtual Reference Feedback Tuning**, **ADP — Adaptive Dynamic Programming**) are *strictly model-free* yet can **asymptotically match** the LQR benchmark.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Paradigm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrochemical Refineries** | MBC (MPC) | Heavy constraints, slow dynamics, expensive models justified |
| **Consumer Electronics** (drones, cameras) | MFC (PID + FLC) | Cheap, fast, no time to identify per unit |
| **Robotics / Walking Machines** | Hybrid (MBC core + MFC adaptation) | Real-time adaptation to terrain |
| **Power Electronics** | MBC (state feedback) | High switching frequency demands precise model |
| **Process Industries (general)** | MFC (PID) | 95% of regulatory loops in any plant are PID |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU favorite):** *"If you cannot identify a useful model, do not pretend you have one — use model-free. If the system has hard constraints and you can afford the modeling effort, go model-based."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Comparison — Closed-Loop Error Derivation

To make the comparison **analytical** rather than merely conceptual, let us derive how each paradigm approaches the same multivariable tracking problem.

### **Setup**

Consider a stable, square MIMO plant with $m$ inputs and $m$ outputs, described by the transfer function matrix $\mathbf{G}(s) \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times m}$. The control objective is to drive the tracking error $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{r}(t) - \mathbf{y}(t) \to 0$ for a bounded reference $\mathbf{r}(t)$.

The closed-loop transfer function from $\mathbf{r}$ to $\mathbf{e}$ is

$$
\mathbf{E}(s) = \big[\mathbf{I}_m + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{K}(s)\big]^{-1}\mathbf{R}(s)
$$

where $\mathbf{K}(s) \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times m}$ is the controller matrix.

---

### **Derivation 1: Model-Based Synthesis (LQR for SISO simplification)**

For a single-loop SISO plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ and a state-feedback gain $K_f$, the closed-loop characteristic polynomial is

$$
\tau s + 1 + K K_f = 0
$$

Solving for the closed-loop pole $s^*$,

$$
s^* = -\frac{1 + K K_f}{\tau}
$$

The closed-loop time constant is therefore

$$
\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K K_f}
$$

**Interpretation:** By choosing $K_f$ (using the *known* $K$ and $\tau$), we can place the pole at any desired location — this is **pole placement**, a hallmark of model-based design. The faster the desired pole (more negative real part), the larger $K_f$ and the smaller $\tau_{cl}$.

> *However*, this formula uses $K$ and $\tau$ **explicitly**. If the true plant has $K_\text{true} \neq K$, then the actual closed-loop pole is at

$$
s^*_\text{actual} = -\frac{1 + K_\text{true} K_f}{\tau_\text{true}}
$$

which deviates from the design intent. This is the **model-plant mismatch penalty**.

---

### **Derivation 2: Model-Free Synthesis (Ziegler–Nichols PID)**

For the same plant, the Ziegler–Nichols method finds the **ultimate gain** $K_u$ and **ultimate period** $T_u$ empirically (no $K$ or $\tau$ required):

1. Increase proportional gain until sustained oscillation occurs → record $K_u$.
2. Measure the oscillation period → $T_u$.

Then the PID parameters are set as

$$
K_p = 0.6\,K_u, \qquad T_i = 0.5\,T_u, \qquad T_d = 0.125\,T_u
$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation becomes (using the PID form $K_c\big(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\big)$)

$$
\tau s^2 + \big(1 + K K_c T_d\big)s + \frac{K K_c}{T_i} = 0
$$

Comparing to a standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$,

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K K_c}{\tau T_i}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{1 + K K_c T_d}{2\sqrt{\tau K K_c / T_i}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Notice that $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ *implicitly* contain $K$ and $\tau$, but we never needed to know them! The controller "discovered" the necessary dynamics through the closed-loop oscillation experiment.

> *Trade-off:* Z-N tuning gives a known $\zeta \approx 0.21$ (highly underdamped). The designer has **no direct pole-placement authority** — performance is *empirical*, not optimal.

---

### **Derivation 3: The Performance Gap (Quantitative Insight)**

Let us quantify the gap between an *optimal* LQR (model-based) and a *tuned* PID (model-free) for the same plant.

**Step 1:** Define the LQR cost for the plant $G(s) = K / (\tau s + 1)$ in state-space form

$$
\dot{x} = -\frac{1}{\tau} x + \frac{K}{\tau} u, \qquad y = x
$$

The LQR cost is

$$
J_\text{LQR} = \int_0^\infty \big(q\, x^2 + r\, u^2\big)\, dt
$$

**Step 2:** Solve the Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)

$$
A^\top P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^\top P + Q = 0
$$

with $A = -1/\tau$, $B = K/\tau$, $Q = q$, $R = r$:

$$
-\frac{2P}{\tau} - \frac{P^2 K^2}{r \tau^2} + q = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic in $P$ (taking the positive root for definiteness):

$$
P = \frac{-\dfrac{2}{\tau} + \sqrt{\dfrac{4}{\tau^2} + \dfrac{4 K^2 q}{r \tau^2}}}{\dfrac{2 K^2}{r \tau^2}} = \frac{r\tau}{K^2}\left(-1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{K^2 q}{r}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** The optimal LQR gain is

$$
K_f^\text{LQR} = \frac{1}{r} B^\top P = \frac{K P}{r \tau}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P$ from Step 2:

$$
K_f^\text{LQR} = \frac{K}{r \tau} \cdot \frac{r\tau}{K^2}\left(-1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{K^2 q}{r}}\right)
$$

$$
K_f^\text{LQR} = \frac{1}{K}\left(-1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{K^2 q}{r}}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** The minimum LQR cost is

$$
J_\text{LQR}^* = x_0^2 P = x_0^2 \cdot \frac{r\tau}{K^2}\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{K^2 q}{r}} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 6:** For the PID with Z-N tuning, one can show (via Parseval's theorem) that the cost is approximately

$$
J_\text{PID} \approx x_0^2 \cdot \frac{r_\text{eq}\,\tau}{K^2} \cdot \gamma
$$

where $\gamma \in [1.4, 2.0]$ is the empirical *sub-optimality factor* (PID costs roughly 1.5–2× the LQR optimum).

> **Conclusion:** The model-free PID incurs a **50%–100% performance penalty** for this plant — but with zero modeling effort. The model-based LQR achieves the optimum, at the cost of knowing $K$ and $\tau$ exactly.

---

### **3.2 Comparison Matrix for a 2×2 MIMO Plant**

| Criterion | Model-Based (Decentralized LQR + Decoupler) | Model-Free (Two PIDs with RGA pairing) |
|---|---|---|
| Loop interaction term | Compensated by $\mathbf{D}(s) \approx \mathbf{G}^{-1}(s)$ | Ignored; interaction treated as disturbance |
| Steady-state offset | **Zero** (integral action + LQR) | **Zero** (with I-term) |
| Constraint handling | **Yes** (explicit in MPC) | **No** (anti-windup only) |
| Tuning iterations | Low (analytical) | High (trial-and-error) |
| Robustness margin | Requires design margin ($\gamma \geq 6$ dB) | Inherent (high phase margin) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Side-by-Side Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MBC["MODEL-BASED CONTROL LOOP"]
        direction TB
        rM[Reference r] --> sumM1((+))
        sumM1 --> eM[Error e]
        eM --> cM[Controller K]
        cM --> uM[Control u]
        uM --> pM[Plant G]
        pM --> yM[Output y]
        yM --> sumM1
        yM -.-> modelM[(Mathematical Model)]
        modelM -.-> cM
    end

    subgraph MFC["MODEL-FREE CONTROL LOOP"]
        direction TB
        rF[Reference r] --> sumF1((+))
        sumF1 --> eF[Error e]
        eF --> cF[Heuristic / Data-Driven Controller]
        cF --> uF[Control u]
        uF --> pF[Plant G]
        pF --> yF[Output y]
        yF --> sumF1
        yF -.-> tuneF[(I/O Data)]
        tuneF -.-> cF
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **dashed arrow** in MBC from the output to the *model* then to the controller represents the **explicit use of the plant equations** in the control law computation.
- The **dashed arrow** in MFC represents the **empirical / data-driven feedback** used to *adjust* the controller — but no internal model of $G$ is ever formed.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Which Paradigm to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Multivariable Control Problem] --> B{Can you identify\nan accurate model?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Are there hard\nconstraints on u or y?}
    C -- Yes --> D[MBC: Model Predictive Control]
    C -- No --> E{Is the plant highly\nnonlinear?}
    E -- Yes --> F[MBC: Gain-Scheduled LQR\nor Nonlinear MPC]
    E -- No --> G[MBC: LQR / H-infinity]
    B -- No --> H{Is I/O data\nabundant and rich?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Model-Free: Data-Driven Control\nVRFT / ADP / ILC]
    H -- No --> J[Model-Free: PID + FLC\nwith conservative tuning]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **middle box "I"** is the **hybrid bridge** that the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes. It is *model-free* in design (no explicit equations) yet can *match* model-based performance asymptotically.

---

## 4.3 Stability & Robustness Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plant Uncertainty Region] --> B[Nominal Model N]
    B --> C{Uncertainty Bound Δ}
    C -- Small --> D[Model-Based:\nNominal synthesis OK]
    C -- Moderate --> E[Model-Based:\nRobust H-infinity / μ-synthesis]
    C -- Large --> F[Model-Free:\nAdaptive PID / Fuzzy / NN]
    C -- Unknown --> G[Model-Free:\nReinforcement Learning\nor Extremum Seeking]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Distinguish between Model-Based Control (MBC) and Model-Free Control (MFC). Give one example of each used in industrial practice.**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

| Feature | Model-Based Control | Model-Free Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant model required?** | Yes, explicit (TF / SS) | No, treated as black-box |
| **Design method** | Optimization / pole placement | Heuristic / iterative |
| **Industrial example** | MPC in oil refineries | PID in flow / temperature loops |

In MBC, the controller is designed using a *known* dynamic model — for example, **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** uses an internal model to predict future outputs and optimize a cost subject to constraints. In MFC, the controller is tuned from I/O data alone — the **PID controller**, which still dominates over **90% of industrial regulatory loops**, is the canonical example because its three gains can be adjusted (e.g., via Ziegler–Nichols) without any model of the plant.

> **Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for *two correct distinguishing points* + *one correct example per paradigm*. Vague answers (e.g., "MBC uses math") will lose 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What are the main limitations of Model-Free Control in multivariable (MIMO) systems?**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

Model-free controllers, while simple and robust, suffer from three principal limitations in MIMO applications:

1. **Inability to handle cross-coupling explicitly** — interactions between loops are treated as disturbances, not as a structured system property. This leads to **loop interaction** and potential instability when Relative Gain Array (RGA) elements deviate significantly from unity.
2. **No systematic constraint handling** — variables may exceed physical limits, especially during set-point changes or disturbances.
3. **Sub-optimal performance** — without a model, the controller cannot compute an *optimal* action, leading to a performance gap of typically 30–100% relative to model-based optima (LQR / MPC).

> **Examiner's Note:** A fourth valid point (e.g., lack of analytical stability guarantees) earns a partial bonus mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, adapted]**

**(a)** Compare the design philosophy, mathematical requirements, and stability analysis methods of **Model-Based** and **Model-Free** control. Present your answer as a structured comparison table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a first-order plant $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s + 1}$, design **(i)** an LQR state-feedback controller (model-based) and **(ii)** a Ziegler–Nichols PID controller (model-free). Compare the closed-loop step responses in terms of rise time, settling time, and overshoot. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Structured Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Model-Based Control | Model-Free Control |
|---|---|---|
| Design philosophy | First identify, then synthesize using optimization | Tune directly from I/O experiments |
| Mathematical model | Required (e.g., $G(s)$ or $\{A,B,C,D\}$) | Not required |
| Stability analysis | Lyapunov / Riccati / $H_\infty$ | Empirical (Bode, root-locus from closed-loop data) |
| Constraint handling | Explicit (MPC) | Indirect (anti-windup) |
| Computational effort (online) | High for MPC; medium for LQR | Very low (PID) |
| Robustness to model error | Sensitive — needs robust variants | Inherently robust |

**Brief explanatory text (2 marks):**
Model-Based Control treats the plant as a *known* system and computes control actions by solving a model-dependent optimization (e.g., minimizing the LQR cost $J = \int (x^\top Q x + u^\top R u)\,dt$). Model-Free Control instead treats the plant as a *black box* and uses only input–output data to adjust the controller, as in the Ziegler–Nichols PID tuning method.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Convert to state-space form (1 mark)**

The plant $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s+1}$ becomes

$$
\dot x = -\frac{1}{3}\, x + \frac{2}{3}\, u, \qquad y = x
$$

So $A = -\frac{1}{3}$, $B = \frac{2}{3}$, $C = 1$, $D = 0$.

**Step 2: Solve the Algebraic Riccati Equation for LQR (3 marks)**

Choose $Q = 1$, $R = 1$. The ARE is

$$
A^\top P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^\top P + Q = 0
$$

$$
-\frac{2P}{3} - \frac{4 P^2}{9} + 1 = 0
$$

Multiply by $-9/4$:

$$
P^2 + \frac{3}{2}P - \frac{9}{4} = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
P = \frac{-\frac{3}{2} \pm \sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + 9}}{2} = \frac{-\frac{3}{2} \pm \sqrt{\frac{45}{4}}}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
P = \frac{-1.5 + \sqrt{11.25}}{2} = \frac{-1.5 + 3.354}{2} \approx 0.927
$$

**Step 3: Compute the LQR gain (1 mark)**

$$
K_f^\text{LQR} = R^{-1} B^\top P = 1 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.927 \approx 0.618
$$

The closed-loop pole is at $A - B K_f = -\frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3}(0.618) \approx -0.745$, giving a time constant $\tau_{cl}^\text{LQR} \approx 1.34$ s.

**Step 4: Ziegler–Nichols PID (1 mark)**

For $G(s) = 2/(3s+1)$, no sustained oscillation is achievable with proportional-only control (the plant is first-order, no integrator, no delay). The Z-N method in its basic form is **not directly applicable** to a first-order plant without delay. A *modified* approach uses $K_u$ estimated as the gain at which the Nyquist plot crosses the critical point.

A practical alternative is **Cohen–Coon** or direct synthesis, but the *intent* of the question is to highlight that:

- **LQR** gives a clean, optimal response with known pole placement.
- **Z-N PID** is approximate, requires an ultimate-gain experiment, and gives a known underdamped response ($\zeta \approx 0.21$).

**Step 5: Comparison Table (1 mark)**

| Metric | LQR (Model-Based) | Z-N PID (Model-Free) |
|---|---|---|
| Rise time $t_r$ | ≈ $2.2 / 0.745 \approx 2.95$ s | ≈ $1.8 / \omega_n$ (longer) |
| Settling time $t_s$ (2%) | ≈ $4 / 0.745 \approx 5.37$ s | Comparable, with overshoot |
| Overshoot $M_p$ | ≈ 5% (tunable via $Q, R$) | ≈ 50% (typical for Z-N) |
| Modeling required | Yes | No |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams why **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** is classified as a *model-based* technique and why **PID control with auto-tuning** is classified as a *model-free* technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multivariable 2×2 process has the transfer function matrix

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{0.5}{s+1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{0.3}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Discuss the suitability of (i) a *model-free* decentralized PID scheme and (ii) a *model-based* centralized MPC scheme. Use the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** at steady state to justify your recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block diagrams (3 marks):** Provide the Mermaid / hand-drawn block diagrams from **Section 4.1** of these notes, properly labeled to show the model is *inside* the MPC block and *absent* from the auto-tuning PID.

**MPC is model-based (2 marks):** MPC at each sampling instant:
1. Measures the current state $x(k)$.
2. Uses the model $\hat{x}(k+1) = A \hat{x}(k) + B u(k)$ to *predict* future states over a horizon $H_p$.
3. Solves $\min_{u} \sum_{k=0}^{H_p-1} \big(x_k^\top Q x_k + u_k^\top R u_k\big)$ subject to constraints.
4. Applies the first control move $u^*(0)$ and repeats (receding horizon).

The model is *essential* to the optimization — hence **model-based**.

**Auto-tuning PID is model-free (2 marks):** Auto-tuning PID (e.g., relay-feedback method of Åström–Hägglund):
1. Applies a *relay* to the closed loop, inducing a limit cycle.
2. Measures the ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$ from the oscillation.
3. Sets $K_p, T_i, T_d$ from $K_u, T_u$ (Z-N rules).

No transfer function $G(s)$ is ever identified or used; only the *I/O oscillation* data is processed. Hence **model-free**.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the steady-state (DC) gain matrix (2 marks)**

At $s = 0$:

$$
\mathbf{G}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the RGA (3 marks)**

The RGA is defined as $\Lambda = \mathbf{G}(0) \circ \big[\mathbf{G}(0)^{-1}\big]^\top$, where $\circ$ denotes the Hadamard (element-wise) product.

First, invert $\mathbf{G}(0)$:

$$
\det \mathbf{G}(0) = (1)(1) - (0.5)(0.3) = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(0)^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.85}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ -0.3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.176 & -0.588 \\ -0.353 & 1.176 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now the transpose of the inverse:

$$
[\mathbf{G}(0)^{-1}]^\top \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.176 & -0.353 \\ -0.588 & 1.176 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Element-wise product with $\mathbf{G}(0)$:

$$
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \times 1.176 & 0.5 \times (-0.353) \\ 0.3 \times (-0.588) & 1 \times 1.176 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.176 & -0.176 \\ -0.176 & 1.176 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The row sums are $1.176 - 0.176 = 1.0$ ✓ (RGA is row-stochastic by definition).

**Step 3: Interpretation (2 marks)**

- The diagonal elements $\lambda_{11} = \lambda_{22} \approx 1.18$ are **positive and close to 1**, indicating that the *diagonal pairing* (loop 1: $u_1 \to y_1$; loop 2: $u_2 \to y_2$) is **acceptable**.
- The off-diagonal elements are **negative** ($-0.176$), indicating mild but tolerable interaction.
- Since $|\lambda_{ii} - 1| \approx 0.18 < 0.5$, a **decentralized model-free PID** *can* work, but with some performance penalty.

**Recommendation:**

| Approach | Verdict | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Decentralized PID (model-free) | **Workable** | RGA diagonal is ≈ 1, low interaction |
| Centralized MPC (model-based) | **Better** | Compensates cross-coupling, handles constraints |

> **Best Practice:** Even though PID is workable here, the **model-based MPC will yield a 30–50% performance improvement** (smaller ISE) by actively compensating the cross-coupling terms, at the cost of higher online computation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. Confusing **model-free** with **adaptive** — adaptation still requires *some* model structure (e.g., a reference model).
> 2. Stating that PID is "always model-based" because it has a formula — the formula is a *control law*, not a *plant model*. PID gains are tuned empirically; the plant is not modeled.
> 3. In RGA problems, forgetting to **transpose the inverse** before the Hadamard product.
> 4. Failing to mention that the **diagonal RGA pairing must be positive AND close to 1** for decentralized control to be safe.
> 5. Writing "MPC = AI" or "fuzzy = AI" — these are not the same; AI in control implies *learning*, which is a *subset* of model-free methods.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- ✅ **Definition of MBC:** Controller synthesis requires an explicit plant model $G(s)$ or $\{A,B,C,D\}$.
- ✅ **Definition of MFC:** Controller is designed using only I/O data; the plant is a black-box.
- ✅ **Examples of MBC:** MPC, LQR, LQG, $H_\infty$, pole placement, IMC.
- ✅ **Examples of MFC:** PID, FLC, ILC, IFT, ESC, VRFT, ADP, RL-based control.
- ✅ **Three Trade-off Dimensions:** Performance ⇄ Effort ⇄ Robustness.
- ✅ **Model-Plant Mismatch:** The principal weakness of MBC; addressed by robust variants ($H_\infty$, $\mu$-synthesis) or by re-identification.
- ✅ **MIMO Challenge:** Cross-coupling requires either decouplers (MBC) or RGA-based pairing (MFC).
- ✅ **RGA Rule:** If diagonal elements $\lambda_{ii} \in [0.67, 1.5]$, decentralized control is acceptable.
- ✅ **MPC Receding Horizon:** Predict $\to$ Optimize $\to$ Apply first move $\to$ Repeat.
- ✅ **Z-N PID Rule:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- ✅ **Data-Driven Bridge:** VRFT, ADP, and RL form the modern *hybrid* paradigm — strictly model-free in design yet asymptotically optimal in performance.
- ✅ **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** 90%+ of regulatory loops worldwide are PID (MFC); advanced loops in refineries and aerospace are MPC/LQR (MBC).
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions:** Always structure your answer as **(i) a comparison table**, **(ii) an analytical derivation**, **(iii) a numerical example** — KTU examiners reward this format.
- ✅ **Avoid these red-flag phrases:** "MFC is outdated," "MBC is always better," "PID doesn't need math" (it does — Laplace-domain analysis is still required).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Importance of Considering Modelling Errors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Importance of Considering Modelling Errors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Multivariable Control Systems**, a *mathematical model* of a physical plant is never an exact replica of the true system. The discrepancy between the **nominal model** $G_{0}(s)$ (used for controller synthesis) and the **actual plant** $G_{p}(s)$ is termed a **Modelling Error**, **Model Uncertainty**, or **Plant-Model Mismatch**.

Mathematically, it is formally expressed as the *additive uncertainty representation*:

$$
\Delta G(s) = G_{p}(s) - G_{0}(s)
$$

Or equivalently, in the *multiplicative uncertainty* form:

$$
G_{p}(s) = G_{0}(s)\bigl[\,I + \Delta_{m}(s)\,\bigr]
$$

where $\Delta_{m}(s)$ is a stable, unknown perturbation transfer matrix that captures all unmodelled dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Any controller $K(s)$ designed using $G_{0}(s)$ must guarantee **Nominal Stability**, **Nominal Performance**, **Robust Stability**, and **Robust Performance** for all perturbed plants $G_{p}(s) \in \Pi$, where $\Pi$ is the *uncertainty set* (also called the *ball of plants*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Should We Care?

Imagine you are driving a car using a **paper road map (the model)** to navigate a **real city (the plant)**.

| Real-World Driving Analogy | Control Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A road shown on the map is **closed** for construction | **Unmodelled Dynamics** — high-frequency modes neglected during model derivation |
| The map's scale is slightly off (distance error) | **Parametric Uncertainty** — values of $L$, $R$, $C$, $J$, $b$ are approximate |
| Traffic rules changed since the map was printed | **Environmental Drift** — operating point shifts due to temperature, aging |
| A flyover is missing on the paper map | **Structural Uncertainty** — system order is higher than assumed |

If you blindly trust the map, you may take a wrong turn, or worse, end up in a dead-end. Similarly, if we design a controller assuming the nominal model is perfect, the **closed-loop system may become unstable, sluggish, oscillatory, or even dangerous** when deployed on the real plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The *real plant is not a point in parameter space — it is a region (or ball)*. A robust controller must perform well over the **entire ball**, not just at the centre (nominal point).

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Stability Margin:** typically $\geq 6\,\text{dB}$ gain margin and $\geq 45^{\circ}$ phase margin (classical SISO benchmark).
- **Robustness Bound:** $\bar{\sigma}\bigl(\Delta_{m}(j\omega)\bigr) \leq \ell_{m}(\omega)$, where $\bar{\sigma}$ is the **maximum singular value** and $\ell_{m}(\omega)$ is the **multiplicative uncertainty weight**.
- **Engineering tolerance band:** typical industrial actuators carry $\pm \mathbf{5\%}$ to $\pm \mathbf{10\%}$ parametric tolerance on stated gains.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Modelling Errors

The modelling error $\Delta G(s)$ in a physical multivariable plant arises from **five primary sources**:

1. **Parametric Uncertainty** — Inexact knowledge of physical parameters:
   - Resistor $R$ has $\pm 5\%$ tolerance.
   - Inertia $J$ varies with load.
   - Fluid viscosity $\mu$ changes with temperature.

2. **Unmodelled High-Frequency Dynamics** — When deriving state-space models, designers often neglect:
   - Actuator time-constants (e.g., valve dynamics $\tau_{v}$).
   - Sensor noise filters.
   - Resonant modes of mechanical structures.
   - Transport delays (e.g., $\tau_{d}$ in chemical pipelines).

3. **Linearisation Errors** — Most control design assumes **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** models. The linearisation of a nonlinear plant $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ about an operating point $(x_{0}, u_{0})$ gives:
   $$ \Delta \dot{x} = A\Delta x + B\Delta u + \underbrace{\text{H.O.T.}}_{\text{Higher-Order Terms}} $$
   The H.O.T. constitute a *linearisation error* that grows with the deviation from the operating point.

4. **Environmental / Operating Point Drift** — Plant dynamics depend on ambient conditions:
   - Aircraft dynamics change with altitude, Mach number, and payload.
   - Chemical reactor gains vary with catalyst deactivation.

5. **Reduced-Order Modelling** — A 50-state finite-element model is often truncated to a 6-state lumped model for control design. The 44 discarded states become *unmodelled dynamics*.

### 2.2 Classification of Uncertainty Representations

| Representation | Mathematical Form | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Additive** | $G_{p} = G_{0} + \Delta_{a}$ | Sensor/actuator additive disturbances |
| **Multiplicative (Output)** | $G_{p} = (I + \Delta_{m})G_{0}$ | Actuator gain uncertainty |
| **Multiplicative (Input)** | $G_{p} = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{i})$ | Sensor-side gain uncertainty |
| **Inverse Multiplicative** | $G_{p} = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{i}G_{0})^{-1}$ | Right-coprime factorisation |
| **Coprime Factor** | $G_{p} = (M + \Delta_{M})^{-1}(N + \Delta_{N})$ | Robust stabilisation via $H_{\infty}$ / $H_{2}$ |
| **LFT (Linear Fractional Transform)** | $G_{p} = \mathcal{F}_{u}(P, \Delta)$ | General structured uncertainty |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\Delta$ denotes a stable, unknown perturbation matrix. The block $\Delta$ is assumed to satisfy $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta(j\omega)) \leq 1 \;\;\forall\,\omega$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Concept} & \textbf{Formula / Condition} & \textbf{Units / Note} \\
\hline
\text{Additive error} & \Delta_{a}(s) = G_{p}(s) - G_{0}(s) & \text{Transfer matrix} \\
\hline
\text{Multiplicative error} & \Delta_{m}(s) = G_{0}^{-1}(s)\,\Delta_{a}(s) & \text{Dimensionless} \\
\hline
\text{Nominal closed-loop} & T_{0}(s) = G_{0}K(I - G_{0}K)^{-1} & \text{Idealised response} \\
\hline
\text{Perturbed closed-loop} & T_{p}(s) = G_{p}K(I - G_{p}K)^{-1} & \text{True response} \\
\hline
\text{Stability robustness (multiplicative)} & \bar{\sigma}\bigl[T_{0}(j\omega)\bigr] < \ell_{m}^{-1}(\omega) & \text{Small-gain form} \\
\hline
\text{Stability robustness (additive)} & \bar{\sigma}\bigl[K(I - G_{0}K)^{-1}\bigr] < \ell_{a}^{-1}(\omega) & \text{Loops at output} \\
\hline
\text{Coprime uncertainty bound} & \bigl\Vert \Delta_{M},\,\Delta_{N}\bigr\Vert_{\infty} < \gamma & \gamma = \text{robustness margin} \\
\hline
\text{Linearisation residual} & r(x,u) = f(x,u) - A\Delta x - B\Delta u & \text{H.O.T. neglected} \\
\hline
\text{Small-gain condition} & \Vert G_{1}\Vert_{\infty}\,\Vert G_{2}\Vert_{\infty} < 1 & \text{Ensures robust stability} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 2.4 Why Is This Important in Multivariable Control?

In **MIMO** (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) systems, the situation worsens dramatically compared to SISO:

- **Directionality Issue:** Singular values $\sigma_{i}$ of $G(j\omega)$ describe the *gain* of the system in different input directions. Modelling error in one channel can be amplified by a controller designed in another direction due to **RGA (Relative Gain Array)** interactions.
- **Condition Number $\kappa(G)$:** A plant with large $\kappa$ is *ill-conditioned*; small modelling errors cause large performance degradation.
- **Cross-Coupling:** Off-diagonal terms in $G_{0}$ are notoriously hard to model accurately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Ignoring modelling errors is the **#1 cause of controller failure in industrial deployment**. Robust control design (e.g., $H_{\infty}$, $\mu$-synthesis) explicitly embeds uncertainty bounds $\ell(\omega)$ into the synthesis procedure to deliver controllers that work despite the inevitable plant-model mismatch.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Linearisation to Modelling Error

Consider a **single-input nonlinear plant**:

$$
\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u, \qquad y = h(x)
$$

We seek an LTI model about the equilibrium point $(x_{0}, u_{0})$ satisfying $0 = f(x_{0}) + g(x_{0})u_{0}$.

**Step 1.** Define small deviations $\tilde{x} = x - x_{0}$ and $\tilde{u} = u - u_{0}$.

**Step 2.** Expand $f(x_{0} + \tilde{x})$ using a multivariate Taylor series:

$$
f(x_{0} + \tilde{x}) = f(x_{0}) + \underbrace{\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x_{0}}\,\tilde{x}}_{A\tilde{x}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\tilde{x}^{T}\,\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}\,\tilde{x} + \cdots}_{\text{H.O.T.} = r(\tilde{x})}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute back into the nonlinear ODE:

$$
\dot{\tilde{x}} = A\tilde{x} + B\tilde{u} + r(\tilde{x}) + s(\tilde{x},\tilde{u})
$$

**Step 4.** Define the **linearised model** as the nominal plant:

$$
G_{0}(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D
$$

**Step 5.** The **modelling error** (neglected higher-order terms) is therefore:

$$
\Delta G(s) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; r(\tilde{x}) + s(\tilde{x},\tilde{u}) \quad \text{in state-space form}
$$

> This derivation shows that *every* LTI model obtained by linearisation carries an inherent modelling error whose magnitude depends on the operating-point deviation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Robust Stability Condition (Multiplicative Uncertainty)

Consider a unity negative-feedback system with nominal plant $G_{0}$, controller $K$, and true plant $G_{p} = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{m})$.

**Step 1.** The nominal closed-loop transfer matrix is:

$$
T_{0}(s) = G_{0}(s)K(s)\bigl[I - G_{0}(s)K(s)\bigr]^{-1}
$$

**Step 2.** The perturbed closed-loop transfer matrix is:

$$
T_{p}(s) = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{m})K\bigl[I - G_{0}(I + \Delta_{m})K\bigr]^{-1}
$$

**Step 3.** A sufficient condition for the perturbed closed-loop to remain stable (using the **Small Gain Theorem**) is:

$$
\bar{\sigma}\bigl(\Delta_{m}(j\omega)\bigr)\,\bar{\sigma}\bigl(T_{0}(j\omega)\bigr) < 1 \quad \forall\,\omega \in [0,\infty)
$$

**Step 4.** This is equivalently written using the uncertainty weight $\ell_{m}(\omega)$:

$$
\bar{\sigma}\bigl(T_{0}(j\omega)\bigr) < \frac{1}{\ell_{m}(\omega)} \quad \forall\,\omega
$$

where $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_{m}) \leq \ell_{m}(\omega)$.

**Step 5.** In the $\log$-scale (dB), this becomes the **classical robustness constraint**:

$$
20\log_{10}\bar{\sigma}\bigl(T_{0}(j\omega)\bigr) < -20\log_{10}\ell_{m}(\omega) \quad \text{dB}
$$

This is the celebrated "loop-shaping" inequality: the complementary sensitivity $T_{0}$ must lie **below** the inverse uncertainty bound for *all* frequencies.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Example — SISO Illustration

Suppose $G_{0}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ and the true plant is $G_{p}(s) = \dfrac{1.2}{s+1.1}$ (gain error $+20\%$, pole error $+10\%$).

**Step 1.** Compute $\Delta_{m}(s)$:

$$
\Delta_{m}(s) = G_{0}^{-1}(s)\,G_{p}(s) - I = \frac{s+1}{1.2/(s+1.1)} \cdot \frac{1}{(s+1)} - 1
$$

**Step 2.** Simplify:

$$
G_{0}^{-1}G_{p} = \frac{(s+1)(s+1.1)}{1.2(s+1)} = \frac{s+1.1}{1.2}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$
\Delta_{m}(s) = \frac{s+1.1}{1.2} - 1 = \frac{s - 0.1}{1.2}
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate $\vert \Delta_{m}(j\omega)\vert$:

$$
\vert \Delta_{m}(j\omega)\vert = \frac{\sqrt{\omega^{2} + 0.01}}{1.2}
$$

This is a *low-pass* uncertainty profile — small at low $\omega$ and growing unboundedly at high $\omega$, illustrating the typical structure of multiplicative modelling errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Closed-Loop with Model Uncertainty

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["Reference r(t)"]:::sig --> SumA["Summing Junction"]
    SumA --> K["Controller K(s)"]
    K --> Gp["True Plant G_p(s) = G_0 * I + Delta_m"]
    Gp --> Y["Output y(t)"]
    Y --> SumB["Feedback"]
    SumB --> SumA

    subgraph UncBlock["Modelling Error Block"]
        G0["Nominal Model G_0(s)"] --> Diff["Difference: G_p - G_0"]
        Diff --> Delta["Uncertainty Delta_m(s)"]
    end

    Gp -.-> UncBlock
    UncBlock -.-> K

    classDef sig fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2E86C1,color:#1B4F72;
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Sources-of-Error Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Modelling Errors in Multivariable Control"]:::root --> B["Parametric"]
    A --> C["Unmodelled Dynamics"]
    A --> D["Linearisation"]
    A --> E["Environmental Drift"]
    A --> F["Reduced Order"]

    B --> B1["R, L, C, J, b tolerances"]
    C --> C1["Actuator, sensor, transport delay"]
    D --> D1["H.O.T. residual r(x,u)"]
    E --> E1["Temp, humidity, aging"]
    F --> F1["Truncated high-order states"]

    A --> G["Robustness Methods"]:::meth
    G --> G1["H_infinity synthesis"]
    G --> G2["mu-synthesis"]
    G --> G3["Sliding mode"]
    G --> G4["QFT"]

    classDef root fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B,color:#7D6608;
    classDef meth fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56,color:#196F3D;
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Robust Stability Test (Small-Gain Form)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W["Uncertainty Weight l_m(w)"] --> Mloop["M-Delta Loop T_0 * l_m"]
    Mloop --> Chk{"Bar sigma < 1 ?"}
    Chk -- "Yes" --> Stab["ROBUSTLY STABLE"]
    Chk -- "No" --> Unst["NOT ROBUST: Reduce K gain or redesign"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above architecture captures the **M-$\Delta$** structure fundamental to robust control. Any modern $H_{\infty}$ or $\mu$-synthesis problem in KTU's Multivariable Control syllabus is reducible to this canonical form.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Define modelling error. List any four sources of modelling error in a multivariable control system.*

**Model Answer:**
Modelling error is the discrepancy between the mathematical model $G_{0}(s)$ used for controller design and the true physical plant $G_{p}(s)$, expressed as $\Delta G(s) = G_{p}(s) - G_{0}(s)$. Four sources are:
1. **Parametric uncertainty** — inexact physical parameters ($R, L, J, b$).
2. **Unmodelled dynamics** — neglected high-frequency modes, time-delays.
3. **Linearisation error** — higher-order Taylor terms dropped.
4. **Environmental drift** — operating point changes with temperature, aging.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*Distinguish between additive and multiplicative uncertainty representations with mathematical expressions.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Additive | Multiplicative |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $G_{p} = G_{0} + \Delta_{a}$ | $G_{p} = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{m})$ |
| Interpretation | Plant deviation is added at output | Plant deviation scales the nominal |
| Magnitude check | $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_{a}) \leq \ell_{a}$ | $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_{m}) \leq \ell_{m}$ |
| Typical use | Sensor noise / bias | Actuator gain drift |

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the importance of considering modelling errors in the design of multivariable control systems. Discuss the consequences of neglecting these errors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A nominal plant is $G_{0}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$. The actual plant is $G_{p}(s) = \dfrac{1.1}{s+1.9}$. Determine:
(i) the multiplicative uncertainty $\Delta_{m}(s)$, and
(ii) the magnitude $\vert \Delta_{m}(j\omega)\vert$ at $\omega = 1$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Modelling errors are inevitable because every mathematical model is a *simplified abstraction* of a complex physical system. Their consideration is critical for three reasons:

1. **Robust Stability:** A controller $K(s)$ synthesised for $G_{0}(s)$ may destabilise $G_{p}(s)$ if $\Delta G$ is ignored. The **Small Gain Theorem** quantifies the safety margin needed.

2. **Performance Degradation:** Even if closed-loop stability is preserved, the bandwidth, overshoot, and tracking error of $T_{p}(s)$ may differ substantially from $T_{0}(s)$. For MIMO systems, **directionality** of errors can corrupt decoupled control.

3. **Safety & Reliability:** In aerospace, nuclear, and process industries, an unmodelled resonance can lead to structural failure. The **Bode Integral / Waterbed Effect** further constrains achievable performance, making explicit uncertainty handling mandatory.

Consequences of neglecting modelling errors include: closed-loop instability, sluggish or oscillatory response, violation of safety margins, and economic losses from plant shutdowns.

**Model Solution (b):**

*(i)* Compute $\Delta_{m}(s)$:

$$
\Delta_{m}(s) = G_{0}^{-1}(s)\,G_{p}(s) - I
$$

$$
G_{0}^{-1}(s) = s+2, \quad G_{0}^{-1}(s)G_{p}(s) = \frac{s+2}{1.1/(s+1.9)} = \frac{(s+2)(s+1.9)}{1.1}
$$

$$
\Delta_{m}(s) = \frac{(s+2)(s+1.9)}{1.1} - 1 = \frac{(s+2)(s+1.9) - 1.1}{1.1}
$$

Expanding: $(s+2)(s+1.9) = s^{2} + 3.9s + 3.8$. Thus:

$$
\Delta_{m}(s) = \frac{s^{2} + 3.9s + 2.7}{1.1}
$$

*(ii)* At $\omega = 1$, $s = j$:

$$
\Delta_{m}(j) = \frac{-1 + 3.9j + 2.7}{1.1} = \frac{1.7 + 3.9j}{1.1}
$$

$$
\vert \Delta_{m}(j)\vert = \frac{\sqrt{1.7^{2} + 3.9^{2}}}{1.1} = \frac{\sqrt{2.89 + 15.21}}{1.1} = \frac{\sqrt{18.1}}{1.1} \approx \frac{4.254}{1.1} \approx 3.87
$$

Since $\vert \Delta_{m}\vert \gg 1$, the uncertainty is *not* small — controller design must accommodate this large plant-model mismatch.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, derive the **multiplicative robust stability condition** for a SISO unity-feedback system using the small-gain theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain *coprime factor uncertainty* representation. A plant $G_{0}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s-1}$ (unstable) is to be robustly stabilised. State the coprime factors $M$ and $N$ and the condition under which a controller $K$ robustly stabilises all plants in the set $\mathcal{G}_{\gamma}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Refer to **Section 3.2** above for the full derivation. The final robust stability condition is:

$$
\bar{\sigma}\bigl(T_{0}(j\omega)\bigr) < \frac{1}{\ell_{m}(\omega)} \quad \forall\,\omega
$$

In SISO notation, this reduces to:

$$
\vert T_{0}(j\omega)\vert < \frac{1}{\ell_{m}(\omega)} \;\;\forall\;\omega \in [0,\infty)
$$

**Block Diagram** (reproduced in conceptual form):

> Reference $r$ → [+/-] → Controller $K$ → Plant $G_{p} = G_{0}(1+\Delta_{m})$ → Output $y$ → feedback to summing junction.

**Model Solution (b):**
**Coprime factor representation:** The plant $G_{0}$ is written as $G_{0} = M^{-1}N$ where $M, N \in \mathcal{RH}_{\infty}$ (stable proper transfer matrices) and satisfy $M M^{*} + N N^{*} = I$ (normalized coprime factorisation). The perturbed plant set is:

$$
\mathcal{G}_{\gamma} = \{(M + \Delta_{M})^{-1}(N + \Delta_{N}) : \Vert[\Delta_{M}\;\;\Delta_{N}]\Vert_{\infty} < \gamma\}
$$

For $G_{0}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s-1}$: a coprime factorisation is $N(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ and $M(s) = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1}$, satisfying $M + N G_{0} = 0$ identically.

A controller $K$ robustly stabilises the entire set $\mathcal{G}_{\gamma}$ if and only if:

$$
\gamma < \bigl(1 - \Vert[ N_{K} \;\; M_{K}]\,\bigr)^{-1}
$$

where $K = \tilde{M}_{K}^{-1}\tilde{N}_{K}$ is the right-coprime factorisation of the controller. This is the celebrated **Glover–McFarlane $H_{\infty}$ loop-shaping** result.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often forget that $\Delta_{m}(s)$ is *dimensionless* while $\Delta_{a}(s)$ has *plant units*. Mixing them leads to wrong bounds.
> 2. **Small-Gain Misapplication:** The condition is **sufficient but not necessary** for SISO; for MIMO with structured $\Delta$, use $\mu$ (structured singular value), not $\bar{\sigma}$.
> 3. **Coprime Factor Sign Error:** Mistaking left-coprime vs. right-coprime factorisation is a 3-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> 4. **Skipping $\omega$-dependence:** Students frequently write $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_{m}) < 1$ as a constant; always state "$\forall\,\omega$" for full marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modelling error** $\Delta G = G_{p} - G_{0}$ is *inevitable* in every practical control system.
- Two standard representations: **additive** $G_{p} = G_{0} + \Delta_{a}$ and **multiplicative** $G_{p} = G_{0}(I + \Delta_{m})$.
- The **Small-Gain Theorem** gives the sufficient robust stability condition: $\bar{\sigma}(T_{0}) < 1/\ell_{m}(\omega)$.
- **Coprime factor uncertainty** is the most general form and is the basis of $H_{\infty}$ robust stabilisation.
- **MIMO-specific concerns:** directionality, RGA, condition number $\kappa(G)$ amplify the effect of small modelling errors.
- **Robust control methods:** $H_{\infty}$, $\mu$-synthesis, QFT, sliding mode — all explicitly account for modelling errors.
- **Bode's integral** imposes a fundamental trade-off: improving nominal performance at low $\omega$ worsens robustness at high $\omega$.
- Always evaluate $\bar{\sigma}(\Delta_{m}(j\omega))$ across the **entire frequency range** $[0, \infty)$, not just at DC.
- Linearisation error is a *function of operating point* — the same plant has different $\Delta G$ at different setpoints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivariable Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multivariable Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multivariable System** (also called a **MIMO – Multiple-Input Multiple-Output** system) is a dynamic system in which the number of manipulated (control) inputs is greater than or equal to **two**, AND the number of controlled outputs is also greater than or equal to **two**. Formally, it is a system described by a strictly proper rational **Transfer Function Matrix (TFM)** $\mathbf{G}(s)$ of dimensions $p \times m$, where $m \geq 2$ and $p \geq 2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** "A system with more than one input and more than one output is known as a multivariable system; it requires careful study because of coupling between input–output pairs."

The general input–output relationship in the Laplace domain is:

$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$

where $\mathbf{u}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the vector of inputs, $\mathbf{y}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the vector of outputs, and $\mathbf{G}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ is the **transfer function matrix**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cockpit of an Aircraft

Imagine you are piloting an aircraft. You do **not** have just one knob and one dial. You have:

- **Inputs (Actuators):** Ailerons, Elevator, Rudder, Throttle → $m = 4$
- **Outputs (Measured Variables):** Roll angle, Pitch angle, Yaw angle, Altitude → $p = 4$

When you deflect the **aileron** (an input), it primarily affects **roll**, but it ALSO slightly affects **yaw** and **pitch** because of aerodynamic coupling. Likewise, the rudder mainly affects yaw but couples into roll. This is the **hallmark of a multivariable system**: *every input affects every output to some degree.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A SISO system is like a single water tap controlling one bucket. A multivariable (MIMO) system is like a shower panel with multiple taps and showerheads — twisting one knob changes the pressure and temperature of several outlets simultaneously, because the internal pipes are interconnected.

## 1.3 Classification of Linear Systems by I/O Count

| Class | Inputs $(m)$ | Outputs $(p)$ | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SISO** | 1 | 1 | Domestic room heater with single thermostat |
| **SIMO** | 1 | $>1$ | Single loudspeaker producing pressure at multiple microphones |
| **MISO** | $>1$ | 1 | Two pumps feeding one reservoir |
| **MIMO (Multivariable)** | $\geq 2$ | $\geq 2$ | Distillation column, aircraft autopilot, robotic arm |

## 1.4 Why Study Multivariable Systems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industrial process plants are overwhelmingly multivariable. In a typical refinery, a Crude Distillation Unit alone has **20–40** controlled variables and an equal number of manipulated variables. Treating each loop independently (the classical SISO approach) **fails** because of interaction, so a dedicated multivariable control theory is mandated — this is the focus of the entire course PEICT746.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representations of a Multivariable System

A linear time-invariant (LTI) multivariable system can be represented in **three equivalent formalisms**.

### 2.1.1 Transfer Function Matrix (TFM) Form

The output vector is related to the input vector by:

$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s) + \mathbf{G}_{d}(s)\,\mathbf{d}(s)$$

where $\mathbf{G}(s)$ is the **plant TFM** and $\mathbf{G}_{d}(s)$ is the **disturbance TFM**.

### 2.1.2 Individual Element Interpretation

For a $2 \times 2$ system, the matrix equation expands as:

$$y_1(s) = g_{11}(s)\,u_1(s) + g_{12}(s)\,u_2(s)$$
$$y_2(s) = g_{21}(s)\,u_1(s) + g_{22}(s)\,u_2(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The element $g_{ij}(s)$ is the SISO transfer function from the $j^{th}$ input to the $i^{th}$ output when **all other inputs are held at zero** (superposition principle). The **diagonal elements** are the *primary* or *direct* loops, while the **off-diagonal elements** represent *interaction* or *coupling*.

### 2.1.3 State-Space Form

A continuous-time LTI multivariable system in state space is given by:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$
$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$

with matrix dimensions: $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, $\mathbf{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$.

The TFM is obtained via the resolvent formula:

$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}$$

### 2.1.4 Discrete-Time Equivalent

For computer-controlled or sampled-data systems:

$$\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \mathbf{A}_d\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{B}_d\,\mathbf{u}(k)$$
$$\mathbf{y}(k) = \mathbf{C}_d\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{D}_d\,\mathbf{u}(k)$$

with pulse transfer function matrix:

$$\mathbf{G}(z) = \mathbf{C}_d(z\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_d)^{-1}\mathbf{B}_d + \mathbf{D}_d$$

## 2.2 Essential Multivariable Terminology

- **Inputs/Manipulated Variables (MVs):** Variables adjusted by the controller.
- **Outputs/Controlled Variables (CVs):** Variables to be maintained at desired setpoints.
- **Disturbances (DVs):** Unmeasured load changes affecting outputs.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** The number $m$ of independent MVs.
- **Square System:** When $p = m$ (equal number of inputs and outputs).
- **Non-square System:** When $p \neq m$ (rectangular TFM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Square System Convention:** Most textbooks (and KTU) emphasize *square* systems of dimension $n \times n$ for analysis. For non-square systems, pseudo-inverse or row/column elimination is applied to first make the system square.

## 2.3 The Two Pillars of Multivariable Control Architecture

1. **Centralized Control:** A single controller matrix $\mathbf{K}(s)$ acts on the full error vector $\mathbf{e}(s) = \mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{y}(s)$. This is mathematically optimal but requires perfect tuning of all elements.
2. **Decentralized Control:** $m$ independent SISO controllers $k_i(s)$ are placed on the diagonal; the off-diagonal interaction is either ignored or treated as a disturbance. This is the most widely used approach in industry (≈ 90 % of loops).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Definition | Dimensionality |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$ | Multivariable I/O equation | $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$, $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| $\mathbf{G}(s) = [g_{ij}(s)]_{p \times m}$ | Transfer Function Matrix (TFM) | $p \times m$ |
| $g_{ij}(s) = \dfrac{Y_i(s)}{U_j(s)}\Big\vert_{U_k=0,\;k\neq j}$ | Individual SISO TF element | scalar |
| $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$ | Continuous state-space model | $n, m, p$ are dimensions |
| $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}$ | State-space to TFM conversion | $p \times m$ |
| $\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \mathbf{A}_d\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{B}_d\mathbf{u}(k)$ | Discrete state-space model | sampled-data |
| $\sigma\{\mathbf{G}(j\omega)\}$ | Singular values of TFM | frequency-dependent |
| $\det\{\mathbf{G}(s)\}$ | Characteristic scalar for square systems | scalar polynomial |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule (Hand-graded exam):** Always underline or bold the symbol for a *vector/matrix* (e.g., $\mathbf{G}$) and keep regular font for scalar transfer functions (e.g., $g_{ij}$). Examiners specifically look for this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From a $2 \times 2$ Block Diagram to the TFM

Consider two SISO plants $g_{11}(s)$ and $g_{22}(s)$ that are *purely independent*. Their individual block diagrams are:

- $u_1 \rightarrow g_{11} \rightarrow y_1$
- $u_2 \rightarrow g_{22} \rightarrow y_2$

**Step 1 — Write the decoupled equations.**

$$y_1(s) = g_{11}(s)\,u_1(s)$$
$$y_2(s) = g_{22}(s)\,u_2(s)$$

**Step 2 — Introduce physical coupling.** In real processes, a fraction of $u_2$ also reaches $y_1$ through path $g_{12}$, and a fraction of $u_1$ reaches $y_2$ through $g_{21}$. By superposition, we **add** these contributions:

$$y_1(s) = g_{11}(s)\,u_1(s) + g_{12}(s)\,u_2(s)$$
$$y_2(s) = g_{21}(s)\,u_1(s) + g_{22}(s)\,u_2(s)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to compact matrix form.** Factor out the input vector:

$$\begin{bmatrix} y_1(s) \\ y_2(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11}(s) & g_{12}(s) \\ g_{21}(s) & g_{22}(s) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_1(s) \\ u_2(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

Thus,

$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$

with

$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11}(s) & g_{12}(s) \\ g_{21}(s) & g_{22}(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Disturbance addition (process reality).** Real plants also suffer from load disturbances $d_1, d_2$ entering additively at each output. This is included as:

$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s) + \mathbf{G}_d(s)\,\mathbf{d}(s)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: TFM from State-Space Realisation

**Given:** $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$, $\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times m}$, $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{p\times n}$, $\mathbf{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{p\times m}$.

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of the state equation.** Assuming zero initial conditions:

$$s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\mathbf{X}(s)$.** Move the $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$ term to the left and factor:

$$(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})\,\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

Left-multiply both sides by the inverse matrix $(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{X}(s) = (s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the output equation.**

$$\mathbf{Y}(s) = \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

Replace $\mathbf{X}(s)$:

$$\mathbf{Y}(s) = \mathbf{C}\,(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{U}(s) + \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

**Step 4 — Group $\mathbf{U}(s)$ and identify $\mathbf{G}(s)$.**

$$\mathbf{Y}(s) = \big[\,\mathbf{C}\,(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\,\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}\,\big]\,\mathbf{U}(s)$$

Therefore,

$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{C}\,(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\,\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $(s\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ is called the **resolvent** of $\mathbf{A}$. Its determinant $\det(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})$ defines the characteristic polynomial whose roots are the system poles (eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — $2 \times 2$ TFM from State Matrices

**Given:** For a system with $n = 2$, $m = 2$, $p = 2$:
