## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define a Transfer Function Matrix (TFM) for a MIMO system. How does it differ from the SISO transfer function? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **Transfer Function Matrix** $\mathbf{G}(s)$ for a $q$-output, $p$-input MIMO system is a $q \times p$ matrix whose $(i,j)$-th element $G_{ij}(s)$ is the Laplace-transformed output at port $i$ divided by the Laplace-transformed input at port $j$, with all other inputs set to zero:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} G_{11}(s) & G_{12}(s) & \cdots & G_{1p}(s) \\ G_{21}(s) & G_{22}(s) & \cdots & G_{2p}(s) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ G_{q1}(s) & G_{q2}(s) & \cdots & G_{qp}(s) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The SISO transfer function is a $1 \times 1$ scalar $G(s)$. The TFM reduces to SISO when $q = p = 1$. Crucially, in MIMO systems the off-diagonal terms $G_{ij}(s)$ ($i \neq j$) represent **inter-channel coupling**, which has no SISO counterpart. (3 Marks)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State the closed-loop transfer function matrix for a negative-feedback interconnection of a forward path $\mathbf{G}(s)$ and a feedback path $\mathbf{H}(s)$. Why is matrix order important? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The closed-loop TFM is:

$$
\mathbf{T}(s) = \bigl[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\bigr]^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)
$$

**Matrix order is critical** because matrix multiplication is non-commutative: in general $\mathbf{GH} \neq \mathbf{HG}$. The expression $\mathbf{GH}$ is the *loop gain matrix* whose determinant appears in the denominator of the closed-loop characteristic equation $\det(\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}) = 0$. Reversing the product changes the closed-loop eigenvalues and hence the stability of the system. (3 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Two MIMO subsystems $\mathbf{G}_1(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s)$ are given by:

$$
\mathbf{G}_1(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\ 0 & \dfrac{1}{s} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{G}_2(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+3} & 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+4} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determine the composite TFM when:
  (i) The two subsystems are connected in **cascade** (with $\mathbf{G}_1$ first, then $\mathbf{G}_2$). (4 Marks)
  (ii) The two subsystems are connected in **parallel**. (3 Marks)

**(b)** A unity-feedback MIMO system has a forward path:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{1}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Derive the closed-loop TFM $\mathbf{T}(s)$ and determine the characteristic equation. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Cascade: $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{G}_2(s)\,\mathbf{G}_1(s)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{G}(s) &= \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+3} & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+4} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[4pt] 0 & \dfrac{1}{s} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute element-by-element:

- $G_{11} = \dfrac{1}{s+3}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+1} + 0 \cdot 0 = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+3)}$

- $G_{12} = \dfrac{1}{s+3}\cdot\dfrac{2}{s+2} + 0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{2}{(s+2)(s+3)}$

- $G_{21} = \dfrac{1}{s+1}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+4}\cdot 0 = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}$

- $G_{22} = \dfrac{1}{s+1}\cdot\dfrac{2}{s+2} + \dfrac{1}{s+4}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} + \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{G}_{\text{casc}}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+3)} & \dfrac{2}{(s+2)(s+3)} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2} & \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} + \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

(4 Marks)

**(a)(ii) Parallel: $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{G}_{\text{par}}(s) &= \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} + 0 \\[6pt] 0 + \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s} + \dfrac{1}{s+4} \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{2s+4}{(s+1)(s+3)} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{2s+4}{s(s+4)} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 Marks)

**(b) Unity-Feedback Closed-Loop TFM**

With $\mathbf{H}(s) = \mathbf{I}$:

$$
\mathbf{T}(s) = \bigl[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\bigr]^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)
$$

Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)$:

$$
\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{s+3} & 1 + \dfrac{1}{s+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the determinant:

$$
\det(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G}) = \left(\dfrac{s+2}{s+1}\right)^2 - \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{(s+2)^2(s+2)(s+3) - (s+1)^2}{(s+1)^2(s+2)(s+3)}
$$

Numerator expansion:

$$
(s+2)^2(s+2)(s+3) = (s+2)^3(s+3)
$$

After algebraic expansion (letting $N = (s+2)^3(s+3) - (s+1)^2$):

$$
N = (s+2)^3(s+3) - (s+1)^2
$$

Step 3 — Adjugate and inverse:

$$
(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} & -\dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] -\dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 — Multiply by $\mathbf{G}(s)$ to obtain $\mathbf{T}(s)$:

$$
\mathbf{T}(s) = \dfrac{1}{N} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} & -\dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] -\dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{s+3} & \dfrac{1}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Carrying out the multiplication (each element is a rational function), and identifying the characteristic equation:

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\text{cl}}(s) = (s+2)^3(s+3) - (s+1)^2 = 0}
$$

Stability is determined by the location of the roots of this polynomial in the open left-half plane. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A multivariable system is formed by the series interconnection of two state-space subsystems:

$$
\Sigma_1: \mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{B}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{C}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{D}_1 = 0
$$

$$
\Sigma_2: \mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -5 & -4 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{B}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{C}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix},\ \mathbf{D}_2 = 0
$$

Determine the augmented state-space matrices $(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{D})$ for the composite system. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A two-loop signal flow graph has the following paths and loops:
- Forward path $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$
- Forward path $P_2 = -G_1 G_4$
- Loop $L_1 = -G_2 H_1$ (touches both $P_1$ and $P_2$)
- Loop $L_2 = G_3 H_2$ (touches $P_1$ only)
- Loop $L_3 = -G_4 H_2$ (touches $P_2$ only)
- $L_1$ and $L_2$ are non-touching with each other. $L_1$ and $L_3$ are non-touching with each other. $L_2$ and $L_3$ touch.

Apply Mason's Gain Formula to find $T = C/R$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) State-Space Augmentation**

Since $\mathbf{D}_1 = \mathbf{D}_2 = 0$ (strictly proper), the augmented matrices are:

$$
\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A}_1 & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{C}_1 & \mathbf{A}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -2 & -3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & -5 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{C}_1$:

$$
\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{C}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore the $(3,4)$-block of $\mathcal{A}$ becomes:

$$
\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -2 & -3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -5 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B}_1 \\ \mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{D}_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{D}_2\mathbf{C}_1 & \mathbf{C}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathcal{D} = \mathbf{D}_2\mathbf{D}_1 = 0
$$

(7 Marks)

**(b) Mason's Gain Formula**

Step 1 — Compute the graph determinant $\Delta$:

$$
\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) + (L_1 L_2 + L_1 L_3) - (L_1 L_2 L_3) + \cdots
$$

Note $L_2 L_3$ is excluded (they touch). $L_1 L_2 L_3$ is excluded (the triple would require all three to be mutually non-touching, but $L_2$ and $L_3$ touch). So:

$$
\Delta = 1 - (-G_2H_1 + G_3H_2 - G_4H_2) + (G_2H_1)(G_3H_2) + (G_2H_1)(G_4H_2)
$$

$$
\Delta = 1 + G_2H_1 - G_3H_2 + G_4H_2 + G_2G_3H_1H_2 + G_2G_4H_1H_2
$$

Step 2 — Cofactor $\Delta_1$ for $P_1 = G_1G_2G_3$:

Path $P_1$ touches $L_1$ and $L_2$. It does **not** touch $L_3$ (since $L_3$ contains $G_4$, which is not in $P_1$).

$$
\Delta_1 = 1 - L_3 = 1 + G_4 H_2
$$

Step 3 — Cofactor $\Delta_2$ for $P_2 = -G_1G_4$:

Path $P_2$ touches $L_1$ and $L_3$. It does **not** touch $L_2$ (since $L_2$ contains $G_3$, not in $P_2$).

$$
\Delta_2 = 1 - L_2 = 1 - G_3H_2
$$

Step 4 — Apply Mason's formula:

$$
\boxed{T = \dfrac{P_1\Delta_1 + P_2\Delta_2}{\Delta} = \dfrac{G_1G_2G_3(1+G_4H_2) - G_1G_4(1 - G_3H_2)}{1 + G_2H_1 - G_3H_2 + G_4H_2 + G_2G_3H_1H_2 + G_2G_4H_1H_2}}
$$

(7 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Reversed cascade order (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often write $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{G}_1\mathbf{G}_2$ instead of $\mathbf{G}_2\mathbf{G}_1$ for a cascade where $\mathbf{G}_1$ is upstream. The downstream matrix multiplies from the **left**.
> 2. **Forgetting the matrix inverse in feedback (–3 marks):** For SISO, the formula is $G/(1+GH)$. For MIMO, it is $(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$. The scalar shortcut is **not** valid for matrix expressions.
> 3. **Wrong cofactor sign in Mason's formula (–2 marks):** The sign of $\Delta_1, \Delta_2$ depends on the *number* of non-touching loops removed. Use the alternation rule carefully, including the sign convention for *negative* feedback loops.
> 4. **Dimensional mismatch in parallel connection (–2 marks):** A frequent mistake is summing two TFMs of different dimensions. Always check $q_1 = q_2$ and $p_1 = p_2$ before adding.
> 5. **Conflating SISO and MIMO characteristic equations (–2 marks):** For SISO, the characteristic equation is $1+GH = 0$. For MIMO, it is $\det(\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}) = 0$, which is a polynomial of degree $\sum_i n_i$ (sum of subsystem orders).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Systems and Subsystems: Interconnection**

- **Cascade TFM:** $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{G}_2\mathbf{G}_1$ (rightmost first; matrix multiplication is non-commutative).
- **Parallel TFM:** $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2$ (requires identical TFM dimensions).
- **Negative Feedback CLTFM:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- **Positive Feedback CLTFM:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- **Characteristic Equation (MIMO):** $\det(\mathbf{I} \pm \mathbf{GH}) = 0$.
- **Unity Feedback Special Case:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- **Mason's Gain Formula:** $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\sum_k P_k \Delta_k$; cofactors set touching loops to zero.
- **Cascade State-Space:** $\mathcal{A}$ is block lower-triangular; $\mathcal{B}$ contains $\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{D}_1$ in lower block.
- **Parallel State-Space:** $\mathcal{A}$ is block-diagonal; $\mathcal{C}$ is the row concatenation of $\mathbf{C}_1, \mathbf{C}_2$.
- **Feedback State-Space:** $\mathcal{A}$ is full block-matrix with cross-coupling terms $-\mathbf{B}_G\mathbf{C}_H$ and $\mathbf{B}_H\mathbf{C}_G$.
- **Dimensional Check:** Always verify that inner dimensions of multiplied TFMs match.
- **Inverse Existence:** $\det(\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}) \neq 0$ is a prerequisite for the feedback CLTFM to exist.
- **Mason's Pitfall:** Two loops are non-touching **iff** they share **no common node**, not just no common branch.
- **Engineer's Mantra:** *Right-to-left multiplication, left-to-right signal flow.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Series

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Multivariable Control Systems**, a **Series (Cascade) Connection** refers to the interconnection of two or more Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) subsystems in such a way that the **output vector** of one subsystem is fed as the **input vector** to the next subsystem. 

For two linear time-invariant (LTI) multivariable subsystems characterized by their Transfer Function Matrices (TFM), $\mathbf{G_1(s)}$ and $\mathbf{G_2(s)}$, the resulting series-combined system's overall TFM is given by the **matrix product**:

$$\mathbf{G(s)} = \mathbf{G_2(s)} \cdot \mathbf{G_1(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The order of multiplication is **strictly reverse** to the signal flow direction. If $\mathbf{u} \rightarrow \mathbf{G_1} \rightarrow \mathbf{G_2} \rightarrow \mathbf{y}$, then $\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{G_1(s)}\mathbf{u}(s)$. This is because matrix multiplication is generally **non-commutative** ($\mathbf{G_1 G_2} \neq \mathbf{G_2 G_1}$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water purification plant** with three sequential stages:
- **Stage 1 (G₁):** Sand filter (takes raw water, gives partially filtered water)
- **Stage 2 (G₂):** Carbon filter (takes filtered water, gives cleaner water)
- **Stage 3 (G₃):** UV sterilizer (takes cleaner water, gives pure water)

The water flows from one stage to the next in a pipeline. The total purification effect is the **sequential composition** of each stage's effect. In multivariable control, if each stage has multiple inputs and outputs (e.g., removing different contaminants), the overall transformation is the **matrix product** of individual stage transformations. The **order matters** — sand filter MUST come before carbon filter; reversing them ruins the system.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The dimensions (number of inputs/outputs) of subsystems must match at the connection point.
- For $\mathbf{G_1(s)}$ of dimension $(p \times m)$ and $\mathbf{G_2(s)}$ of dimension $(q \times p)$, the resulting series system has dimension $(q \times m)$.
- **Key Metric:** *Internal dimension matching* — the number of outputs of $\mathbf{G_1}$ must equal the number of inputs of $\mathbf{G_2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why series connections matter in industry:** Most modern chemical plants, aerospace autopilots, and robotic manipulators use **cascade control structures** where inner loops (fast) and outer loops (slow) are arranged in series for improved disturbance rejection and stability margins.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series Connection — Structural Breakdown

Consider two MIMO LTI subsystems $S_1$ and $S_2$ defined as:
- **Subsystem $S_1$:** $\mathbf{y_1}(s) = \mathbf{G_1(s)}\mathbf{u_1}(s)$ where $\mathbf{G_1(s)}$ is $(p \times m)$
- **Subsystem $S_2$:** $\mathbf{y_2}(s) = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{u_2}(s)$ where $\mathbf{G_2(s)}$ is $(q \times p)$

The **series connection** is constructed by tying $\mathbf{u_2} = \mathbf{y_1}$:

$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{y_2}(s) = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{u_2}(s) = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{y_1}(s) = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{G_1(s)}\mathbf{u}(s)$$

#### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. **Identify Signal Flow Direction:** Output of $S_1$ becomes input to $S_2$.
2. **Verify Dimension Compatibility:** $\dim(\mathbf{u_1}) = m$, $\dim(\mathbf{y_1}) = p$, $\dim(\mathbf{y_2}) = q$.
3. **Compute Composite TFM:** Right-multiply $\mathbf{G_1}$ by $\mathbf{G_2}$ on the left.
4. **State-Space Formulation:** Build augmented state-space model from individual ones.

### 2.2 State-Space Realization of Series Connection

Given two subsystems in state-space form:
- $S_1$: $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_1 = \mathbf{A_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{B_1}\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y_1} = \mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u}$
- $S_2$: $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 = \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2}\mathbf{u_2}$, $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2}\mathbf{u_2}$

With $\mathbf{u_2} = \mathbf{y_1}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_1 &= \mathbf{A_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{B_1}\mathbf{u} \\
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 &= \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2}(\mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u}) \\
\mathbf{y} &= \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2}(\mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u})
\end{aligned}$$

Augmented state vector $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x_1} \\ \mathbf{x_2} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}} &= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{u} \\
\mathbf{y} &= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{D_2 C_1} & \mathbf{C_2} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{D_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Dimensions / Conditions |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Series TFM | $\mathbf{G(s)} = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{G_1(s)}$ | $(q \times m)$ matrix |
| 2 | State Augmentation | $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{x_1}^T \; \mathbf{x_2}^T]^T$ | Dimension: $n_1 + n_2$ |
| 3 | Composite A-matrix | $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix}$ | Block lower triangular |
| 4 | Composite B-matrix | $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix}$ | $(n_1 + n_2) \times m$ |
| 5 | Composite C-matrix | $\mathbf{C} = [\mathbf{D_2 C_1} \; \mathbf{C_2}]$ | $q \times (n_1 + n_2)$ |
| 6 | Composite D-matrix | $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{D_2 D_1}$ | $q \times m$ |
| 7 | Eigenvalue Property | $\sigma(\mathbf{A}) = \sigma(\mathbf{A_1}) \cup \sigma(\mathbf{A_2})$ | Block triangular ⇒ diagonal eigenvalues |
| 8 | Non-commutativity | $\mathbf{G_2 G_1} \neq \mathbf{G_1 G_2}$ in general | KTU high-yield trap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In aerospace, the **Inner-Loop / Outer-Loop** design pattern uses series connection: the inner loop (rate damper) is followed by the outer loop (attitude controller). The eigenvalues of the composite system are the **union of individual eigenvalues**, which simplifies stability analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Series Composition

**Given:** Two LTI MIMO subsystems in controllable canonical state-space form.

$$
S_1: \quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}_1 = \mathbf{A_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{B_1}\mathbf{u}, \quad \mathbf{y_1} = \mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x_1} + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u}
$$

$$
S_2: \quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 = \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2}\mathbf{u}_2, \quad \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2}\mathbf{u}_2
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Series Constraint:**

The defining constraint of series connection is that the output of $S_1$ becomes the input of $S_2$:

$$
\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{y}_1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $S_2$'s Equations:**

Insert $\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{y}_1 = \mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x_1} + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u}$ into $S_2$'s state and output equations:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 = \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2}(\mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x_1} + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u})
$$

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2}(\mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x_1} + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u})
$$

**Step 3 — Expand Both Equations:**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 = \mathbf{B_2 C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2 D_1}\mathbf{u}
$$

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{D_2 C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2 D_1}\mathbf{u}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Composite System with $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{x_1}; \mathbf{x_2}]$:**

Stacking $\mathbf{x_1}$ and $\mathbf{x_2}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{x}}_1 \\ \dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_1 \\ \mathbf{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}
$$

$$
\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{D_2 C_1} & \mathbf{C_2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_1 \\ \mathbf{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} + [\mathbf{D_2 D_1}] \mathbf{u}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify TFM Equivalence:**

Compute $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}$:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{D_2 C_1} & \mathbf{C_2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s\mathbf{I}_1 - \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ -\mathbf{B_2 C_1} & s\mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{D_2 D_1}
$$

Using block-matrix inversion on lower triangular form:

$$
(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} (s\mathbf{I_1} - \mathbf{A_1})^{-1} & \mathbf{0} \\ (s\mathbf{I_2} - \mathbf{A_2})^{-1}\mathbf{B_2 C_1}(s\mathbf{I_1} - \mathbf{A_1})^{-1} & (s\mathbf{I_2} - \mathbf{A_2})^{-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying through and simplifying (algebraic steps shown in detail below):

$$
\mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{D_2 C_1}(s\mathbf{I_1} - \mathbf{A_1})^{-1}\mathbf{B_1} + \mathbf{C_2}(s\mathbf{I_2} - \mathbf{A_2})^{-1}\mathbf{B_2 D_1}\mathbf{C_1}(s\mathbf{I_1} - \mathbf{A_1})^{-1}\mathbf{B_1} + \mathbf{C_2}(s\mathbf{I_2} - \mathbf{A_2})^{-1}\mathbf{B_2 D_1}
$$

Regrouping with $\mathbf{G_i}(s) = \mathbf{C_i}(s\mathbf{I_i} - \mathbf{A_i})^{-1}\mathbf{B_i} + \mathbf{D_i}$:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = [\mathbf{C_2}(s\mathbf{I_2} - \mathbf{A_2})^{-1}\mathbf{B_2} + \mathbf{D_2}][\mathbf{C_1}(s\mathbf{I_1} - \mathbf{A_1})^{-1}\mathbf{B_1} + \mathbf{D_1}] = \mathbf{G_2}(s)\mathbf{G_1}(s)
$$

This **proves** the equivalence of the state-space series formulation with the TFM formulation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given:

$$
S_1: \mathbf{A_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{B_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{C_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{D_1} = 0
$$

$$
S_2: \mathbf{A_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{B_2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{C_2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{D_2} = 0
$$

**Find:** The composite state-space model for series connection $S = S_2 \circ S_1$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute composite A-matrix (block lower triangular):

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -2 & -3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 — Compute composite B-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Compute composite C-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 — Compute composite D-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{D} = 0
$$

The overall TFM is:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{G_2}(s)\mathbf{G_1}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Series Connection in MIMO Systems

```mermaid
graph LR
    U(["Input Vector u"]) --> SYS1["Subsystem S1 G1(s)"]
    SYS1 --> Y1(["Intermediate Output y1"])
    Y1 --> SYS2["Subsystem S2 G2(s)"]
    SYS2 --> Y(["Output Vector y"])
    SYS1 -. State x1 .-> STORE1["State Vector x1"]
    SYS2 -. State x2 .-> STORE2["State Vector x2"]
    STORE1 --> COMPOSITE["Composite System G(s) = G2(s)G1(s)"]
    STORE2 --> COMPOSITE
```

### 4.2 Augmented State-Space Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP(["u"]) --> B1["B1"]
    INP --> BD["B2 D1"]
    B1 --> X1DOT["x1 dot"]
    BD --> X2DOT["x2 dot"]
    C1PATH["C1"] --> BD
    X1["State x1"] --> A1["A1"]
    X2["State x2"] --> A2["A2"]
    A1 --> X1DOT
    C1PATH --> X2DOT
    A2 --> X2DOT
    X1 --> DC1["D2 C1"]
    X2 --> C2PATH["C2"]
    DC1 --> SUM["Sum Output"]
    C2PATH --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT(["y"])
```

### 4.3 Eigenvalue Preservation Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1["Eigenvalues of A1: lambda1, lambda2"] --> UNION["Union of Eigenvalues"]
    E2["Eigenvalues of A2: lambda3"] --> UNION
    UNION --> STABLE["Composite System Stability Check"]
    STABLE --> RESULT{{"All Re lambda < 0 ?"}}
```

### 4.4 Series vs Parallel Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    MIMO["MIMO Interconnections"] --> SERIES["Series Cascade: G = G2 G1"]
    MIMO --> PARALLEL["Parallel: G = G1 + G2"]
    MIMO --> FEEDBACK["Feedback: G = G1 (I + G2 G1) power -1"]
    SERIES --> SBLOCK["Block lower triangular A"]
    PARALLEL --> PBLOCK["Block diagonal A"]
    FEEDBACK --> FBLOCK["Closed-loop structure"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define series connection of two multivariable systems. Why is matrix multiplication order significant in series connection?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Series connection: Output of subsystem $S_1$ is fed as input to subsystem $S_2$.
- **(1 Mark)** Resulting TFM: $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{G_2}(s)\mathbf{G_1}(s)$.
- **(1 Mark)** Order matters because matrix multiplication is **non-commutative**: $\mathbf{G_2 G_1} \neq \mathbf{G_1 G_2}$ in general, leading to different system dynamics and possibly instability if order is reversed.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the form of the composite A-matrix when two multivariable systems are connected in series?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Composite state vector: $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{x_1}^T \; \mathbf{x_2}^T]^T$.
- **(1 Mark)** Composite A-matrix is block lower triangular: $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix}$.
- **(1 Mark)** Its eigenvalues are the union of eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A_1}$ and $\mathbf{A_2}$: $\sigma(\mathbf{A}) = \sigma(\mathbf{A_1}) \cup \sigma(\mathbf{A_2})$.

---

### Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the state-space representation of the overall system obtained by connecting two LTI multivariable subsystems in series. Clearly state the composite matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $\mathbf{D}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given two subsystems in series:

$$
S_1: \mathbf{A_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{B_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{C_1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{D_1} = 0
$$

$$
S_2: \mathbf{A_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{B_2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{C_2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{D_2} = 0
$$

Determine the composite state-space model and verify the composite TFM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from two subsystems (2 marks):

$$
S_1: \dot{\mathbf{x}}_1 = \mathbf{A_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{B_1}\mathbf{u}, \quad \mathbf{y_1} = \mathbf{C_1}\mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{D_1}\mathbf{u}
$$

$$
S_2: \dot{\mathbf{x}}_2 = \mathbf{A_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{B_2}\mathbf{u}_2, \quad \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C_2}\mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{D_2}\mathbf{u}_2
$$

Apply the series constraint $\mathbf{u_2} = \mathbf{y_1}$ (1 mark). Substitute and form the augmented system with $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{x_1}; \mathbf{x_2}]$ (2 marks):

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = [\mathbf{D_2 C_1} \; \mathbf{C_2}], \quad \mathbf{D} = \mathbf{D_2 D_1}
$$

Verify that $\mathbf{G}(s) = \mathbf{G_2}(s)\mathbf{G_1}(s)$ using block-matrix inversion (2 marks). $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) — Computation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute composite A-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -6 & -5 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute composite B-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute composite C-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute composite D-matrix:

$$
\mathbf{D} = 0
$$

Step 5 (1 mark) — Verification: TFM equals $\mathbf{G_2}(s)\mathbf{G_1}(s)$ confirmed via block-triangular structure.

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the significance of dimension compatibility in series connection of MIMO systems. What are the necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of a valid series connection? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two SISO systems have TFMs $G_1(s) = \frac{2}{s+1}$ and $G_2(s) = \frac{3}{s+2}$. Compute (i) the series connection TFM, and (ii) the poles and zeros of the composite system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 Marks):**

(2 marks) **Definition of dimension compatibility:** For series connection, the number of outputs of $S_1$ must equal the number of inputs of $S_2$.

(2 marks) **Necessary and sufficient conditions:** 
- $\mathbf{G_1}(s)$ has dimensions $(p \times m)$
- $\mathbf{G_2}(s)$ has dimensions $(q \times p)$ — inner dimensions must match
- Subsystems must be physically realizable and stable (or controllability/observability intact)

(3 marks) **Significance:** If dimension incompatibility occurs, the matrix product $\mathbf{G_2 G_1}$ is undefined; in physical systems this corresponds to incompatible signal types (e.g., voltage to force without a transducer). The series connection preserves the **input-output mapping** only when these dimensions match. Otherwise, a transformation matrix (e.g., interface block) is required.

**Part (b) — Computation (7 Marks):**

(i) (3 marks) Series TFM:

$$
G(s) = G_2(s) \cdot G_1(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} \cdot \frac{3}{s+2} = \frac{6}{(s+1)(s+2)}
$$

(ii) (4 marks) Poles and zeros:
- **Poles:** $s = -1$ and $s = -2$
- **Zeros:** None (no finite zeros)
- **Stability:** Both poles have negative real parts → **BIBO stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order Reversal Trap (Major!):** Students often write $\mathbf{G_1 G_2}$ instead of $\mathbf{G_2 G_1}$ in series connections. The output is $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{G_2 G_1 u}$, NOT $\mathbf{G_1 G_2 u}$. **Lose 2 marks instantly** for this error.
> 2. **Dimension Mismatch:** When the problem gives subsystems of mismatched inner dimensions, students forget to check compatibility. Always verify $\dim(\text{output of } S_1) = \dim(\text{input of } S_2)$.
> 3. **Block Matrix Sign Error:** The composite A-matrix has $\mathbf{B_2 C_1}$ in the lower-left block, not $\mathbf{C_2 B_1}$ or any other permutation.
> 4. **Eigenvalue Confusion:** Some students compute eigenvalues of the full $\mathbf{A}$ matrix using $4 \times 4$ determinants, missing the shortcut that block triangular matrices have eigenvalues that are simply the union of diagonal block eigenvalues.
> 5. **D-matrix Error:** The composite $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{D_2 D_1}$, not $\mathbf{D_1 D_2}$ (though for scalars this commutes, in matrices it generally does not).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series Connection Definition:** Output of $S_1$ → Input of $S_2$. The composite TFM is $\mathbf{G(s)} = \mathbf{G_2(s)}\mathbf{G_1(s)}$ (reverse order to signal flow).
- **Order Matters:** Matrix multiplication is non-commutative. Reversing connection order gives a different (and possibly unstable) system.
- **Composite State-Space Form:**
  - $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A_1} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{B_2 C_1} & \mathbf{A_2} \end{bmatrix}$ — block lower triangular
  - $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B_1} \\ \mathbf{B_2 D_1} \end{bmatrix}$
  - $\mathbf{C} = [\mathbf{D_2 C_1} \; \mathbf{C_2}]$
  - $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{D_2 D_1}$
- **Eigenvalue Property:** $\sigma(\mathbf{A}) = \sigma(\mathbf{A_1}) \cup \sigma(\mathbf{A_2})$ — composite stability requires both subsystems to be stable.
- **Dimension Rule:** Inner dimensions must match: $(q \times p) \cdot (p \times m) = (q \times m)$.
- **Composite TFM Equivalence:** Verified via block matrix inversion of $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$.
- **Industrial Applications:** Cascade control (inner-outer loops), multi-stage filtering, robotic manipulator chains, distillation column control trains.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the block diagram first; it makes dimension checking and order-of-multiplication errors obvious. State the series constraint $\mathbf{u_2} = \mathbf{y_1}$ explicitly in your solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel and Feedback Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parallel and Feedback Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parallel Connection (MIMO Systems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **parallel connection** of two (or more) linear time-invariant (LTI) multivariable systems $\mathbf{G}_1(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s)$ is formed by applying the **same input vector** $\mathbf{u}(s)$ to both systems simultaneously and **summing their output vectors** to obtain the resultant output $\mathbf{y}(s)$.

The resultant transfer function matrix (TFM) of the parallel combination is:

$$\mathbf{G}_p(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s)$$

### 🌊 Intuitive Analogy — "The Twin Engineers"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Two Engineers Solving the Same Task**
> Imagine two engineers (Engineer-A and Engineer-B) working in **parallel** in the same office. They are handed the **identical set of instructions** (the input $\mathbf{u}$). Engineer-A produces an output vector $\mathbf{y}_1 = \mathbf{G}_1\mathbf{u}$ and Engineer-B produces $\mathbf{y}_2 = \mathbf{G}_2\mathbf{u}$. The supervisor at the desk simply **adds up** both their reports and hands the combined final report to the client. The overall "transfer behaviour" of the office is therefore $\mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2$ — the *parallel connection*.

### Properties of Parallel Connection
- The dimensions of $\mathbf{G}_1(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s)$ **must match exactly** for addition to be defined.
- Parallel connection is **commutative**: $\mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2 = \mathbf{G}_2 + \mathbf{G}_1$.
- Parallel connection is **associative**: $(\mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2) + \mathbf{G}_3 = \mathbf{G}_1 + (\mathbf{G}_2 + \mathbf{G}_3)$.
- The combined system inherits stability if the individual TFMs are stable.

---

## 1.2 Feedback Connection (MIMO Systems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **feedback connection** of two multivariable systems $\mathbf{G}(s)$ (forward path) and $\mathbf{H}(s)$ (feedback path) is formed by feeding a portion of the output $\mathbf{y}(s)$ back to the input through $\mathbf{H}(s)$, and comparing it with the reference $\mathbf{r}(s)$ via a summing junction to form the actuating error signal $\mathbf{e}(s)$.

For **negative feedback** (the standard control configuration):

$$\mathbf{e}(s) = \mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{H}(s)\,\mathbf{y}(s)$$

The closed-loop transfer function matrix is:

$$\mathbf{T}(s) = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-Posedness Condition** (KTU Favourite!)
> The feedback connection is said to be **well-defined** (or *well-posed*) if and only if:
> $$\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right] \neq 0 \quad \forall s \text{ in the region of interest.}$$
> If the determinant vanishes, the closed-loop system becomes singular — the controller output becomes unbounded (algebraic loop in the continuous-time case, or division by zero).

### 🔄 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cruise Control"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Car's Cruise Control**
> In a car, the *driver* (or cruise controller) sets a desired speed $r$. The *car engine and gearbox* form the forward path $\mathbf{G}$. A *speed sensor* on the wheel acts as the feedback path $\mathbf{H}$, which continuously reports the actual speed $y$ back to the controller. The error $e = r - Hy$ represents "how far off" the car is from the desired speed. The engine then **amplifies this small error** to drive the car towards the target. This is the essence of *negative feedback* — it forces the output to *track* the reference automatically.

### Why Feedback? (Engineering Utility)
- **Disturbance Rejection:** Cancels external disturbances acting on the plant.
- **Set-Point Tracking:** Forces the output to follow a changing reference.
- **Sensitivity Reduction:** Decreases the sensitivity of the closed-loop system to plant parameter variations.
- **Bandwidth & Transient Shaping:** Modifies the natural dynamics of the plant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parallel Connection — Step-by-Step Logic

**Operational Breakdown:**
1. The reference input vector $\mathbf{u}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is **broadcast** simultaneously to both $\mathbf{G}_1(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s)$.
2. Each subsystem produces an output: $\mathbf{y}_1 = \mathbf{G}_1 \mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{y}_2 = \mathbf{G}_2 \mathbf{u}$.
3. The summer (typically an adder block) **sums the outputs** element-wise: $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}_1 + \mathbf{y}_2$.
4. Substituting: $\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2)\mathbf{u} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{G}_p = \mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2$.

**Why does matrix addition work for MIMO?**
- Because both $\mathbf{G}_1$ and $\mathbf{G}_2$ act on the **same** $\mathbf{u}$, distributivity of matrix multiplication over matrix addition allows the sum to be factored out.

## 2.2 Feedback Connection — Step-by-Step Logic

**Operational Breakdown (Negative Feedback):**
1. Error signal: $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$.
2. Forward path: $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{G}\mathbf{e}$.
3. Substitute (1) into (2): $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{Gr} - \mathbf{GHy}$.
4. Collect $\mathbf{y}$ terms: $\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{GHy} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{Gr}$.
5. Pre-multiply by $(\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})^{-1}$: $\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{Gr}$.
6. Therefore $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.

**Why is the *negative* sign critical?**
- Negative feedback ensures that when $\mathbf{y}$ rises above $\mathbf{r}$, the error $\mathbf{e}$ shrinks, **counteracting** the deviation. This is the basis of stability in negative feedback systems (Nyquist/Bode stability theorems).

**Key Subtleties for MIMO:**
- The product $\mathbf{GH}$ is a **square matrix product** of size $p \times p$ (where $p$ is the number of outputs).
- The matrix $\mathbf{GH}$ is **non-commutative** in general: $\mathbf{GH} \neq \mathbf{HG}$.
- The closed-loop poles are the **roots of** $\det[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}] = 0$.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Configuration** | **Closed-Loop / Resultant TFM** | **Key Condition** | **Pole Equation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel | $\mathbf{G}_p = \mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2$ | Compatible dimensions $(p \times m)$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2\right] = 0$ |
| Negative Feedback (Forward $\mathbf{G}$, Feedback $\mathbf{H}$) | $\mathbf{T} = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}\right] \neq 0$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}\right] = 0$ |
| Positive Feedback | $\mathbf{T} = \left[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{GH}\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{GH}\right] \neq 0$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{GH}\right] = 0$ |
| Unity Feedback ($\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$) | $\mathbf{T} = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}\right] \neq 0$ | $\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}\right] = 0$ |
| Sensitivity Function | $\mathbf{S} = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}\right]^{-1}$ | Used in robustness analysis | — |
| Complementary Sensitivity | $\mathbf{T} = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}\right]^{-1}\mathbf{GH}$ | $\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}$ (identity) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Identity Recall:** For the SISO case, $\mathbf{G}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are scalars and the inverse becomes a simple reciprocal:
> $$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$
> This is the classic Mason's-rule result you studied in undergraduate control theory — the MIMO formula is its matrix generalization.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Configurations

- **Parallel:** Used to implement *redundant control channels*, *additive actuators*, or to *combine observers* (e.g., Luenberger observer + Kalman filter).
- **Feedback:** The backbone of **every industrial controller** — PID controllers, model-reference adaptive control, internal model control (IMC), and $\mathbf{H}_\infty$ robust control all rely on feedback interconnection of TFMs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parallel Connection

### Setup
Let $\mathbf{G}_1(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ be two TFM blocks connected in parallel.

### Derivation
By the definition of a transfer function matrix:
$$\mathbf{y}_1(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$
$$\mathbf{y}_2(s) = \mathbf{G}_2(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$

The total output is the sum of individual outputs:
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{y}_1(s) + \mathbf{y}_2(s)$$

Substituting the two relations:
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s)\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$

Using the distributive property of matrix multiplication over addition:
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \left[\mathbf{G}_1(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s)\right]\,\mathbf{u}(s)$$

Therefore the overall TFM is:
$$\mathbf{G}_p(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Negative Feedback Connection

### Setup
Let $\mathbf{G}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}(s)$ be the plant (forward path) and $\mathbf{H}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}(s)$ be the sensor (feedback path). Let $\mathbf{r}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ be the reference input.

### Step 1 — Define the error signal at the summing junction
For a *negative* feedback connection:
$$\mathbf{e}(s) = \mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{H}(s)\,\mathbf{y}(s)$$

### Step 2 — Define the forward path input-output relation
The output of the plant is driven by the error signal:
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{e}(s)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\left[\mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{H}(s)\,\mathbf{y}(s)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Distribute
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\,\mathbf{y}(s)$$

### Step 5 — Collect all $\mathbf{y}$ terms on the LHS
$$\mathbf{y}(s) + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\,\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{r}(s)$$

Factor out $\mathbf{y}$ (factoring is valid because $\mathbf{I}$ is the identity matrix):
$$\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right]\,\mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{r}(s)$$

### Step 6 — Pre-multiply both sides by $\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right]^{-1}$
Provided the inverse exists (i.e., the system is well-posed):
$$\mathbf{y}(s) = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)\,\mathbf{r}(s)$$

### Step 7 — Identify the closed-loop TFM
$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{T}(s) = \left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right]^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)\;} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (MIMO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Given two $2 \times 2$ TFMs, find the resultant TFM for both parallel and feedback connections.

**Given:**
$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{H}(s) = \mathbf{I}_{2 \times 2}$$

**Task 1 — Unity Negative Feedback:**

Compute $\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH} = \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}$:
$$\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & 1 + \dfrac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{s+3}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the determinant:
$$\det(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G}) = \dfrac{(s+2)(s+3)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{s+3}{s+1}$$

Compute the inverse (using the $2 \times 2$ formula):
$$(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})^{-1} = \dfrac{s+1}{s+3} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+3}{s+2} & 0 \\[4pt] -1 & \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+1}{s+2} & 0 \\[4pt] -\dfrac{s+1}{s+3} & \dfrac{s+2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $\mathbf{G}$:
$$\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})^{-1}\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+1}{s+2} & 0 \\[4pt] -\dfrac{s+1}{s+3} & \dfrac{s+2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing term by term:
- $T_{11} = \dfrac{s+1}{s+2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s+1} = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- $T_{12} = 0$
- $T_{21} = -\dfrac{s+1}{s+3}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{s+2}{s+3}\cdot 1 = \dfrac{-(1) + (s+2)}{s+3} = \dfrac{s+1}{s+3}$
- $T_{22} = \dfrac{s+2}{s+3}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+2} = \dfrac{1}{s+3}$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{T}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+2} & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{s+1}{s+3} & \dfrac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice that the *cross-coupling* ($T_{21} \neq 0$) persists in the closed loop — a hallmark of multivariable systems! In SISO, feedback *fully* decouples the dynamics, but in MIMO, the off-diagonal terms survive unless the plant is *diagonally dominant* or a *decoupler* is inserted.

**Task 2 — Parallel Connection with another TFM $\mathbf{G}_2$:**
If $\mathbf{G}_2(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dfrac{1}{s} \\[4pt] 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, then:
$$\mathbf{G}_p = \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{G}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s} \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Parallel Connection

```mermaid
graph LR
    u((u)) --> G1[Block G1 s]
    u --> G2[Block G2 s]
    G1 --> sumA((Sum +))
    G2 --> sumA
    sumA --> y((y))

    classDef io fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef block fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sum fill:#fdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class u,y io
    class G1,G2 block
    class sumA sum
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Negative Feedback Connection

```mermaid
graph LR
    r((r)) --> sumE((Sum +/-))
    sumE -->|e| Gfwd[Forward G s]
    Gfwd --> y((y))
    y --> Hfb[Feedback H s]
    Hfb --> sumE

    classDef io fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef block fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sum fill:#fdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class r,y io
    class Gfwd,Hfb block
    class sumE sum
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Closed-Loop MIMO

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reference Vector r] --> SM[(Summing Junction e = r - Hy)]
    SM --> EP[Error Processor Controller]
    EP --> AC[Actuator Plant G s]
    AC --> OUT[Output Vector y]
    OUT --> SN[Sensor H s]
    SN --> SM
    OUT --> FB[Disturbance Rejection Loop]
    FB --> AC

    classDef sig fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fb fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    class R,OUT,SM sig
    class EP,AC,SN proc
    class FB fb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **forward path** (top) carries the actuating signal from error $\mathbf{e}$ through the plant $\mathbf{G}$ to the output $\mathbf{y}$.
> - The **feedback path** (right side) carries $\mathbf{y}$ through the sensor $\mathbf{H}$ back to the summer.
> - The summer subtracts the feedback from the reference to produce the *new* error — this is the **closed-loop cycle**.
> - The bottom branch represents the **disturbance rejection** mechanism — a defining advantage of feedback control.

## 4.4 Why Mermaid (Not Physical Drawings)?

For control-system block diagrams, Mermaid is preferred because:
- The mathematical "shape" of the diagram is what matters (signal flow), not the physical aesthetics.
- Mermaid's `graph LR` syntax with summed junctions clearly shows the algebraic summation $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{Hy}$, which mirrors the equation directly.
- It scales gracefully for **large MIMO systems** with $10+$ blocks without becoming cluttered.

---
