<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the conditions under which the feedback connection of two multivariable systems is well-defined.**

**Model Answer:**

> The feedback connection of $\mathbf{G}(s)$ and $\mathbf{H}(s)$ is said to be *well-defined* (or *well-posed*) if the closed-loop system admits a unique solution for the output $\mathbf{y}(s)$ for every bounded reference $\mathbf{r}(s)$. Mathematically, this requires:
>
> $$\det\!\left[\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)\right] \neq 0 \quad \text{over the region of interest}$$
>
> Equivalently, the loop transfer function matrix $\mathbf{L}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{H}(s)$ must **not** cause any pole-zero cancellation that drives the determinant to zero. If violated, the closed-loop poles include values of $s$ for which $(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})$ is singular, making the inverse undefined and the system un-implementable in continuous time.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Two MIMO systems with transfer function matrices $\mathbf{G}_1$ and $\mathbf{G}_2$ are connected in parallel. Write the expression for the overall transfer function matrix and mention any dimensional constraints.**

**Model Answer:**

> The overall transfer function matrix for the parallel connection is:
> $$\mathbf{G}_p(s) = \mathbf{G}_1(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s)$$
>
> **Dimensional constraint:** Both $\mathbf{G}_1(s)$ and $\mathbf{G}_2(s)$ must have the *same dimensions* $p \times m$, where $p$ is the number of outputs and $m$ is the number of inputs. If the dimensions differ, matrix addition is undefined and the parallel connection cannot be realized in a meaningful way.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign error at the summer** (positive vs negative feedback) is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in feedback problems. Always re-check the polarity of $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ at the summing junction.
> - **Order of multiplication in $\mathbf{GH}$:** In MIMO, $\mathbf{GH} \neq \mathbf{HG}$. The product $\mathbf{GH}$ is square only if the number of outputs of $\mathbf{G}$ equals the number of inputs of $\mathbf{H}$.
> - **Inversion assumption:** Writing $(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}$ without verifying $\det \neq 0$ loses 1–2 marks. Always quote the well-posedness condition.
> - **Block-diagonal shortcut misuse:** Students often treat MIMO feedback as $n$ independent SISO loops. This is **valid only if $\mathbf{G}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are simultaneously diagonalisable**, which rarely holds in practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Consider the multivariable system with:

$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function matrix $\mathbf{T}(s)$ for the negative feedback configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Another system $\mathbf{G}_2(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+4} & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{s+5} & \dfrac{1}{s+6} \end{bmatrix}$ is connected in parallel with $\mathbf{G}(s)$. Find the resultant TFM and verify the well-posedness of the original feedback loop. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{GH}$:
$$\mathbf{GH} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH}$:
$$\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & 1 + \dfrac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} & \dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{s+5}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the determinant:
$$\det(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH}) = \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \cdot \dfrac{s+5}{s+3} - 0 = \dfrac{(s+2)(s+5)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$

This is non-zero for all $s$ except $s = -1, -3, -5, -2$, which are the *open-loop pole locations*. The system is **well-posed**.

**Step 4:** Compute the inverse using the $2 \times 2$ matrix inverse formula:
$$(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+5}{s+3} & -\dfrac{2}{s+2} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+1}{s+2} & -\dfrac{2(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+5)} \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{s+3}{s+5} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $\mathbf{G}$ to obtain $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$:

For the $(1,1)$ entry:
$$T_{11} = \dfrac{s+1}{s+2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+1} - \dfrac{2(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+5)}\cdot 0 = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$$

For the $(1,2)$ entry:
$$T_{12} = \dfrac{s+1}{s+2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+2} - \dfrac{2(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+5)}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+3} = \dfrac{s+1}{(s+2)^2} - \dfrac{2(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)}$$

Putting over a common denominator $(s+2)^2(s+5)$:
$$T_{12} = \dfrac{(s+1)(s+5) - 2(s+1)(s+2)}{(s+2)^2(s+5)} = \dfrac{(s+1)\!\left[(s+5) - 2(s+2)\right]}{(s+2)^2(s+5)} = \dfrac{(s+1)(s+1)}{(s+2)^2(s+5)} = \dfrac{(s+1)^2}{(s+2)^2(s+5)}$$

For the $(2,1)$ entry:
$$T_{21} = 0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{s+3}{s+5}\cdot 0 = 0$$

For the $(2,2)$ entry:
$$T_{22} = 0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{s+2} + \dfrac{s+3}{s+5}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s+3} = \dfrac{1}{s+5}$$

**Final Answer (a):**
$$\mathbf{T}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+2} & \dfrac{(s+1)^2}{(s+2)^2(s+5)} \\[10pt] 0 & \dfrac{1}{s+5} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Parallel connection:**
$$\mathbf{G}_p(s) = \mathbf{G}(s) + \mathbf{G}_2(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+4} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{s+5} & \dfrac{1}{s+3} + \dfrac{1}{s+6} \end{bmatrix}$$

Simplifying the diagonal entries:
- $\dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+4} = \dfrac{2s+5}{(s+1)(s+4)}$
- $\dfrac{1}{s+3} + \dfrac{1}{s+6} = \dfrac{2s+9}{(s+3)(s+6)}$

Therefore:
$$\mathbf{G}_p(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{2s+5}{(s+1)(s+4)} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{s+5} & \dfrac{2s+9}{(s+3)(s+6)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification of well-posedness (original feedback):**
From part (a), $\det[\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH}] = \dfrac{(s+2)(s+5)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$. This is non-zero for all $s$ other than the open-loop poles. Hence, the closed-loop is **well-posed**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function matrix for a negative feedback interconnection of $\mathbf{G}(s)$ and $\mathbf{H}(s)$ in a multivariable system. Clearly state the well-posedness condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given:
$$\mathbf{G}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{1}{s} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
Find the closed-loop TFM and comment on the cross-coupling behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram:**

The negative feedback interconnection is:
$$\mathbf{e}(s) = \mathbf{r}(s) - \mathbf{H}(s)\mathbf{y}(s); \quad \mathbf{y}(s) = \mathbf{G}(s)\mathbf{e}(s)$$

**Derivation:**
1. $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{Hy}) = \mathbf{Gr} - \mathbf{GHy}$
2. $\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{GHy} = \mathbf{Gr}$
3. $(\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{Gr}$
4. $\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{Gr}$ *(valid if $(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}$ exists)*

**Closed-loop TFM:**
$$\mathbf{T}(s) = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}(s)$$

**Well-posedness condition:**
$$\det[\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH}] \neq 0$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{GH} = \mathbf{G}$ (since $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$):
$$\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{1}{s} & 0 \\[6pt] 1 & 1 + \dfrac{1}{s} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+1}{s} & 0 \\[6pt] 1 & \dfrac{s+1}{s} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Determinant:
$$\det = \dfrac{s+1}{s} \cdot \dfrac{s+1}{s} = \dfrac{(s+1)^2}{s^2}$$

Non-zero for $s \neq 0$ and $s \neq -1$, so the system is well-posed for $s$ in the right-half plane (region of interest for stability).

**Step 3:** Inverse:
$$(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})^{-1} = \dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2}\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s+1}{s} & 0 \\[6pt] -1 & \dfrac{s+1}{s} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s}{s+1} & 0 \\[6pt] -\dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2} & \dfrac{s}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\mathbf{G}$:
$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{s}{s+1} & 0 \\[6pt] -\dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2} & \dfrac{s}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s} & 0 \\[4pt] 1 & \dfrac{1}{s} \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing entries:
- $T_{11} = \dfrac{s}{s+1}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$
- $T_{12} = 0$
- $T_{21} = -\dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s} + \dfrac{s}{s+1}\cdot 1 = -\dfrac{s}{(s+1)^2} + \dfrac{s(s+1)}{(s+1)^2} = \dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2}$
- $T_{22} = 0 + \dfrac{s}{s+1}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{T}(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2} & \dfrac{1}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Comment on cross-coupling:**
The off-diagonal term $T_{21} = \dfrac{s^2}{(s+1)^2}$ is **non-zero**, confirming persistent cross-coupling. A step change in reference $r_1$ alone will produce a transient response in output $y_2$, even after closing the loop. To eliminate this, a *decoupler* $\mathbf{D}(s)$ must be inserted in series with $\mathbf{G}(s)$ to make the open-loop TFM diagonal — this is the principle behind **Inverse Nyquist Array (INA)** and **Characteristic Locus** design methods covered in higher modules.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Parallel TFM:** $\mathbf{G}_p = \mathbf{G}_1 + \mathbf{G}_2$ (requires same dimensions, commutative, associative).
- ✅ **Negative Feedback TFM:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- ✅ **Positive Feedback TFM:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- ✅ **Unity Feedback TFM:** $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{G})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$.
- ✅ **Well-posedness:** $\det[\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH}] \neq 0$ is **mandatory**; always quote it.
- ✅ **Order of multiplication matters:** $\mathbf{GH} \neq \mathbf{HG}$ in general — keep the correct order.
- ✅ **Closed-loop poles:** Solutions of $\det[\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH}(s)] = 0$.
- ✅ **Cross-coupling persists** in MIMO feedback loops unless a decoupler is added.
- ✅ **Sensitivity $\mathbf{S} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}$** and **Complementary Sensitivity $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{GH}$** satisfy the **fundamental identity** $\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}$ — useful in $\mathbf{H}_\infty$ robust control.
- ✅ **Diagonal-dominant feedback** is the key to *diagonal controller design* (covered in INA method).
- ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Treating MIMO feedback as decoupled SISO loops — only valid if $\mathbf{G}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are both diagonal matrices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU Exam:** In every feedback problem, write the formula $\mathbf{T} = (\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{GH})^{-1}\mathbf{G}$ *first*, then verify the well-posedness condition *before* computing the inverse. This shows the examiner that you understand the conceptual underpinnings, not just mechanical algebra — it is worth **2 marks** even if the subsequent matrix algebra is partially incorrect.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalised Interconnection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Generalised Interconnection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In **Multivariable (MIMO) Control Systems**, the **Generalised Interconnection** refers to the systematic, mathematically rigorous framework for combining two or more dynamical subsystems into a larger composite system through a set of well-defined input–output (signal) paths. Each subsystem is described by a **Transfer Function Matrix (TFM)** $G(s)$ or its equivalent state-space realisation $(A, B, C, D)$, and the interconnection specifies how the output vectors of certain subsystems are routed to become the input vectors of others. For two linear time-invariant (LTI) subsystems $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ with compatible dimensions, the **generalised interconnection** is characterised by the algebraic operation on their TFMs dictated by the chosen topology — most commonly: - **Series (Cascade):** $G(s) = G_2(s) \cdot G_1(s)$ - **Parallel (Summation):** $G(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$ - **Negative Feedback (Closed Loop):** $G(s) = [I + G_1(s)G_2(s)]^{-1} G_1(s)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In the **Multivariable** context, $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ are **matrices**, not scalars. Therefore, the order of multiplication is **non-commutative** in general: $G_1 G_2 \neq G_2 G_1$. Students must verify dimensional compatibility ($p \times m$) before performing any interconnection. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Signal Traffic Junction" Imagine a busy multi-lane roundabout where several highways (subsystems) merge. Each highway carries multiple lanes of traffic (a **vector** of signals), and the roundabout's rules (series, parallel, or feedback) dictate how cars from one highway are funnelled into the next. - **Series Interconnection** = Highway 1 funnels *all* its cars *directly* into Highway 2. The traffic exiting Highway 1 is the exact entry traffic for Highway 2. - **Parallel Interconnection** = Two highways starting at the same point are later combined, and cars from both are added together lane-by-lane. - **Feedback Interconnection** = Cars exiting a downstream section are routed *back upstream* through a return ramp (feedback path) and merged with the incoming traffic — a smart adaptive system that self-corrects. This "signal traffic" analogy captures the essence of a **Generalised Interconnection**: a structured way of routing **vectors** (not just scalars) of signals between subsystems. ### 1.3 Why Generalised Interconnection Matters in MIMO In a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) system, all three standard interconnections commute freely. In MIMO, this is **not true**, and the *physical* interconnection topology determines the *mathematical* operation. The general framework uses the **Linear Fractional Transformation (LFT)** to describe every possible closed-loop topology in one unified formula. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Linear Fractional Transformation (LFT):** Given a partitioned plant $P = \begin{bmatrix} P_{11} & P_{12} \\ P_{21} & P_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ and a controller $K$, the **lower LFT** $F_l(P, K)$ and **upper LFT** $F_u(P, K)$ capture the closed-loop behaviour for virtually *any* standard interconnection. This is the mathematical heart of "Generalised Interconnection" as taught in the KTU 2024 scheme. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Canonical Interconnections #### (a) Series (Cascade) Interconnection The output of $G_1$ becomes the input of $G_2$: $$u \longrightarrow G_1 \longrightarrow G_2 \longrightarrow y$$ $$y(s) = G_2(s) \cdot G_1(s) \cdot u(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{series}(s) = G_2(s) G_1(s)$$ > **Why the reverse order?** Because signal flow is $u \to G_1 \to (\text{intermediate}) \to G_2 \to y$, and matrices multiply **right-to-left** in cascade. #### (b) Parallel Interconnection Both subsystems share the same input; their outputs are added: $$u \longrightarrow \boxed{\begin{array}{c} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{array}} \oplus \longrightarrow y$$ $$G_{parallel}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$$ #### (c) Negative Feedback (Closed-Loop) Interconnection Output $y$ is fed back through $H(s)$ and subtracted from the reference $r$: $$G_{cl}(s) = [I + G(s) H(s)]^{-1} G(s)$$ For **unity feedback** ($H = I$): $$T(s) = [I + G(s)]^{-1} G(s) = [I + G(s)^{-1}]^{-1}$$ ### 2.2 The Generalised Plant Partitioning The cornerstone of KTU Module 2 is partitioning a multi-port plant $P(s)$ into a $2 \times 2$ block structure: $$P(s) = \begin{bmatrix} P_{11}(s) & P_{12}(s) \\ P_{21}(s) & P_{22}(s) \end{bmatrix}$$ where the signals are arranged as: - $w$ → exogenous inputs (references, disturbances) of dimension $m_1$ - $u$ → control inputs of dimension $m_2$ - $z$ → regulated/output-to-be-controlled signals of dimension $p_1$ - $y$ → measured outputs of dimension $p_2$ So: $$\begin{bmatrix} z(s) \\ y(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} P_{11} & P_{12} \\ P_{21} & P_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w(s) \\ u(s) \end{bmatrix}$$ The controller $K$ acts on $y$ to produce $u$: $u = K y$. ### 2.3 The Linear Fractional Transformations (LFT) #### Lower LFT (most common in KTU problems): $$F_l(P, K) = P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21}$$ This represents the **closed-loop transfer function from $w$ to $z$** when $K$ is the lower block. #### Upper LFT (used for sensor/actuator uncertainties): $$F_u(P, \Delta) = P_{22} + P_{21} \Delta (I - P_{11} \Delta)^{-1} P_{12}$$ This represents the effect of a perturbation $\Delta$ in the upper block. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorise it and verify dimensional compatibility for every term. | # | Interconnection Type | Composite TFM | Dimensional Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Series / Cascade | $G = G_2 \cdot G_1$ | Inner dims of $G_1, G_2$ must match | | 2 | Parallel / Sum | $G = G_1 + G_2$ | $G_1, G_2$ must have identical $(p \times m)$ | | 3 | Negative Feedback | $G_{cl} = (I + GH)^{-1} G$ | $GH$ must be square | | 4 | Positive Feedback | $G_{cl} = (I - GH)^{-1} G$ | $GH$ must be square | | 5 | Sensitivity Function | $S = (I + GK)^{-1}$ | $GK$ must be square | | 6 | Complementary Sensitivity | $T = GK(I + GK)^{-1}$ | $GK$ must be square | | 7 | Identity (Identity Law) | $S + T = I$ | Always (MIMO) | | 8 | Lower LFT | $F_l(P,K) = P_{11} + P_{12}K(I - P_{22}K)^{-1}P_{21}$ | $(I - P_{22}K)$ invertible | | 9 | Upper LFT | $F_u(P,\Delta) = P_{22} + P_{21}\Delta(I - P_{11}\Delta)^{-1}P_{12}$ | $(I - P_{11}\Delta)$ invertible | | 10 | Open-Loop Gain | $L = G K$ | Compatible dims | | 11 | Loop Transfer (at break) | $L = K G$ (alternative) | Compatible dims | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Generalised interconnection theory is the **backbone** of: 1. **Aircraft flight control** — where a multi-input aircraft (aileron, elevator, rudder) must be controlled to track attitude, altitude, and heading simultaneously. 2. **Process control in chemical plants** — distillation columns with MIMO interactions between temperature, pressure, and flow. 3. **Robotics** — coupled joint dynamics where each joint's motion affects the others. 4. **Automotive stability control** — combined braking and steering MIMO systems. 5. **$H_\infty$ and $\mu$-synthesis** robust control design — which relies entirely on LFT-based generalised plants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop TFM for Negative Feedback (MIMO) **Given:** Plant $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$, with reference $r(s)$, control $u(s)$, and output $y(s)$. **Step 1: Write the forward path equation.** $$y(s) = G(s) \, u(s)$$ **Step 2: Write the feedback path equation.** The feedback signal is: $$u(s) = r(s) - H(s) \, y(s)$$ **Step 3: Substitute (2) into (1).** $$y(s) = G(s) [r(s) - H(s) y(s)]$$ $$y(s) = G(s) r(s) - G(s) H(s) y(s)$$ **Step 4: Collect $y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.** $$y(s) + G(s) H(s) y(s) = G(s) r(s)$$ $$[I + G(s) H(s)] y(s) = G(s) r(s)$$ **Step 5: Pre-multiply both sides by $[I + GH]^{-1}$ (assuming the inverse exists).** $$y(s) = [I + G(s) H(s)]^{-1} G(s) \, r(s)$$ **Step 6: Identify the closed-loop TFM.** $$\boxed{\,T(s) = [I + G(s) H(s)]^{-1} G(s)\,}$$ > **Dimensional check:** If $G$ is $p \times m$ and $H$ is $m \times p$, then $GH$ is $m \times m$ and $I$ is $m \times m$ — the inverse is well-defined. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity and Complementary Sensitivity **Step 1: Define the Sensitivity function $S$ as the transfer function from disturbance (at output) to output.** For a disturbance $d(s)$ entering at the output, $y = G u + d$, and $u = r - H y$: $$y = G(r - Hy) + d = Gr - GHy + d$$ $$[I + GH] y = Gr + d$$ $$y = (I + GH)^{-1} G r + (I + GH)^{-1} d$$ So the **disturbance-to-output** transfer function is: $$S(s) = (I + GH)^{-1}$$ **Step 2: Define the Complementary Sensitivity $T$.** From the reference $r$ to $y$: $$T(s) = (I + GH)^{-1} G H \cdot (H^{-1}) = \cdots$$ A cleaner derivation: $T$ is the transfer function from $r$ to $y$ when the loop is closed: $$T(s) = G(I + HG)^{-1} = (I + GH)^{-1} G \quad \text{(these are equal when dims match)}$$ **Step 3: Prove the Identity $S + T = I$.** $$S + T = (I + GH)^{-1} + (I + GH)^{-1} GH$$ Factor out $(I + GH)^{-1}$: $$S + T = (I + GH)^{-1} [I + GH] = I \quad \blacksquare$$ This identity is **always** true in the LTI MIMO case (unlike the SISO scalar proof, here we use matrix associativity correctly). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Linear Fractional Transformation (LFT) **Given:** Partitioned plant $P$ and controller $K$: $$\begin{bmatrix} z \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} P_{11} & P_{12} \\ P_{21} & P_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w \\ u \end{bmatrix}, \quad u = K y$$ **Step 1: Substitute $u = K y$ into the second row of the plant equation.** $$y = P_{21} w + P_{22} u = P_{21} w + P_{22} K y$$ **Step 2: Solve for $y$.** $$y - P_{22} K y = P_{21} w$$ $$(I - P_{22} K) y = P_{21} w$$ $$y = (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21} w$$ **Step 3: Substitute back into the first row.** $$z = P_{11} w + P_{12} u = P_{11} w + P_{12} K y$$ **Step 4: Replace $y$ with its expression from Step 2.** $$z = P_{11} w + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21} w$$ **Step 5: Factor $w$ to obtain the LFT.** $$\boxed{\,z = \left[ P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21} \right] w = F_l(P, K) \cdot w\,}$$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** Given $G_1(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$ and $G_2(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, find: (i) The series interconnection $G = G_2 G_1$. (ii) The closed-loop TFM with unity negative feedback. (iii) The sensitivity function $S(s)$. **Solution:** **Part (i):** Series TFM is $G = G_2 G_1$: $$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ Multiplying row-by-column: $$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} + 0 & 0 + \frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 + 0 & 0 + \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} & \frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 & \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Part (ii):** Closed-loop with unity feedback, $H = I$: $$T(s) = (I + G)^{-1} G$$ Compute $I + G$: $$I + G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \frac{1}{s+1} & \frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 & 1 + \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+2}{s+1} & \frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 & \frac{s+5}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ Determinant: $$\det(I + G) = \frac{(s+2)(s+5)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$ Inverse using the $2 \times 2$ formula: $$(I + G)^{-1} = \frac{(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+5)} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+5}{s+3} & -\frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 & \frac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}$$ $$= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+1}{s+2} & -\frac{2(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} \\ 0 & \frac{s+3}{s+5} \end{bmatrix}$$ Finally: $$T(s) = (I + G)^{-1} G = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+1}{s+2} \cdot \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{2(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} \cdot 0 & \frac{s+1}{s+2} \cdot \frac{2}{s+3} - \frac{2(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} \cdot \frac{2}{s+3} \\ 0 & \frac{s+3}{s+5} \cdot \frac{2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ After simplification: $$T(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+2} & \frac{2(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)} - \frac{4(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)(s+3)} \\ 0 & \frac{2}{s+5} \end{bmatrix}$$ > **Note:** The off-diagonal element is non-zero — this is the **MIMO interaction (coupling)** that makes multivariable control fundamentally harder than SISO. **Part (iii):** Sensitivity is simply $S = (I + G)^{-1}$, which we have already computed above. ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Table of Key Results | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Form | |---|---|---| | $G_{series}$ | Two systems in cascade | $G_2 G_1$ | | $G_{parallel}$ | Two systems in parallel | $G_1 + G_2$ | | $T$ | Closed-loop (unity feedback) | $(I + G)^{-1} G$ | | $S$ | Sensitivity | $(I + G)^{-1}$ | | $L$ | Loop gain | $G K$ (or $K G$) | | $F_l(P,K)$ | Lower LFT | $P_{11} + P_{12}K(I - P_{22}K)^{-1}P_{21}$ | | $F_u(P,\Delta)$ | Upper LFT | $P_{22} + P_{21}\Delta(I - P_{11}\Delta)^{-1}P_{12}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Canonical Interconnections ```mermaid graph LR A[Reference r] --> B[Summing Junction] B --> C[Controller K] C --> D[Plant G] D --> E[Output y] E --> F[Sensor H] F --> B E --> G[Disturbance d] G -.-> E style A fill:#e1f5ff style E fill:#fff4e1 style B fill:#ffe1e1 style C fill:#e1ffe1 style D fill:#e1ffe1 ``` > The above is the **unity-equivalent** negative feedback loop. For $H = I$, the sensor block reduces to a direct wire. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generalised Plant Partitioning ```mermaid graph TB W[Exogenous Input w: refs and disturbances] --> P11[P11 Block] U[Control Input u] --> P12[P12 Block] W --> P21[P21 Block] U --> P22[P22 Block] P11 --> Z[Regulated Output z] P12 --> Y[Measured Output y] P21 --> Y P22 --> Y Y --> K[Controller K] K --> U style W fill:#e1f5ff style Z fill:#ffe1e1 style Y fill:#fff4e1 style U fill:#e1ffe1 style K fill:#f0e1ff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — LFT Visualisation ```mermaid graph LR W1[w] --> P11A[P11] W1 --> P21A[P21] P11A --> SUM1((+)) P21A --> Yout[y] U1[u] --> P12A[P12] U1 --> P22A[P22] P12A --> SUM1 SUM1 --> Zout[z] P22A --> Yout Yout --> Kblock[K] Kblock --> U1 style W1 fill:#e1f5ff style Zout fill:#ffe1e1 style Yout fill:#fff4e1 style U1 fill:#e1ffe1 style Kblock fill:#f0e1ff ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Interconnection Selection Logic ```mermaid graph TD START[Given two or more subsystems] --> Q1{Is the output of one
the input of another?} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is there any path
from output back
to input?} Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Do they share the
same input signal?} Q2 -- YES --> FB[Use Feedback
Interconnection Formula] Q2 -- NO --> SER[Use Series Cascade
G = G2 G1] Q3 -- YES --> PAR[Use Parallel
G = G1 + G2] Q3 -- NO --> LFT[Use Generalised LFT
with Partitioned Plant] style START fill:#e1f5ff style FB fill:#ffe1e1 style SER fill:#e1ffe1 style PAR fill:#fff4e1 style LFT fill:#f0e1ff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the **Sensitivity function** $S(s)$ and the **Complementary Sensitivity function** $T(s)$ for a multivariable closed-loop system. State and prove the identity $S + T = I$ for a MIMO system with unity feedback. **Model Answer:** The sensitivity function is defined as the transfer function matrix from an output disturbance to the output: $$S(s) = (I + G(s) K(s))^{-1}$$ The complementary sensitivity function is the transfer function matrix from the reference to the output: $$T(s) = G(s) K(s) (I + G(s) K(s))^{-1}$$ **Proof of $S + T = I$:** $$S + T = (I + GK)^{-1} + GK(I + GK)^{-1}$$ Factoring out $(I + GK)^{-1}$: $$S + T = (I + GK)^{-1} [I + GK] = I \quad \blacksquare$$ **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between the **lower LFT** $F_l(P, K)$ and the **upper LFT** $F_u(P, \Delta)$. State the formula for each. **Model Answer:** - The **Lower LFT** $F_l(P, K)$ is obtained when the controller $K$ closes the loop on the **lower** signal path of the partitioned plant $P$: $$F_l(P, K) = P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21}$$ - The **Upper LFT** $F_u(P, \Delta)$ is obtained when a perturbation $\Delta$ acts on the **upper** signal path: $$F_u(P, \Delta) = P_{22} + P_{21} \Delta (I - P_{11} \Delta)^{-1} P_{12}$$ The lower LFT is used to model **controller** feedback, while the upper LFT is used to model **plant uncertainty**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** [7 Marks] Consider a multivariable plant: $$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} & \frac{1}{s+2} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ Find the **closed-loop transfer function matrix** when $G(s)$ is placed in unity negative feedback. **(b)** [7 Marks] For a generalised plant: $$P(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & I \\ I & G(s) \end{bmatrix}, \quad G(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} I_2$$ with controller $K(s) = k \cdot I_2$, derive the **closed-loop transfer function** $F_l(P, K)$ from the exogenous input to the regulated output. #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a):** For unity negative feedback, $H = I$, so $T = (I + G)^{-1} G$. Step 1: Compute $I + G$: $$I + G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \frac{1}{s+1} & \frac{1}{s+2} \\ 0 & 1 + \frac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+2}{s+1} & \frac{1}{s+2} \\ 0 & \frac{s+4}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ Step 2: Determinant: $$\det(I + G) = \frac{(s+2)(s+4)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$ Step 3: Inverse: $$(I + G)^{-1} = \frac{(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+4)} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+4}{s+3} & -\frac{1}{s+2} \\ 0 & \frac{s+2}{s+1} \end{bmatrix}$$ $$(I + G)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+1}{s+2} & -\frac{s+3}{(s+2)(s+4)} \\ 0 & \frac{s+3}{s+4} \end{bmatrix}$$ Step 4: Multiply by $G$: $$T(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{s+1}{s+2} \cdot \frac{1}{s+1} + 0 & \frac{s+1}{s+2} \cdot \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{s+3}{(s+2)(s+4)} \cdot \frac{1}{s+3} \\ 0 & 0 + \frac{s+3}{s+4} \cdot \frac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$ Simplify the (1,1) entry: $\frac{1}{s+2}$. Simplify the (1,2) entry: $\frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+4} = \frac{(s+4) - (s+2)}{(s+2)(s+4)} = \frac{2}{(s+2)(s+4)}$. Simplify the (2,2) entry: $\frac{1}{s+4}$. $$\boxed{T(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+2} & \frac{2}{(s+2)(s+4)} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s+4} \end{bmatrix}}$$ **Part (b):** With $P_{11} = 0$, $P_{12} = I_2$, $P_{21} = I_2$, $P_{22} = G(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} I_2$, and $K = k I_2$: $$F_l(P, K) = P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21}$$ $$= 0 + I_2 \cdot k I_2 \cdot (I_2 - \frac{1}{s+2} I_2 \cdot k I_2)^{-1} \cdot I_2$$ $$= k \left(I_2 - \frac{k}{s+2} I_2\right)^{-1} = k \left(\frac{s+2-k}{s+2} I_2\right)^{-1}$$ $$F_l(P, K) = \frac{k(s+2)}{s + 2 - k} I_2$$ $$\boxed{F_l(P, K) = \frac{k(s+2)}{s + 2 - k} \cdot I_2}$$ For closed-loop stability, we need the pole $s = k - 2$ to lie in the open left half-plane, i.e., $k < 2$. #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice] **(a)** [7 Marks] Two MIMO systems $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ are connected in **series**. Derive the overall transfer function matrix. Explain why the order of multiplication matters in the multivariable case. Give a $2 \times 2$ numerical example where $G_1 G_2 \neq G_2 G_1$. **(b)** [7 Marks] For the partitioned plant: $$P(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s+1} & \frac{1}{s} \\ 1 & \frac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = 2$$ Compute the **lower LFT** $F_l(P, K)$. #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a):** In series interconnection, the output of $G_1$ becomes the input of $G_2$. If $u$ is the input and $y$ is the output: $$y_1 = G_1 u, \quad y = G_2 y_1 = G_2 G_1 u$$ So $G_{series} = G_2 G_1$ (note: the **second** system is written **first** in the multiplication because of right-to-left signal flow). **Order matters:** Matrix multiplication is non-commutative in general. For example: $$G_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$G_1 G_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_2 G_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Clearly $G_1 G_2 \neq G_2 G_1$, demonstrating the **non-commutativity** of MIMO interconnections. **Part (b):** $P_{11} = \frac{1}{s+1}$, $P_{12} = \frac{1}{s}$, $P_{21} = 1$, $P_{22} = \frac{1}{s+2}$, $K = 2$. $$F_l(P, K) = P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21}$$ Step 1: $I - P_{22} K = 1 - \frac{2}{s+2} = \frac{s+2-2}{s+2} = \frac{s}{s+2}$. Step 2: $(I - P_{22} K)^{-1} = \frac{s+2}{s}$. Step 3: $P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21} = \frac{1}{s} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{s+2}{s} \cdot 1 = \frac{2(s+2)}{s^2}$. Step 4: $F_l(P, K) = \frac{1}{s+1} + \frac{2(s+2)}{s^2}$. Combining over a common denominator: $$F_l(P, K) = \frac{s^2 + 2(s+1)(s+2)}{(s+1)s^2} = \frac{s^2 + 2(s^2 + 3s + 2)}{s^2(s+1)} = \frac{3s^2 + 6s + 4}{s^2(s+1)}$$ $$\boxed{F_l(P, K) = \frac{3s^2 + 6s + 4}{s^2(s+1)}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Order of multiplication in cascade:** Many students write $G_1 G_2$ instead of $G_2 G_1$. The signal flow dictates the order: *rightmost* system acts *first* on the input. **(–2 marks)** > 2. **Forgetting the identity matrix:** In $(I + GK)^{-1}$, the $I$ must be of dimension matching $GK$, not just a scalar 1. **(–1 to –2 marks)** > 3. **Assuming commutativity:** Writing $GK = KG$ is a fatal error in MIMO. **(–2 marks)** > 4. **Wrong block in LFT formula:** Students confuse $P_{11}$ vs $P_{22}$ in upper vs lower LFT. Memorise the *form* — lower LFT uses $P_{22}$ inside the inverse, upper LFT uses $P_{11}$. **(–3 marks)** > 5. **Inversion conditions:** Forgetting to verify that $(I - P_{22}K)$ or $(I + GK)$ is invertible (i.e., non-singular) before inverting. **(–1 mark)** > 6. **Dimensional mismatch:** Multiplying matrices of incompatible dimensions. **(–2 marks)** > 7. **Skipping the $S + T = I$ proof:** It is *not* a trivial identity in MIMO — show the matrix algebra step. **(–1 to –2 marks)** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Generalised Interconnection** - **Series (Cascade):** Output of one feeds input of next → $G = G_2 G_1$ (right-to-left signal flow). - **Parallel:** Common input, summed output → $G = G_1 + G_2$. - **Negative Feedback (Unity):** $T(s) = (I + G)^{-1} G$; $S(s) = (I + G)^{-1}$. - **General Negative Feedback:** $T = (I + GH)^{-1} G$ (with feedback path $H$). - **Sensitivity identity (always true for LTI MIMO):** $S + T = I$. - **MIMO ≠ SISO:** Matrix multiplication is non-commutative — verify block order from signal flow. - **Generalised Plant:** Partition $P$ into $2 \times 2$ blocks $(P_{11}, P_{12}, P_{21}, P_{22})$ corresponding to (w→z), (u→z), (w→y), (u→y). - **Lower LFT (controller feedback):** $F_l(P, K) = P_{11} + P_{12} K (I - P_{22} K)^{-1} P_{21}$. - **Upper LFT (uncertainty):** $F_u(P, \Delta) = P_{22} + P_{21} \Delta (I - P_{11} \Delta)^{-1} P_{12}$. - **Invertibility prerequisite:** $(I - P_{22}K)$ and $(I + GK)$ must be non-singular for the closed-loop to be well-defined. - **Stability check:** All poles of $F_l(P, K)$ or $T(s)$ must lie in the open left-half complex plane. - **Engineering uses:** Aircraft, robotics, process control, automotive, robust $H_\infty$ design. - **Numerical safety:** Always simplify the determinant of $2 \times 2$ matrices as $\frac{ad - bc}{}$ before inverting. - **Mermaid mental model:** Series = conveyor belt; Parallel = fork-and-merge; Feedback = return ramp.

topics.title: Simple Distillation Column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Distillation Column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

A **Simple Distillation Column** in the context of **Multivariable Control** refers to a **2 × 2 MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output) process** that serves as the canonical case study for studying **loop interaction, Relative Gain Array (RGA) analysis, and decoupling control strategies**. The column separates a binary mixture into a more volatile distillate product (top) and a less volatile bottoms product (bottom).

In control engineering terms, the column is modeled as:

$$
Y(s) = G(s)\,U(s) + D(s)
$$

where $Y(s) = [X_D(s),\ X_B(s)]^T$ are the **controlled variables** (compositions), $U(s) = [L(s),\ V(s)]^T$ are the **manipulated variables** (reflux and steam flow), and $D(s)$ represents load disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT746 – Multivariable Control)**, the Simple Distillation Column is the **benchmark 2 × 2 process** used to teach *interaction, RGA pairing, Niederlinski Index, and decoupling design* in Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car with two passengers** sitting on the same single-axle suspension. When the front passenger (one input) shifts weight, BOTH the front and rear ride heights change. You cannot adjust one without affecting the other.

A distillation column behaves similarly:
- **Reflux (L)** and **Steam (V)** are the two "passengers" (inputs).
- **Distillate composition (X_D)** and **Bottoms composition (X_B)** are the two "ride heights" (outputs).
- Changing reflux alters BOTH compositions, and changing steam also alters BOTH compositions → this is **loop interaction**.

## 1.3 Key Process Constants (Wood & Berry Column)

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical values used in KTU problems and most multivariable-control textbooks correspond to the **Wood & Berry (1973)** pilot-scale methanol-water column:
> - **Number of trays:** 8
> - **Feed location:** Tray 4
> - **Feed composition:** 0.5 mole fraction methanol
> - **Distillate composition (X_D):** 0.96
> - **Bottoms composition (X_B):** 0.04
> - **Operating pressure:** 1 atm
> - **Reflux flow (L):** 2.7 kmol/min
> - **Steam flow (V):** 3.0 kmol/min

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU

- It is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in End-Semester Examinations.
- It demonstrates *all* key Module 3 concepts in a single plant: interaction, RGA, pairing, stability, and decoupling.
- It connects theory to **real chemical engineering practice**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2 × 2 MIMO Structure of the Column

A binary distillation column is governed by **two material-balance loops** (top and bottom) interacting through internal flows. The standard control configuration is:

| Loop | Manipulated Variable (MV) | Controlled Variable (CV) |
|:----:|:--------------------------|:-------------------------|
| Loop 1 | Reflux flow $L$ | Distillate composition $X_D$ |
| Loop 2 | Steam flow $V$ | Bottoms composition $X_B$ |

The **steady-state gain matrix** captures how each MV affects each CV at operating point:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each element $k_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial (\text{CV}_i)}{\partial (\text{MV}_j)}\bigg|_s$

## 2.2 Physical Origin of Interaction

- An increase in **reflux L** enriches the top of the column → $X_D$ rises, but it also strips the bottom → $X_B$ falls.
- An increase in **steam V** strips the bottom more efficiently → $X_B$ falls, but it also carries more light component up → $X_D$ rises.

This **bidirectional coupling** is what makes the column a true MIMO system and demands multivariable analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU examiner can ask on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:---------------|
| 1 | Transfer Function Matrix | $G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11}(s) & g_{12}(s) \\ g_{21}(s) & g_{22}(s) \end{bmatrix}$ | $g_{ij}(s)$: i-th output response to j-th input |
| 2 | Open-loop steady-state gain | $k_{ij} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,g_{ij}(s)$ | Magnitude of steady response |
| 3 | RGA Element (λ_11) | $\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}$ | Pairing measure |
| 4 | RGA Elements (others) | $\lambda_{22} = \lambda_{11},\ \lambda_{12}=\lambda_{21}=1-\lambda_{11}$ | Complementary property |
| 5 | Condition Number | $\gamma(G) = \dfrac{\bar{\sigma}(G)}{\underline{\sigma}(G)}$ | Directional sensitivity |
| 6 | Niederlinski Index | $N_I = \dfrac{\det[K]}{\prod_i k_{ii}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{11}}$ | Stability indicator |
| 7 | Determinant Interaction Measure | $\text{DIM} = \dfrac{\vert \det[K] \vert}{\prod_i k_{ii}}$ | Cross-coupling magnitude |
| 8 | Decoupler (Ideal) | $D(s) = G(s)^{-1} \cdot \text{diag}[g_{ii}(s)]$ | Makes plant diagonal |
| 9 | Singular Value Decomposition | $G = U \Sigma V^T$ | Robust analysis tool |
| 10 | Closed-loop transfer matrix | $H(s) = G(s)\,D(s)\,[I + G(s)\,D(s)]^{-1}$ | With decoupler |

> [!NOTE]
> For pairing: choose $\lambda_{ij}$ **closest to +1** with all other $\lambda$ values between 0 and 1. A negative $\lambda$ ⇒ **never pair there** (will lead to instability for integral control).

## 2.4 The Wood & Berry Model (Canonical KTU Form)

The transfer-function model of the pilot column (gains in mol-fraction / kmol-min, time in minutes):

$$
G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{12.8\,e^{-s}}{16.7s+1} & \dfrac{-18.9\,e^{-3s}}{21s+1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{6.6\,e^{-7s}}{10.9s+1} & \dfrac{-19.4\,e^{-3s}}{14.4s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

with controlled vector $Y = [X_D,\ X_B]^T$ and manipulated vector $U = [L,\ V]^T$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical refineries** use 30–50 tray columns whose control is designed using exactly these RGA-based methods.
- **Pharmaceutical purification**, **cryogenic air separation**, and **ethanol dehydration** columns all rely on this analysis.
- Helps engineers decide **which sensor-actuator pair** to wire up before commissioning — saving millions in plant redesign.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Gain Matrix from Transfer Functions

**Step 1:** At steady state, $s \to 0$, so each first-order lag $\dfrac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} \to K$.

Therefore, from the Wood & Berry $G(s)$:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & -18.9 \\ 6.6 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> *Why negative off-diagonals?* Increasing reflux/steam **drives the controlled variables in opposite directions** (X_D rises while X_B falls relative to its setpoint impact), producing opposing signs in the gain matrix.

## 3.2 Detailed RGA Derivation for the Distillation Column

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$
\Lambda = K \circ (K^{-1})^T
$$

where $\circ$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.

**Step 2 — Compute $K^{-1}$.**

$$
\det(K) = (12.8)(-19.4) - (-18.9)(6.6) = -248.32 + 124.74 = -123.58
$$

$$
K^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det(K)} \begin{bmatrix} -19.4 & 18.9 \\ -6.6 & 12.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{-123.58} \begin{bmatrix} -19.4 & 18.9 \\ -6.6 & 12.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Transpose the inverse:**

$$
(K^{-1})^T = \dfrac{1}{-123.58} \begin{bmatrix} -19.4 & -6.6 \\ 18.9 & 12.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Hadamard product element-wise:**

$$
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & -18.9 \\ 6.6 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix} \circ \dfrac{1}{-123.58} \begin{bmatrix} -19.4 & -6.6 \\ 18.9 & 12.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each element:

$$
\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{(12.8)(-19.4)}{-123.58} = \dfrac{-248.32}{-123.58} = 2.009
$$

$$
\lambda_{12} = \dfrac{(-18.9)(-6.6)}{-123.58} = \dfrac{124.74}{-123.58} = -1.009
$$

$$
\lambda_{21} = \dfrac{(6.6)(18.9)}{-123.58} = \dfrac{124.74}{-123.58} = -1.009
$$

$$
\lambda_{22} = \dfrac{(-19.4)(12.8)}{-123.58} = \dfrac{-248.32}{-123.58} = 2.009
$$

**Step 5 — Final RGA Matrix:**

$$
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 2.009 & -1.009 \\ -1.009 & 2.009 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row sums = 1 ✓** and **column sums = 1 ✓** — the fundamental RGA property is satisfied.

## 3.3 Interpretation of the RGA

- $\lambda_{11} = \lambda_{22} = 2.01 \gg 1$ → **very strong interaction** when using the natural pairing (L–X_D, V–X_B).
- $\lambda_{12}, \lambda_{21} = -1.01 < 0$ → **forbidden pairing** (L–X_B, V–X_D) would cause instability under integral action.

**Recommended pairing rule (KTU examiner's favourite test point):**

> Pair MV₁ with CV₁ and MV₂ with CV₂ **only if** $\lambda_{11} > 0$ and $\lambda_{11}$ is closest to 1 among all positive entries. Here both diagonals are positive and large, so we accept the **diagonal pairing** (L–X_D and V–X_B), but we MUST implement a **decoupler** to handle the strong interaction.

## 3.4 Niederlinski Index Check

$$
N_I = \dfrac{\det(K)}{\prod_i k_{ii}} = \dfrac{-123.58}{(12.8)(-19.4)} = \dfrac{-123.58}{-248.32} = 0.498
$$

Since $0 < N_I < 1$ → the natural pairing is **stable** (closed-loop stable with integral control) but interaction is high. *Alternatively, $N_I = 1/\lambda_{11} = 1/2.009 = 0.498$ ✓*

## 3.5 Derivation of the Ideal Decoupler

To eliminate interaction, design a decoupler $D(s)$ such that the apparent plant $Q(s) = G(s) D(s)$ is **diagonal**.

**Step 1 — Set the off-diagonal elements to zero:**

$$
Q_{12}(s) = g_{11}(s) D_{12}(s) + g_{12}(s) D_{22}(s) = 0
$$

Solving for $D_{12}$:

$$
D_{12}(s) = -\dfrac{g_{12}(s)}{g_{11}(s)} D_{22}(s)
$$

**Step 2 — By symmetry:**

$$
D_{21}(s) = -\dfrac{g_{21}(s)}{g_{22}(s)} D_{11}(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Choose diagonal terms as unity** (simplified decoupler):

$$
D_{11}(s) = D_{22}(s) = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Wood & Berry transfer functions:**

$$
D_{12}(s) = -\dfrac{-18.9\,e^{-3s}/(21s+1)}{12.8\,e^{-s}/(16.7s+1)} = \dfrac{18.9}{12.8} \cdot \dfrac{16.7s+1}{21s+1} \cdot e^{-2s}
$$

$$
D_{12}(s) = 1.477 \cdot \dfrac{16.7s+1}{21s+1} \cdot e^{-2s}
$$

$$
D_{21}(s) = -\dfrac{6.6\,e^{-7s}/(10.9s+1)}{-19.4\,e^{-3s}/(14.4s+1)} = \dfrac{6.6}{19.4} \cdot \dfrac{14.4s+1}{10.9s+1} \cdot e^{-4s}
$$

$$
D_{21}(s) = 0.340 \cdot \dfrac{14.4s+1}{10.9s+1} \cdot e^{-4s}
$$

**Step 5 — The decoupler matrix:**

$$
D(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1.477 \cdot \dfrac{16.7s+1}{21s+1} e^{-2s} \\[6pt] 0.340 \cdot \dfrac{14.4s+1}{10.9s+1} e^{-4s} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified decoupler is preferred** over the ideal decoupler (which would require $D(s) = G(s)^{-1}$ and is more sensitive to model errors). It is the form most expected in KTU answers.

## 3.6 Resulting Effective Plant

After inserting $D(s)$, the effective plant becomes:

$$
Q(s) = G(s)\,D(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{12.8\,e^{-s}}{16.7s+1} & 0 \\ 0 & \dfrac{-19.4\,e^{-3s}}{14.4s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The two loops are now **completely decoupled** and can be tuned independently using classical SISO methods (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Process Architecture of a Simple Distillation Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Feed Mixture Tray 4] --> C1{Column Shell 8 Trays}
    C1 --> C2[Condenser]
    C2 --> R1[Reflux Drum]
    R1 --> P1[Reflux Pump L]
    P1 --> C1
    R1 --> D1[Distillate Product X_D]
    C1 --> R2[Reboiler Steam V]
    R2 --> B1[Bottoms Product X_B]
    SV1[Composition Analyzer X_D] --> C1
    SV2[Composition Analyzer X_B] --> C1
```

## 4.2 2 × 2 MIMO Control Block Diagram (with Interaction Paths)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Setpoint X_D_sp] --> E1[Summing Junction e1]
    SP2[Setpoint X_B_sp] --> E2[Summing Junction e2]
    E1 --> GC1[Controller Gc1]
    E2 --> GC2[Controller Gc2]
    GC1 --> DC[Decoupler D s]
    GC2 --> DC
    DC --> u1[MV1 Reflux L]
    DC --> u2[MV2 Steam V]
    u1 --> P11[g11 Plant]
    u1 --> P12[g12 Interaction]
    u2 --> P21[g21 Interaction]
    u2 --> P22[g22 Plant]
    P11 --> ADD1((+))
    P21 --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> CV1[X_D Output]
    P12 --> ADD2((+))
    P22 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> CV2[X_B Output]
    CV1 --> E1
    CV2 --> E2
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph of RGA Pairing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Process Gain Matrix K] --> B0[Compute K inverse]
    B0 --> C0[Transpose K inverse]
    C0 --> D0[Hadamard Product K circ K inv transpose]
    D0 --> E0[RGA Matrix Lambda]
    E0 --> F0{lambda 11 greater than 0 and closest to 1}
    F0 -->|Yes| G0[Diagonal Pairing ACCEPTED]
    F0 -->|No| H0[Swap Pairing or Add Decoupler]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Decoupled Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reference X_D_sp] --> A1[Gc1 PI Controller]
    A1 --> D11[Decoupler D11 = 1]
    A1 --> D12[Decoupler D12]
    R2[Reference X_B_sp] --> A2[Gc2 PI Controller]
    A2 --> D22[Decoupler D22 = 1]
    A2 --> D21[Decoupler D21]
    D11 --> P11[g11 Process]
    D12 --> P22[g22 Process]
    P11 --> Y1[X_D Output Loop 1]
    P22 --> Y2[X_B Output Loop 2]
    Y1 --> R1
    Y2 --> R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the student:** The decoupler computes **compensating corrections** in real time. When reflux L is increased, the decoupler simultaneously nudges steam V by an amount $D_{21} \cdot \Delta L$ so that the bottom composition $X_B$ does not drift. This restores SISO behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*State the Relative Gain Array (RGA) for a 2 × 2 system and explain what a value of $\lambda_{11} = 2$ indicates about the loop interaction in a distillation column.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The RGA is defined as $\Lambda = K \circ (K^{-1})^T$. For a 2 × 2 system:

$$
\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}
$$

A value of $\lambda_{11} = 2$ means that when Loop 2 is closed using the natural pairing, Loop 1 effectively experiences **twice the open-loop gain** it would have had if Loop 2 were open. This indicates **strong interaction** — changes in reflux L affect distillate composition $X_D$ twice as much when the steam loop is active. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*List the four elements of the Wood and Berry transfer function matrix of a binary distillation column and identify the controlled and manipulated variables.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For the Wood and Berry column:
- **Controlled variables (CV):** Distillate composition $X_D$, Bottoms composition $X_B$
- **Manipulated variables (MV):** Reflux flow $L$, Steam flow $V$

$$
G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{12.8\,e^{-s}}{16.7s+1} & \dfrac{-18.9\,e^{-3s}}{21s+1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{6.6\,e^{-7s}}{10.9s+1} & \dfrac{-19.4\,e^{-3s}}{14.4s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$g_{11}$: $X_D$ response to $L$; $g_{12}$: $X_D$ response to $V$; $g_{21}$: $X_B$ response to $L$; $g_{22}$: $X_B$ response to $V$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Most students **forget to check $\det(K) \neq 0$** before computing $K^{-1}$ — losing 1 mark.
> - Many compute only $\lambda_{11}$ and **omit $\lambda_{12}, \lambda_{21}, \lambda_{22}$** — losing 2 marks.
> - Students often write the **decoupler without justifying the pairing** from the RGA — losing 2 marks.
> - **Time delays in the decoupler** are frequently dropped — losing 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** For the Wood–Berry binary distillation column with transfer function matrix given below, compute the **steady-state gain matrix** $K$, the **RGA matrix** $\Lambda$, and **recommend the loop pairing** with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **simplified decoupler** $D(s)$ for the recommended pairing and derive the **effective decoupled plant** $Q(s) = G(s) D(s)$. **(7 marks)**

$$
G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{12.8\,e^{-s}}{16.7s+1} & \dfrac{-18.9\,e^{-3s}}{21s+1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{6.6\,e^{-7s}}{10.9s+1} & \dfrac{-19.4\,e^{-3s}}{14.4s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steady-state gain matrix and RGA (7 marks):**

Set $s = 0$:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & -18.9 \\ 6.6 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$\det(K) = (12.8)(-19.4) - (-18.9)(6.6) = -248.32 + 124.74 = -123.58$

$$
K^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{-123.58} \begin{bmatrix} -19.4 & 18.9 \\ -6.6 & 12.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

RGA element:

$$
\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} = \dfrac{(12.8)(-19.4)}{-123.58} = \dfrac{-248.32}{-123.58} = 2.01
$$

By row/column sum property:

$$
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & -1.01 \\ -1.01 & 2.01 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Pairing recommendation:** $\lambda_{11} = 2.01$ is positive and the largest positive diagonal element. Hence, **diagonal pairing** is recommended (Loop 1: L → X_D; Loop 2: V → X_B). The negative off-diagonals forbid the alternate pairing. (7 marks)

**(b) Simplified Decoupler Design (7 marks):**

Choose $D_{11} = D_{22} = 1$. Off-diagonal decouplers:

$$
D_{12}(s) = -\dfrac{g_{12}(s)}{g_{11}(s)} = -\dfrac{(-18.9\,e^{-3s})/(21s+1)}{(12.8\,e^{-s})/(16.7s+1)} = 1.477 \cdot \dfrac{16.7s+1}{21s+1} e^{-2s}
$$

$$
D_{21}(s) = -\dfrac{g_{21}(s)}{g_{22}(s)} = -\dfrac{(6.6\,e^{-7s})/(10.9s+1)}{(-19.4\,e^{-3s})/(14.4s+1)} = 0.340 \cdot \dfrac{14.4s+1}{10.9s+1} e^{-4s}
$$

$$
D(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1.477 \cdot \dfrac{16.7s+1}{21s+1} e^{-2s} \\[6pt] 0.340 \cdot \dfrac{14.4s+1}{10.9s+1} e^{-4s} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Effective decoupled plant:

$$
Q(s) = G(s) D(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{12.8\,e^{-s}}{16.7s+1} & 0 \\[6pt] 0 & \dfrac{-19.4\,e^{-3s}}{14.4s+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The two loops are now fully decoupled and can be tuned as independent SISO systems. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **Niederlinski Index** as a measure of stability for a multivariable control system. Compute the index for the Wood–Berry column and comment on the pairing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Determinant Interaction Measure (DIM)** and the **Condition Number**. Compute both for the Wood–Berry column steady-state gain matrix and interpret. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Niederlinski Index (7 marks):**

The Niederlinski Index is defined as:

$$
N_I = \dfrac{\det[K]}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} k_{ii}}
$$

**Rule:** If $N_I < 0$ for any pairing, the closed-loop system with integral control is **unstable** for that pairing. If $0 < N_I < 1$, the system is **stable** but has interaction. If $N_I > 1$, the system is **stable** but may have large interaction.

For the Wood–Berry column:

$$
N_I = \dfrac{-123.58}{(12.8)(-19.4)} = \dfrac{-123.58}{-248.32} = 0.498
$$

Since $0 < N_I = 0.498 < 1$, the diagonal pairing is **stable** under integral action but suffers from significant interaction. A decoupler is recommended. (7 marks)

**(b) DIM and Condition Number (7 marks):**

The **Determinant Interaction Measure** is:

$$
\text{DIM} = \dfrac{\vert \det[K] \vert}{\prod_i \vert k_{ii} \vert}
$$

Computing:

$$
\text{DIM} = \dfrac{123.58}{(12.8)(19.4)} = \dfrac{123.58}{248.32} = 0.498
$$

A DIM close to 1 means **low interaction**; values near 0 indicate **strong interaction**. Since DIM = 0.498, interaction is significant.

**Condition Number** (using singular values of $K$):

$$
\gamma(K) = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}(K)}{\sigma_{\min}(K)}
$$

Singular values of $K$ are obtained from eigenvalues of $K^T K$:

$$
K^T K = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & -18.9 \\ 6.6 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & 6.6 \\ -18.9 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 521.05 & -281.78 \\ -281.78 & 419.92 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Trace = 940.97, Determinant = $(521.05)(419.92) - (281.78)^2 = 218{,}796.8 - 79{,}400.4 = 139{,}396.4$

Eigenvalues: $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{940.97 \pm \sqrt{940.97^2 - 4 \cdot 139{,}396.4}}{2} = \dfrac{940.97 \pm \sqrt{153{,}213}}{2}$

$\sqrt{153{,}213} \approx 391.4$

$\sigma_{\max}^2 \approx 666.2 \Rightarrow \sigma_{\max} \approx 25.8$

$\sigma_{\min}^2 \approx 274.8 \Rightarrow \sigma_{\min} \approx 16.6$

$$
\gamma(K) \approx \dfrac{25.8}{16.6} = 1.55
$$

A condition number close to 1 indicates a **well-conditioned** plant (low directional sensitivity). Here $\gamma = 1.55$ is moderately small — the column is reasonably well-conditioned. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ A simple distillation column is the **canonical 2 × 2 MIMO** process in Multivariable Control.
- ✅ **Controlled variables:** $X_D$ (distillate) and $X_B$ (bottoms).
- ✅ **Manipulated variables:** $L$ (reflux) and $V$ (steam).
- ✅ **Wood & Berry steady-state gain matrix:** $K = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 & -18.9 \\ 6.6 & -19.4 \end{bmatrix}$
- ✅ **RGA:** $\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & -1.01 \\ -1.01 & 2.01 \end{bmatrix}$ — strong interaction, diagonal pairing allowed.
- ✅ **Pairing rule:** pick $\lambda$ closest to **+1** and avoid **negative** entries.
- ✅ **Niederlinski Index** for Wood–Berry: $N_I = 0.498$ → stable but interactive.
- ✅ **Simplified decoupler formulae:** $D_{12} = -g_{12}/g_{11}$, $D_{21} = -g_{21}/g_{22}$, with $D_{11} = D_{22} = 1$.
- ✅ **Decoupled plant** is diagonal — enables SISO tuning.
- ✅ **Negative RGA element** → forbidden pairing (closed-loop unstable with integral control).
- ✅ **DIM = $1/\lambda_{11}$** for 2 × 2 systems.
- ✅ **Condition number** measures directional sensitivity; values near 1 are ideal.
- ✅ Time delays in the decoupler must be retained (otherwise the decoupler becomes physically unrealizable).
- ✅ The **column's interaction** is due to internal coupling via liquid and vapour traffic — change one tray, you change the whole column.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always write the **RGA pairing rule** as justification before designing the decoupler.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Control Variable Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Control Variable Structures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Control Variable Structures A **Variable Structure Control (VSC) System** is a class of nonlinear control systems characterized by a **discontinuous control law** that deliberately switches between two or more distinct continuous control sub-structures (called "structures") as the system state traverses a predefined **switching surface** (or sliding manifold) in the state space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Approved Terminology):** > A *Variable Structure System (VSS)* is a system whose structure intentionally changes during the transient response, governed by a set of switching logic functions. The resulting composite motion is called **Sliding Mode**, and the corresponding controller is the **Sliding Mode Controller (SMC)**. Mathematically, a variable structure controller takes the form: $$ u(t) = \begin{cases} u^{+}(x, t), & \text{if } S(x, t) > 0 \\ u^{-}(x, t), & \text{if } S(x, t) < 0 \end{cases} $$ Where: - $u^+(x, t)$ and $u^-(x, t)$ are **continuous sub-control laws** - $S(x, t) = 0$ defines the **sliding surface (manifold)** - The switching is governed by the **sign of the scalar switching function** $S(x, t)$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gear-Shifting Car on a Slippery Slope" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — "Imagine driving a manual car up an icy hill"** Imagine you are driving a car on a road covered with black ice. The road is so slippery that no single steering angle can keep the car on the desired lane — if you turn left too much, you slide off the right edge; if you turn right too much, you slide off the left edge. - A **novice driver** keeps adjusting the steering smoothly, and the car oscillates dangerously across the lane. - An **expert driver** instead **rapidly toggles the steering between full-LEFT and full-RIGHT**. Because the ice is frictionless, the rapid dithering creates an *effective* "average" steering that the car *smoothly obeys*. The car now slides up the hill along a virtual line — the **sliding surface**. This rapid toggling between two extreme control actions is exactly what a **Sliding Mode Controller** does. The two extreme actions are $u^+$ and $u^-$, and the imaginary line the car follows is the **sliding manifold** $S(x,t)=0$. ### 1.3 The Three Canonical Phases of a VSC Response A variable structure control response always exhibits **three distinct temporal phases**: | Phase | Name | Duration | Description | |:-----:|:-----|:--------:|:------------| | **I** | **Reaching Phase** | $t \in [0, t_{r})$ | System state is driven *toward* the sliding manifold $S=0$ | | **II** | **Sliding Phase** | $t \in [t_{r}, t_{s})$ | State is *constrained* to move along $S=0$; dynamics become reduced-order and invariant | | **III** | **Steady-State Phase** | $t \geq t_{s}$ | State converges to the desired equilibrium point $(x=0)$ | > [!TIP] > **Why is this important in KTU exams?** The Board frequently asks students to **sketch the phase portrait** showing these three phases. Memorize that the *trajectory first approaches* $S=0$, *then slides along it* toward the origin. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics For all KTU numerical problems, the following design parameters are standard: - **Sliding manifold gain matrix**: $S(x) = Cx$ where $C \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ - **Switching gain**: $K \geq 0$ (scalar or vector) - **Boundary layer thickness**: $\varepsilon > 0$ (used to mitigate *chattering*) - **Reaching time**: $t_r \leq t_{\text{max}}$ (user-specified) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Variable Structure Control ### 2.1.1 Canonical State-Space Plant Model Consider a single-input, single-output (SISO) nonlinear system in **canonical controllable form** — this is the **assumed plant form** in nearly every KTU Module 4 VSC problem: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2 $$ $$ \dot{x}_2 = x_3 $$ $$ \vdots $$ $$ \dot{x}_n = f(x) + b(x)\,u(t) + d(t) $$ Where: - $x = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector - $f(x)$ is the **known nonlinear drift function** (or linear term $a^T x$) - $b(x) > 0$ is the **control gain function** (assumed positive without loss of generality) - $d(t)$ is the **matched external disturbance** with known bound $\vert d(t) \vert \leq D$ - $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}$ is the scalar control input ### 2.1.2 The Sliding Surface (Switching Manifold) Design The sliding surface $S(x) = 0$ is the **geometric locus** of desired reduced-order dynamics. For an $n^{th}$-order system, the canonical switching function is: $$ S(x) = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \dots + c_{n-1} x_{n-1} + x_n $$ Or in compact vector form: $$ S(x) = C\,x = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & c_2 & \dots & c_{n-1} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hurwitz Stability Requirement (Board-Favorite Question):** > The coefficient vector $C$ **must** be chosen such that the reduced-order polynomial > $$c_{n-1} s^{n-1} + c_{n-2} s^{n-2} + \dots + c_1$$ > is **Hurwitz** (all roots in the open left-half $s$-plane). This guarantees that during the sliding phase, the system behaves like a stable $(n-1)$-order linear system. ### 2.1.3 The Control Law — Equivalent Control & Switching Control The total SMC law has **two superimposed components**: $$ u(t) = u_{\text{eq}}(x) + u_{\text{sw}}(x, t) $$ **Component 1 — Equivalent Control $u_{\text{eq}}$:** Derived by enforcing the *invariance condition* $S(x) = 0$ and $\dot{S}(x) = 0$ during ideal sliding: $$ u_{\text{eq}}(x) = -\frac{1}{b(x)} \left[ f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} c_i x_{i+1} \right] $$ **Component 2 — Switching Control $u_{\text{sw}}$:** $$ u_{\text{sw}}(x, t) = -\frac{K(x)}{b(x)} \,\text{sgn}(S) $$ Where $\text{sgn}(S)$ is the **signum function**: $$ \text{sgn}(S) = \begin{cases} +1, & S > 0 \\ \ 0, & S = 0 \\ -1, & S < 0 \end{cases} $$ ### 2.1.4 The Reachability Condition (Existence of Sliding Mode) For sliding mode to occur on $S = 0$, the state must reach the manifold in **finite time**. The **Utkin reachability condition** is: $$ S(x) \cdot \dot{S}(x) \leq -\eta \vert S(x) \vert, \quad \eta > 0 $$ Equivalently, using a **Lyapunov candidate** $V(x) = \frac{1}{2}S^2$: $$ \dot{V}(x) = S\,\dot{S} \leq -\eta \vert S \vert < 0, \quad S \neq 0 $$ This guarantees **finite-time reaching** with reaching time bounded by: $$ t_r \leq \frac{\vert S(x(0)) \vert}{\eta} $$ ### 2.1.5 The Chattering Phenomenon > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that ideal sliding mode requires **infinite switching frequency**, which is physically impossible. The finite switching delay $\tau$ causes **chattering** — high-frequency oscillation around $S=0$. The chattering amplitude grows linearly with the switching delay $\tau$: $$ \vert \Delta x \vert_{\text{chatter}} \propto \tau \cdot K $$ **Mitigation: The Boundary Layer Method** — replace $\text{sgn}(S)$ with a smooth approximation $\text{sat}(S/\varepsilon)$: $$ \text{sat}\left(\frac{S}{\varepsilon}\right) = \begin{cases} \text{sgn}(S), & \vert S \vert \geq \varepsilon \\ S/\varepsilon, & \vert S \vert < \varepsilon \end{cases} $$ Where $\varepsilon > 0$ is the **boundary layer thickness**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes **every formula** you must memorize for Module 4 VSC problems. This is the consolidated KTU 2024 formula sheet. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Purpose / Use** | |:-----:|:-------------|:------------|:------------------| | 1 | Sliding Surface (SISO) | $S(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i$ with $c_n = 1$ | Defines the switching manifold | | 2 | Sliding Surface (MIMO) | $S(x) = C\,x$, $\;C \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Multivariable switching function | | 3 | Equivalent Control | $u_{\text{eq}} = -b^{-1}(x)\left[f(x) + \sum c_i \dot{x}_i\right]$ | Continuous part of SMC | | 4 | Switching Control | $u_{\text{sw}} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S)\,/\,b(x)$ | Discontinuous correcting action | | 5 | Reachability Condition | $S\,\dot{S} \leq -\eta \vert S \vert$ | Guarantees finite-time reaching | | 6 | Reaching Time Bound | $t_r \leq \vert S(0) \vert / \eta$ | Maximum time to reach manifold | | 7 | Lyapunov Function | $V = \frac{1}{2} S^2$ | Proof of stability | | 8 | Hurwitz Condition | All roots of $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} c_i s^i$ in LHP | Stability during sliding | | 9 | Saturation Function | $\text{sat}(S/\varepsilon)$ for $\vert S \vert < \varepsilon$ | Chattering suppression | | 10 | Reduced Order Dynamics | $\dot{x}_{n-1} + c_{n-1} x_{n-1} + \dots + c_1 x_1 = 0$ | $(n{-}1)$-order motion on $S=0$ | | 11 | Disturbance Bound | $\vert d(t) \vert \leq D$ | Used to size switching gain $K$ | | 12 | Switching Gain Bound | $K \geq D + \eta$ | Robust rejection condition | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Variable structure control is **not a purely academic exercise**. It is deployed in: - **Robotic manipulator trajectory tracking** (industrial assembly lines, KUKA, ABB arms) - **Aircraft flight control surfaces** (high-bandwidth attitude regulation) - **Electric drives and DC motor speed control** (insensitive to load variation) - **Missile guidance systems** (robust against aerodynamic parameter uncertainty) - **Power electronic converters** (sliding-mode DC-DC buck/boost regulators) - **Magnetic levitation trains** (highly nonlinear, inherently unstable plant) The **killer feature** is *invariance*: once on $S=0$, the system dynamics become **completely insensitive to matched disturbances and parametric uncertainty** — a property no linear controller can match. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Control for a Second-Order Plant > **Problem Statement (from KTU typical past paper):** > Given the second-order nonlinear plant: > $$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) + b\,u$$ > with $a > 0$ and $b > 0$, derive the equivalent control law when the sliding surface is $S(x) = c\,x_1 + x_2$ with $c > 0$. ### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation: **Step 1 — Write the full state vector and plant equations.** $$ x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ b \end{bmatrix} $$ So we have: $$ \dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u $$ **Step 2 — Formulate the sliding surface as a linear functional of the state.** $$ S(x) = C\,x = \begin{bmatrix} c & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = c\,x_1 + x_2 $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $S(x)$ with respect to time along the system trajectories.** $$ \dot{S}(x) = c\,\dot{x}_1 + \dot{x}_2 $$ Substituting the plant dynamics: $$ \dot{S}(x) = c\,x_2 + \big[-a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) + b\,u\big] $$ $$ \dot{S}(x) = c\,x_2 - a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) + b\,u $$ **Step 4 — Apply the invariance (sliding) condition $\dot{S}(x) = 0$ to find the equivalent control.** The defining property of sliding mode is that once on the manifold, the state remains on it for all future time. Mathematically: $$ \dot{S}(x) \big|_{u = u_{\text{eq}}} = 0 $$ Setting $\dot{S}(x) = 0$ and solving for $u$: $$ 0 = c\,x_2 - a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) + b\,u_{\text{eq}} $$ $$ b\,u_{\text{eq}} = -c\,x_2 + a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) $$ $$ u_{\text{eq}}(x) = \frac{a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) - c\,x_2}{b} $$ **Step 5 — Verify the Hurwitz reduced-order dynamics on the sliding manifold.** On $S = 0$, we have $x_2 = -c\,x_1$. Substituting back: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2 = -c\,x_1 $$ This is a **first-order linear ODE** whose solution is: $$ x_1(t) = x_1(0)\,e^{-c\,t} $$ For $c > 0$, $x_1 \to 0$ **exponentially** as $t \to \infty$. The reduced polynomial is $c$ (a positive real scalar), which is Hurwitz. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stability Proof via Lyapunov Direct Method > **Goal:** Prove that the switching control $u_{\text{sw}} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S)$ enforces the reaching condition. ### Exhaustive Derivation: **Step 1 — Construct the scalar Lyapunov candidate function.** $$ V(x) = \frac{1}{2} S^2(x) $$ By construction, $V(x) > 0$ for all $S \neq 0$, and $V(0) = 0$. **Step 2 — Compute the time derivative $\dot{V}$.** $$ \dot{V}(x) = S(x) \cdot \dot{S}(x) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the closed-loop expression for $\dot{S}$.** We have: $$ \dot{S}(x) = c\,x_2 - a\,x_2^2 \sin(x_1) + b\,\big[u_{\text{eq}}(x) + u_{\text{sw}}(x,t)\big] $$ But by construction $u_{\text{eq}}$ makes $\dot{S} = 0$ *without* the switching term. Hence: $$ \dot{S}(x)\big|_{\text{closed-loop}} = b\,u_{\text{sw}}(x,t) = -K\,\text{sgn}(S) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into $\dot{V}$.** $$ \dot{V}(x) = S \cdot \big[-K\,\text{sgn}(S)\big] = -K\,S\,\text{sgn}(S) $$ **Step 5 — Apply the algebraic identity $S\,\text{sgn}(S) = \vert S \vert$.** $$ \dot{V}(x) = -K\,\vert S(x) \vert $$ **Step 6 — Conclude stability.** Since $K > 0$ and $\vert S \vert \geq 0$, we have $\dot{V} \leq 0$ with equality *only* when $S = 0$. By **Barbashin–Krasovskii theorem** (since $V$ is positive definite and $\dot{V}$ is negative definite), the origin of the $S$-dynamics is **globally asymptotically stable**, and the sliding surface is reached in **finite time**. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MIMO Extension: Matrix Switching Surface For a multivariable plant with $m$ inputs and $m$ outputs, the sliding surface becomes an $m$-dimensional manifold: $$ S(x) = \begin{bmatrix} S_1(x) \\ S_2(x) \\ \vdots \\ S_m(x) \end{bmatrix} = C\,x \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1} $$ The equivalent control is found from $\dot{S} = C\dot{x} = 0$: $$ u_{\text{eq}}(x) = -(C\,B)^{-1}\,C\,f(x) $$ where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ and $(C\,B)$ must be **nonsingular**. The reaching condition becomes: $$ S^T \dot{S} = S^T\,C\,\dot{x} \leq -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \eta_i \vert S_i \vert, \quad \eta_i > 0 $$ For a diagonal switching gain matrix $K = \text{diag}(K_1, \dots, K_m)$, the multivariable control law is: $$ u(x,t) = -(C\,B)^{-1}\left[C\,f(x) + K\,\text{sgn}(S)\right] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decoupling Property:** A properly designed MIMO SMC **inherently decouples** the closed-loop dynamics during the sliding phase — each output $y_i$ is governed independently by $S_i = 0$. This is one of the most powerful properties of VSC in multivariable systems. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** Design a sliding mode controller for the plant $\ddot{y} = -3\dot{y} + 5u + d(t)$ where $\vert d(t) \vert \leq 1.5$. Choose sliding surface $S = 2y + \dot{y}$. Compute the reaching condition gain. **Solution:** Step 1 — Define states $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$. $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -3\,x_2 + 5\,u + d(t) $$ Step 2 — Form the switching function. $$ S = 2\,x_1 + x_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{S} = 2\,x_2 + (-3\,x_2 + 5\,u + d) = -x_2 + 5\,u + d $$ Step 3 — Set $\dot{S} = 0$ to find $u_{\text{eq}}$. $$ u_{\text{eq}} = \frac{x_2 - d}{5} $$ For the design, we neglect $d$ (it is handled by the switching term): $$ u_{\text{eq}} \approx \frac{x_2}{5} $$ Step 4 — Apply the switching control. $$ u_{\text{sw}} = -\frac{K}{5}\,\text{sgn}(S) $$ Step 5 — Total control law. $$ u = \frac{x_2}{5} - \frac{K}{5}\,\text{sgn}(S) = \frac{1}{5}\left[x_2 - K\,\text{sgn}(S)\right] $$ Step 6 — Determine minimum $K$ for robustness against $d(t)$. The closed-loop $\dot{S}$ becomes: $$ \dot{S} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S) + d(t) $$ Hence: $$ S\,\dot{S} = S\,d - K\,S\,\text{sgn}(S) \leq \vert S \vert D - K\,\vert S \vert = -(K - D)\,\vert S \vert $$ For finite-time reaching, require $K - D > \eta > 0$, i.e.: $$ \boxed{K > D + \eta = 1.5 + \eta} $$ Choosing $\eta = 0.5$ gives $\boxed{K \geq 2.0}$. **Step 7 — Verify reduced-order dynamics.** On $S = 0$: $\;x_2 = -2\,x_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \dot{x}_1 = -2\,x_1 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1(t) = x_1(0)\,e^{-2t}$. The pole is at $s = -2 < 0$ → **Hurwitz, asymptotically stable.** $\blacksquare$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Sliding Mode Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference r(t)"]:::input --> Sum1((+)) Sum1((+)) --> Plant["Nonlinear Plant
