What is meant by a sliding surface?** **Model Answer:** A Variable Structure Control (VSC) system is a nonlinear control system whose control structure intentionally switches between two or more continuous feedback laws based on the location of the system state relative to a predefined **switching surface** $S(x,t) = 0$ in the state space. The switching action produces a composite discontinuous control signal. A **sliding surface** (or switching manifold) is a hypersurface of dimension $(n-1)$ in the $n$-dimensional state space, defined by the equation $S(x) = C\,x = 0$, on which the system exhibits reduced-order stable dynamics once the state trajectory is constrained to it. > **Key terms to memorize (1-mark each):** (i) discontinuous control law, (ii) switching surface, (iii) reduced-order invariant motion. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **Explain the reaching condition in sliding mode control. State the Lyapunov function used to prove it.** **Model Answer:** The **reaching condition** is the requirement that the system state trajectory, starting from any initial condition, must reach the sliding surface $S(x) = 0$ in **finite time** and remain on it. Mathematically (Utkin's condition): $$ S(x) \cdot \dot{S}(x) \leq -\eta \vert S(x) \vert, \quad \eta > 0 $$ The **Lyapunov candidate function** used to prove finite-time reaching is: $$ V(x) = \frac{1}{2} S^2(x), \quad V(x) \geq 0,\; V(0) = 0 $$ Its time derivative under the closed-loop SMC satisfies: $$ \dot{V}(x) = S\,\dot{S} = -K\,S\,\text{sgn}(S) = -K\,\vert S \vert \leq 0 $$ guaranteeing asymptotic stability of $S = 0$. The bounded reaching time is: $$ t_r \leq \frac{\vert S(x(0)) \vert}{\eta} $$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question — Choice A]** ### Question A — Sliding Mode Control Design (14 Marks) Consider the second-order nonlinear plant: $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -5\,x_2 + 2\,x_1^2 + 7\,u + d(t) $$ with $\vert d(t) \vert \leq 2.0$. Design a sliding mode controller with switching manifold $S(x) = 3\,x_1 + x_2$. **(a)** Derive the equivalent control law $u_{\text{eq}}$. Show that the reduced-order sliding dynamics are stable. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design the switching control $u_{\text{sw}}$ such that the system reaches $S = 0$ in finite time despite the disturbance. Determine the minimum switching gain $K$ with $\eta = 1.0$. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Equivalent Control Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Differentiate $S$ along trajectories. $$ \dot{S} = 3\,\dot{x}_1 + \dot{x}_2 = 3\,x_2 + \big[-5\,x_2 + 2\,x_1^2 + 7\,u + d(t)\big] $$ $$ \dot{S} = -2\,x_2 + 2\,x_1^2 + 7\,u + d(t) $$ **Step 2:** Set $\dot{S} = 0$ and neglect $d(t)$ for the equivalent control: $$ 0 = -2\,x_2 + 2\,x_1^2 + 7\,u_{\text{eq}} $$ $$ \boxed{u_{\text{eq}}(x) = \frac{2\,x_2 - 2\,x_1^2}{7} = \frac{2(x_2 - x_1^2)}{7}} $$ **Step 3:** Verify reduced-order stability. On $S = 0$: $\;x_2 = -3\,x_1$. $$ \dot{x}_1 = x_2 = -3\,x_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1(t) = x_1(0)\,e^{-3t} $$ The reduced-order system has a single pole at $s = -3$ (Hurwitz, strictly negative real part). Therefore, the sliding-mode reduced dynamics are **globally asymptotically stable**. $\blacksquare$ #### Part (b) — Switching Control & Minimum Gain (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Propose the total control law. $$ u = u_{\text{eq}} - \frac{K}{7}\,\text{sgn}(S) $$ **Step 2:** Substitute back into $\dot{S}$. $$ \dot{S} = 7\left[-\frac{K}{7}\,\text{sgn}(S)\right] + d(t) = -K\,\text{sgn}(S) + d(t) $$ **Step 3:** Form $S\,\dot{S}$ and apply the disturbance bound. $$ S\,\dot{S} = -K\,S\,\text{sgn}(S) + S\,d(t) = -K\,\vert S \vert + S\,d(t) $$ $$ S\,\dot{S} \leq -K\,\vert S \vert + \vert S \vert \cdot \vert d(t) \vert \leq -(K - D)\,\vert S \vert $$ **Step 4:** Impose the reaching condition $S\,\dot{S} \leq -\eta \vert S \vert$. $$ K - D \geq \eta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K \geq D + \eta $$ With $D = 2.0$ and $\eta = 1.0$: $$ \boxed{K_{\min} = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0} $$ **Step 5:** Reaching time bound. $$ t_r \leq \frac{\vert S(x(0)) \vert}{\eta} = \frac{\vert 3\,x_1(0) + x_2(0) \vert}{1.0} $$ **Step 6:** Final control law. $$ \boxed{u(x,t) = \frac{2(x_2 - x_1^2)}{7} - \frac{3.0}{7}\,\text{sgn}(3\,x_1 + x_2)} $$ $\blacksquare$ --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question — Choice B]** ### Question B — Boundary Layer & Chattering Mitigation (14 Marks) For the plant in Question A, the ideal SMC law produces high-frequency **chattering**. The boundary-layer method replaces $\text{sgn}(S)$ by a saturation function: $$ \text{sat}\!\left(\frac{S}{\varepsilon}\right) = \begin{cases} +1, & S \geq \varepsilon \\ S/\varepsilon, & \vert S \vert < \varepsilon \\ -1, & S \leq -\varepsilon \end{cases} $$ **(a)** With boundary layer thickness $\varepsilon = 0.5$ and switching gain $K = 5$, derive the modified control law and find the steady-state tracking error bound. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss three practical advantages and two disadvantages of SMC compared to PID control in multivariable systems. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Modified SMC with Boundary Layer (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Modified control law. $$ u(x,t) = \frac{2(x_2 - x_1^2)}{7} - \frac{K}{7}\,\text{sat}\!\left(\frac{S}{\varepsilon}\right) $$ With $K = 5$, $\varepsilon = 0.5$: $$ u(x,t) = \frac{2(x_2 - x_1^2)}{7} - \frac{5}{7}\,\text{sat}(2S) $$ **Step 2:** Inside the boundary layer ($\vert S \vert < \varepsilon$): $$ \dot{S} = -K\cdot\frac{S}{\varepsilon} + d(t) = -\frac{5}{0.5}\,S + d(t) = -10\,S + d(t) $$ **Step 3:** At steady state ($\dot{S} = 0$): $$ S_{\text{ss}} = \frac{d(t)}{10} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert S_{\text{ss}} \vert \leq \frac{D}{K/\varepsilon} = \frac{D \varepsilon}{K} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{5} = 0.2 $$ **Step 4:** Convert to tracking error bound. $$ S = 3\,e + \dot{e}, \quad \text{so for constant disturbance} \;\; e_{\text{ss}} \approx S_{\text{ss}} / 3 $$ $$ \boxed{\vert e_{\text{ss}} \vert \leq \frac{D\,\varepsilon}{3K} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{3 \times 5} = 0.0667} $$ **Step 5:** Trade-off insight. > [!NOTE] > Smaller $\varepsilon$ → smaller error but more chattering; larger $K$ → smaller error but higher control effort. This trade-off is a classic Board question. #### Part (b) — Advantages & Disadvantages (7 Marks) **Advantages of SMC over PID in multivariable systems (any 3):** 1. **Inherent robustness** — invariant to matched disturbances once on $S=0$. 2. **Automatic decoupling in MIMO** — sliding surfaces $S_i$ independently govern each loop. 3. **Finite-time convergence** — guaranteed reaching time $t_r \leq \vert S(0) \vert / \eta$. 4. **No gain-scheduling required** — works across wide operating range. 5. **Simple structure** — easy to implement on microcontrollers and DSPs. **Disadvantages of SMC (any 2):** 1. **Chattering** — high-frequency oscillations damage actuators and excite unmodeled dynamics. 2. **High control effort** — discontinuous action demands actuators with wide bandwidth. 3. **Requires full state measurement** — observer design needed when only output is measurable. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Forgetting the Hurwitz check** — Students frequently skip verifying that the reduced-order polynomial is Hurwitz. *Always check the poles of the sliding dynamics.* > 2. **Confusing $u_{\text{eq}}$ and $u_{\text{sw}}$** — $u_{\text{eq}}$ makes $\dot{S}=0$; $u_{\text{sw}}$ enforces reachability. Mixing them is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction. > 3. **Missing the disturbance bound $D$** — when sizing $K$, never forget $K \geq D + \eta$. Several students lose 2 marks by setting $K = \eta$ only. > 4. **Skipping the time-bound derivation** — the reaching time $t_r \leq \vert S(0) \vert / \eta$ is a Board-favorite 1-mark bonus. > 5. **Sign error in $\text{sgn}(S)$ direction** — verify the sign convention matches $S\,\dot{S} \leq -\eta \vert S \vert$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Variable Structure Control** ### ✅ Core Definitions - [ ] Variable Structure System (VSS) = system with intentional structure-switching - [ ] Sliding Surface $S(x) = Cx = 0$ — geometric locus of desired reduced-order motion - [ ] Sliding Mode = constrained motion along $S = 0$ - [ ] Reaching Phase = trajectory approaches $S = 0$ - [ ] Equivalent Control $u_{\text{eq}}$ enforces $\dot{S} = 0$ (continuous part) - [ ] Switching Control $u_{\text{sw}} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S)$ enforces reachability ### ✅ Must-Memorize Equations - [ ] $S(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i$ with $c_n = 1$ - [ ] $u_{\text{eq}} = -b^{-1}(x)\left[f(x) + \sum c_i x_{i+1}\right]$ - [ ] $u_{\text{sw}} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S)\,/\,b(x)$ - [ ] Reachability: $S\,\dot{S} \leq -\eta \vert S \vert$ - [ ] Lyapunov: $V = \frac{1}{2}S^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \dot{V} = -K \vert S \vert$ - [ ] Reaching time: $t_r \leq \vert S(0) \vert / \eta$ - [ ] Switching gain bound: $K \geq D + \eta$ - [ ] Hurwitz condition on $c_{n-1}s^{n-1}+\dots+c_1 = 0$ ### ✅ Design Procedure (Step-by-Step) 1. Define sliding surface $S(x) = Cx$ and choose $C$ to make reduced-order polynomial Hurwitz 2. Compute $u_{\text{eq}}$ from $\dot{S} = 0$ 3. Add $u_{\text{sw}} = -K\,\text{sgn}(S)/b(x)$ with $K > D + \eta$ 4. Prove stability via Lyapunov $V = \frac{1}{2}S^2$ 5. (Optional) Replace $\text{sgn}(S)$ with $\text{sat}(S/\varepsilon)$ to suppress chattering 6. Verify steady-state error bound $\vert e_{ss} \vert \leq D\varepsilon/(K \cdot c_1)$ ### ✅ Common Pitfalls - Forgetting Hurwitz check (–2 marks) - Wrong sign of $\text{sgn}(S)$ (–1 mark) - Omitting disturbance bound $D$ when computing $K$ (–2 marks) - Drawing phase portrait without labeling the three phases (–1 mark) ### ✅ Comparison Quick Reference | Property | Linear PID | Sliding Mode Control | |:---------|:-----------|:---------------------| | Robustness to disturbance | Moderate | **Excellent** (invariant) | | MIMO decoupling | Requires compensator | **Automatic** | | Chattering | Absent | **Present** (needs mitigation) | | Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate | | Steady-state error | Non-zero | Zero (ideal) / bounded (BL) |

topics.title: Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Feedback in Multivariable Systems

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of **Multivariable (MIMO — Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) Control Theory**, **Feedback** refers to the systematic interconnection of the plant dynamics with a compensator (controller) such that the control action $u(t)$ is generated as a function of measured or estimated states/outputs, closing the loop and altering the closed-loop dynamics, stability, disturbance rejection, and tracking characteristics.

For a continuous-time linear time-invariant (LTI) multivariable plant:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t), \quad y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$

where $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $u \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $y \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$, the three canonical feedback configurations employed in KTU Module 4 are:

| Configuration | Control Law | Loop Closed By |
|---|---|---|
| **State Feedback (SFB)** | $u(t) = -Kx(t) + r(t)$ | Full state vector |
| **Static Output Feedback (SOF)** | $u(t) = -Fy(t) + r(t)$ | Measured output vector |
| **Dynamic Output Feedback (DOF)** | $\dot{x}_c = A_c x_c + B_c y, \quad u = C_c x_c + D_c y$ | Compensator + output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Feedback in MIMO systems is fundamentally different from SISO. In SISO, a single gain $K$ shapes the closed-loop pole; in MIMO, the **gain matrix $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$** reshapes the entire system matrix $A - BK$, and pole-placement depends on the **controllability structure** of the pair $(A,B)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding hill road (the **plant**), with your eyes watching the road (the **sensor/measurement**). Your hands turn the steering wheel based on what you see (the **controller**).

- **No Feedback (Open-Loop):** You pre-program the steering angle for the entire trip without looking. Result → car goes off the cliff at the first turn.
- **Feedback (Closed-Loop):** You continuously *look*, compare where the car **is** vs where the lane **should be**, and adjust. The error drives your correction.

In a MIMO system:
- The **steering, accelerator, and brakes** are multiple inputs ($u_1, u_2, u_3$).
- The **position, speed, and yaw rate** are multiple outputs ($y_1, y_2, y_3$).
- The feedback matrix $K$ decides *how much* each measured variable influences *each* input — this **cross-coupling** is what makes multivariable feedback uniquely powerful (and uniquely challenging).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric — Bandwidth:** A key KTU-design quantity for feedback loops is the **closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_{BW}$** (in **rad/s**), defined as the frequency at which the loop gain magnitude crosses **0 dB**. Higher bandwidth ⇒ faster response but greater noise sensitivity and reduced robustness margins.

---

## 1.3 Why Feedback? — The Five Pillars

1. **Stabilization** of unstable open-loop poles.
2. **Pole-Placement** to shape transient response (rise time, overshoot, settling time).
3. **Disturbance Rejection** — feedback attenuates the effect of $w(t)$ on $y(t)$ by a factor proportional to the **loop gain $(1 + L(s))$** at low frequencies.
4. **Tracking** — drives steady-state error to zero for reference inputs through integral action.
5. **Robustness** against plant parameter variations (gain/phase margins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Feedback reduces sensitivity to plant parameter variation at the cost of **sensor noise amplification** at high frequencies. This is the fundamental **waterbed effect** in robust control (Bode's integral theorem).

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (CO) Mapping

| CO Code | Outcome Description | Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Design state feedback and observer-based output feedback controllers for MIMO LTI systems. | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO5** | Analyze closed-loop stability using Lyapunov and eigenvalue techniques. | Analyze / Evaluate |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of Feedback Interconnection

Consider two LTI systems $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ in standard state-space form, interconnected in a **feedback loop**:

$$\Sigma_1 : \quad \dot{x}_1 = A_1 x_1 + B_1 u_1, \quad y_1 = C_1 x_1 + D_1 u_1$$

$$\Sigma_2 : \quad \dot{x}_2 = A_2 x_2 + B_2 u_2, \quad y_2 = C_2 x_2 + D_2 u_2$$

The feedback interconnection constraints are:

$$u_1 = r_1 - y_2, \quad u_2 = r_2 + y_1$$

The closed-loop augmented state $x_{cl} = [x_1^T \; x_2^T]^T$ evolves as:

$$\dot{x}_{cl} = A_{cl}\, x_{cl} + B_{cl}\, r, \quad y_{cl} = C_{cl}\, x_{cl} + D_{cl}\, r$$

where $r = [r_1^T \; r_2^T]^T$, and the **closed-loop system matrix** is:

$$
A_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix} A_1 - B_1 D_2 C_1 & -B_1 C_2 \\ B_2 C_1 & A_2 - B_2 D_1 C_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The transfer function from $r$ to $y_{cl}$ follows the **Redheffer (Star Product) Star-Product** formula:

$$G_{cl}(s) = G_1(s) \star G_2(s) = G_1(s) + G_1(s)(I - G_2(s) G_1(s))^{-1} G_2(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When $D_1 = 0$ and $D_2 = 0$, the closed-loop matrix simplifies to a **lower block triangular** structure, which decouples the eigenvalue computation: $\sigma(A_{cl}) = \sigma(A_1) \cup \sigma(A_2)$. This is the **separation principle** in its basic form.

---

## 2.2 State Feedback Controller Design

Given the plant $\Sigma = (A, B, C, D)$, apply:

$$u(t) = -K x(t) + N r(t)$$

where $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **state feedback gain matrix** and $N$ is the **feed-forward scaling matrix** for reference tracking. The closed-loop dynamics become:

$$\dot{x}(t) = (A - BK)\, x(t) + BN\, r(t)$$

The new characteristic polynomial is:

$$\det(sI - (A - BK)) = 0$$

### 2.2.1 Pole-Placement via Ackermann's Formula

For a **single-input system** ($m=1$), if the pair $(A, b)$ is **controllable**, the gain $K$ that places the closed-loop poles at desired locations $\{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\}$ is:

$$K = [0 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 1] \, \mathcal{C}^{-1}(A, b)\, \alpha_c(A)$$

where:

- $\mathcal{C}(A, b) = [b \;\; Ab \;\; A^2 b \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1}b]$ is the **controllability matrix**.
- $\alpha_c(s) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 s + \alpha_0$ is the **desired characteristic polynomial** with $\alpha_i$ chosen to match the prescribed poles.

For multi-input systems ($m > 1$), **Bass-Gura** or **LQR-based** algorithms are preferred.

---

## 2.3 Observer-Based Dynamic Output Feedback

When only $y(t)$ is measurable (not $x(t)$), a **Luenberger observer** reconstructs the state:

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = A\hat{x}(t) + Bu(t) + L(y(t) - C\hat{x}(t))$$

The **estimation error** $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$ satisfies:

$$\dot{e}(t) = (A - LC)\, e(t)$$

Choosing $L$ such that $\sigma(A - LC) = \{\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_n\}$ (the **observer poles**) makes $e(t) \to 0$ exponentially.

The **observer-based controller** combines state feedback with state estimation:

$$u(t) = -K\hat{x}(t)$$

The overall closed-loop state is $[x^T, e^T]^T$, governed by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{e} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A - BK & BK \\ 0 & A - LC \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ e \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} BN \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} r
$$

The characteristic polynomial **decouples**:

$$\det(sI - A_{cl}) = \det(sI - (A-BK)) \cdot \det(sI - (A-LC))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Separation Principle:** $K$ and $L$ can be designed **independently**. This is the cornerstone result in KTU Module 4 — designers use $K$ for desired plant dynamics and $L$ for desired estimator dynamics.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Feedback Law | $u = -Kx + Nr$ | Pole-placement, stabilization |
| 2 | Closed-Loop Matrix | $A_{cl} = A - BK$ | Stability via eigenvalues |
| 3 | Ackermann's Formula (SISO) | $K = e_n^T \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_c(A)$ | Direct computation of gain |
| 4 | Controllability Matrix | $\mathcal{C} = [B \; AB \; A^2B \; \cdots \; A^{n-1}B]$ | Verifies pole-placement feasibility |
| 5 | Luenberger Observer | $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$ | State estimation from output |
| 6 | Observer Error Dynamics | $\dot{e} = (A - LC) e$ | Eigenvalues $\sigma(A-LC)$ |
| 7 | Separation Principle | $\sigma(A_{cl}) = \sigma(A-BK) \cup \sigma(A-LC)$ | Independent $K$, $L$ design |
| 8 | Lyapunov for Stability | $A_{cl}^T P + P A_{cl} = -Q, \; Q \succ 0$ | Sufficient stability test |
| 9 | LQR Cost | $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\, dt$ | Optimal $K = R^{-1} B^T P$ |
| 10 | Algebraic Riccati Eq. (ARE) | $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$ | Solves the LQR problem |
| 11 | Internal Model Principle | Controller must contain model of exosystem $A_w$ | Zero steady-state error |
| 12 | Loop Transfer Recovery (LTR) | $\lim_{\rho \to \infty} G_{K}(s) G(s) = s^{-1} I$ | Robustness restoration |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** The observability matrix for dual design is $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \; A^T C^T \; \cdots \; (A^{n-1})^T C^T]^T$. When designing $L$ via Ackermann on the dual system, students often forget to transpose the result!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Aircraft Autopilots:** Multivariable state feedback stabilizes pitch, roll, and yaw simultaneously.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Joint torques are computed via $u = -K x$ where $x = [\theta, \dot{\theta}]^T$ across multiple joints.
- **Process Control (Distillation Columns):** MIMO feedback decouples top/bottom composition loops.
- **Power Systems:** Governor + exciter feedback maintains generator voltage and frequency stability.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Eigenvalues Under State Feedback

### Statement
For plant $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ and control law $u = -Kx$, derive the closed-loop dynamics matrix and prove that pole-placement is possible **iff** $(A,B)$ is controllable.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Substitute the feedback law into the plant equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= A x(t) + B u(t) \\
           &= A x(t) + B (-K x(t)) \\
           &= A x(t) - B K x(t) \\
           &= (A - B K)\, x(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Laplace transform assuming zero initial conditions ($x(0) = 0$):

$$
sX(s) = (A - BK)\, X(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad (sI - (A - BK))\, X(s) = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Non-trivial solution exists when the determinant vanishes (characteristic equation):

$$
\det(sI - (A - BK)) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Controllability Connection:** Using the **PBH (Popov–Belevitch–Hautus) test**, a complex number $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ is an eigenvalue of $A - BK$ iff $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \;\; B] = n$.

If $(A,B)$ is controllable, then $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \;\; B] = n$ holds for every $\lambda$. By PBH, we can always find a $K$ that shifts eigenvalues to any desired $\{\lambda_d\}$.

**Step 5 — Algorithm (Ackermann, SISO):**

For plant $(A, b)$ controllable with $m = 1$:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1}(A, b) \cdot \alpha_c(A)
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}(A, b) &= \begin{bmatrix} b & Ab & A^2 b & \cdots & A^{n-1} b \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \\
\alpha_c(s) &= (s - \lambda_1)(s - \lambda_2)\cdots(s - \lambda_n) \\
            &= s^n + \alpha_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $\alpha_c(A)$ is obtained by substituting $s = A$ into the scalar polynomial $\alpha_c(s)$, using Cayley–Hamilton's theorem.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Observer Design via Dual System

### Statement
Derive the Luenberger observer gain $L$ such that $\sigma(A - LC) = \{\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_n\}$, assuming $(C, A)$ is observable.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the observer error $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$.

Differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}(t) &= \dot{x}(t) - \dot{\hat{x}}(t) \\
          &= (Ax + Bu) - (A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})) \\
          &= A(x - \hat{x}) - L(Cx - C\hat{x}) \\
          &= (A - LC)\, e(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply PBH duality. The pair $(A^T, C^T)$ is **controllable** iff $(C, A)$ is observable.

**Step 3:** Use Ackermann on the dual system to find $L^T$:

$$
L^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1}(A^T, C^T) \cdot \alpha_o(A^T)
$$

**Step 4:** Transpose to obtain $L$:

$$
L = \left( \mathcal{C}^{-1}(A^T, C^T) \cdot \alpha_o(A^T) \right)^T \cdot e_n
$$

where $\alpha_o(s) = \prod_{i=1}^n (s - \mu_i)$ defines the desired observer poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Seborg / KTU recommended):** Observer poles are placed **2 to 5 times faster** than controller poles. This ensures the estimation error decays much faster than the plant dynamics, preserving closed-loop performance.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Lyapunov Stability

### Statement
Prove that if $u = -Kx$ and there exists $P = P^T \succ 0$ satisfying $A_{cl}^T P + P A_{cl} \prec 0$, where $A_{cl} = A - BK$, then the origin $x = 0$ is **asymptotically stable**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the Lyapunov candidate function:

$$
V(x) = x^T P x, \quad P = P^T \succ 0
$$

Since $P \succ 0$, we have $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate along the closed-loop trajectory $\dot{x} = (A - BK)x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}(x) &= \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x} \\
           &= x^T (A-BK)^T P x + x^T P (A-BK) x \\
           &= x^T \left[ (A-BK)^T P + P(A-BK) \right] x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Set $Q = -\left[(A-BK)^T P + P(A-BK)\right]$. The condition $\dot{V}(x) < 0 \; \forall x \neq 0$ is equivalent to:

$$
(A-BK)^T P + P(A-BK) = -Q, \quad Q \succ 0
$$

**Step 4:** Choose any $Q \succ 0$ (e.g., $Q = I$). The above equation is a **Sylvester / Lyapunov equation** in $P$. A unique solution $P \succ 0$ exists **iff** $\text{Re}(\lambda_i(A-BK)) < 0$ for all eigenvalues, i.e., the closed-loop system is **Hurwitz**.

**Step 5:** Conclusion — by Lyapunov's direct method, $x = 0$ is **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For **multi-input** systems, the ARE-based LQR gain $K = R^{-1} B^T P$ always yields a stabilizing controller provided $Q \succeq 0$ with $(A, \sqrt{Q})$ detectable, and $R \succ 0$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Feedback Methods

| Property | State Feedback | Static Output Feedback | Observer-Based DOF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensors Required** | All states | Outputs only | Outputs only |
| **Design Degrees of Freedom** | $m \times n$ | $m \times p$ | $K$ + $L$ |
| **Pole-Placement Guarantee** | Yes (if controllable) | Not always possible | Yes (via observer poles) |
| **Stability Test** | $\sigma(A-BK) \subset \mathbb{C}_-$ | Bilinear matrix inequality (BMI) | $\sigma(A-BK) \cup \sigma(A-LC)$ |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | NP-hard (non-convex) | Medium |
| **Implementation** | Often infeasible | Easy | Practical |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — Pole-Placement

**Plant:** $\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u$, $y = [1 \; 0] x$.

**Desired poles:** $\lambda_{1,2} = -3 \pm j2$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute controllability matrix:

$$
\mathcal{C} = [b \; Ab] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}) = -1 \neq 0
$$

System is controllable. ✓

**Step 2:** Desired characteristic polynomial:

$$
\alpha_c(s) = (s + 3 - j2)(s + 3 + j2) = s^2 + 6s + 13
$$

So $\alpha_1 = 6$, $\alpha_0 = 13$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\alpha_c(A) = A^2 + 6A + 13I$:

$$
A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 6A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 6 \\ 12 & -6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 2+13 & -1+6 \\ -1+12 & 3-6+13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 5 \\ 11 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$:

$$
\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply Ackermann:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 5 \\ 11 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification:** $A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2-15 & -1-5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -13 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$.

Characteristic polynomial: $s^2 + 6s + 13$ ✓ — matches the desired poles.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multivariable State Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[Reference Vector r] --> sumJ[ Summing Junction ]
    sumJ -- e = r - y --> Kfb[Feedback Gain Matrix K]
    Kfb -- u = -Kx_hat + Nr --> plant[Multivariable Plant Sigma A B C D]
    plant -- y --> sumJ
    plant -- x_true --> obsIn[Actual State x t]
    obsIn -.-> sumI[ Observer ]
    sumI -- L --> plant
    plant --> meas[Output Measurement y t]
    meas -- C --> sumI
    obsIn -.-> estK[ Estimated State x_hat ]
    estK --> Kfb
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Observer-Based Closed-Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Reference Input r t] --> nodeB[Pre-filter N]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Comparator: e equals r minus y]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Controller K x_hat t]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Actuator Plant Input u t]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Plant Dynamics A B]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Plant Output y t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Sensor Measurement]
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeI[True State x t]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Observer Estimator A L C]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Estimated State x_hat t]
    nodeK --> nodeD
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Diagram — Closed-Loop Transfer Matrix Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refA[Input Channel 1] --> sumA[Sum Node]
    refA2[Input Channel 2] --> sumA2[Sum Node]
    sumA --> gainA[Gain Matrix K 2x2]
    sumA2 --> gainA
    gainA --> dynA[Plant G s 2x2]
    dynA --> outA[Output Channel 1]
    dynA --> outA2[Output Channel 2]
    outA --> fbA[Feedback Path F 2x2]
    outA2 --> fbA
    fbA --> sumA
    fbA --> sumA2
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Separation Principle Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1[Step 1: Verify Controllability rank C equals n] --> nodeStep2[Step 2: Compute Feedback Gain K via Ackermann or LQR]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Step 3: Verify Observability rank O equals n]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Step 4: Compute Observer Gain L via Dual Ackermann]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 5: Place Observer Poles 3 to 5 times faster than Controller Poles]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6[Step 6: Assemble Closed-Loop A_cl equals Block Diag A-BK and A-LC]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7[Step 7: Validate via Simulation and Lyapunov Test]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Feedback Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1{States Measurable?} -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Plant Controllable?}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ3{Plant Observable?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeAns1[Use State Feedback: u equals -Kx]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeAns2[Use Static Output Feedback or Cascade Compensation]
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeAns3[Use Observer-Based Dynamic Output Feedback]
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeAns4[Use Reduced-Order Observer or Kalman Filter]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the state feedback control law for an LTI multivariable system. State the necessary and sufficient condition for arbitrary pole-placement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

State feedback is a control law of the form:

$$u(t) = -Kx(t) + Nr(t)$$

where $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **state feedback gain matrix** and $N$ is the reference pre-filter. Substituting into $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ gives the closed-loop system matrix $A_{cl} = A - BK$.

**Necessary and sufficient condition:** The pair $(A, B)$ must be **completely controllable**, i.e., the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^2 B \; \cdots \; A^{n-1}B]$ has full row rank $n$.

Equivalently (PBH test): $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \;\; B] = n$ for every $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Separation Principle in observer-based feedback design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Separation Principle states that for an LTI system $(A, B, C)$ with controller $u = -K\hat{x}$ and Luenberger observer $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$, the closed-loop characteristic polynomial **factors** as:

$$\det(sI - A_{cl}) = \det(sI - (A-BK)) \cdot \det(sI - (A-LC))$$

This means the controller gain $K$ and observer gain $L$ can be designed **independently**. The controller poles shape the regulation/transient response, while the observer poles (typically 3–5× faster) shape the estimation error decay. Stability requires both $(A-BK)$ and $(A-LC)$ to be Hurwitz.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Given the multivariable plant:

$$
\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = [1 \; 1]\, x
$$

Design a state feedback gain $K$ to place the closed-loop poles at $\{-2, -5\}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a Luenberger observer with observer poles at $\{-10, -10\}$ and explain how the Separation Principle simplifies the closed-loop stability analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Controllability Check**

$$
\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}) = -6 \neq 0
$$

Controllable ✓.

**Step 2: Desired Characteristic Polynomial**

$$
\alpha_c(s) = (s + 2)(s + 5) = s^2 + 7s + 10
$$

So $\alpha_1 = 7$, $\alpha_0 = 10$.

**Step 3: Compute $\alpha_c(A)$**

$$
A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -4 \\ 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 7A = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 14 \\ 0 & -21 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 10I = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1+7+10 & -4+14+0 \\ 0+0+0 & 9-21+10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 10 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Inverse of $\mathcal{C}$**

$$
\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-6}\begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1/6 & -1/6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Ackermann's Formula**

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 1/6 & -1/6 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 10 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ -3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(sI - (A-BK)) = (s+2)(s+5)$ ✓.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Observer Form**

The Luenberger observer is:

$$\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$$

**Step 2: Dual System for Ackermann**

The pair $(C, A) = ([1 \; 1], \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix})$ must be observable.

$$
\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{O}) = -2 \neq 0
$$

Observable ✓.

**Step 3: Dual Controllability Matrix**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{dual} = \mathcal{O}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Note: $\mathcal{O} = \mathcal{O}^T$ here (symmetric). $\mathcal{C}_{dual}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 4: Desired Observer Polynomial**

$$
\alpha_o(s) = (s + 10)^2 = s^2 + 20s + 100
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\alpha_o(A^T)$**

$A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$. Using Cayley-Hamilton:

$$
(A^T)^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -4 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 20A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 40 & -60 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 100I = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 \\ 0 & 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\alpha_o(A^T) = \begin{bmatrix} 121 & 0 \\ 36 & 49 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Ackermann on Dual**

$$
L^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}_{dual}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_o(A^T) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 121 & 0 \\ 36 & 49 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 42.5 & -24.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7: Transpose**

$$
L = \begin{bmatrix} 42.5 \\ -24.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8: Separation Principle Explanation**

The closed-loop matrix using $u = -K\hat{x}$ is:

$$
A_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix} A - BK & BK \\ 0 & A - LC \end{bmatrix}
$$

With $K = [3 \; 2]$ and $L = [42.5, -24.5]^T$:

$$
A_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\ -3 & -5 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & -10 & -10 \\ 0 & 0 & 42.5 & -24.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The block-triangular structure means $\sigma(A_{cl}) = \{-2, -5\} \cup \{-10, -10\}$. All eigenvalues have negative real parts ⇒ closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable**. The design of $K$ and $L$ is decoupled.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Consider the system with output feedback $u = -Fy$. Discuss the conditions under which static output feedback can achieve arbitrary pole-placement. Why is this generally not guaranteed in MIMO systems? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) problem for the plant $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ with cost $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\, dt$. State the Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE) and derive the optimal feedback law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

For output feedback $u = -Fy$, $y = Cx$, the closed-loop matrix is:

$$A_{cl} = A - BFC$$

**Pole-placement feasibility:** With $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}$, we have $mn$ design parameters vs $n$ poles to place. **Generic pole-placement is possible only if** $m \cdot p \geq n$ and the plant satisfies certain structural conditions.

**Limitations in MIMO:**

1. **Fixed output structure:** The "feedback direction" is constrained by $C$. If certain modes are unobservable in the chosen output channels, those modes cannot be moved.
2. **Non-convex design:** The equation $\sigma(A - BFC) = \{\lambda_d\}$ translates to a **bilinear matrix inequality (BMI)** in $F$, which is NP-hard in general.
3. **Sufficient condition (PBH):** A desired pole set $\{\lambda_d\}$ is achievable via static output feedback iff there exists $F$ such that $\text{rank}[\lambda_i I - A \;\; B] = n$ AND the right eigenvectors lie in the column space of $[B \; A]$ restricted by $C$.

In **SISO** systems, $F$ is a scalar and pole-placement via output feedback reduces to $u = -k y = -k c^T x$, which cannot place $n$ poles with a single gain $k$ (only one degree of freedom). Hence **state feedback or dynamic compensation is required** for full pole-placement in SISO/MIMO alike.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Hamiltonian Formulation**

Minimize $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\, dt$ subject to $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, with $Q \succeq 0$, $R \succ 0$.

Define the Hamiltonian:

$$
\mathcal{H} = x^T Q x + u^T R u + \lambda^T (Ax + Bu)
$$

**Step 2: Stationarity Condition $\partial \mathcal{H} / \partial u = 0$:**

$$
2Ru + B^T \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = -\frac{1}{2} R^{-1} B^T \lambda
$$

**Step 3: Costate Equation** $\dot{\lambda} = -\partial \mathcal{H}/\partial x$:

$$
\dot{\lambda} = -Qx - A^T \lambda
$$

**Step 4: Guess the costate as $\lambda = 2Px$** (where $P$ is symmetric). Substituting:

$$
2P\dot{x} = -Qx - 2A^T P x
$$

Using $\dot{x} = (A - BR^{-1}B^T P) x$:

$$
2P(A - BR^{-1}B^T P)x = -Qx - 2A^T P x
$$

Equating coefficients (and dividing by 2):

$$
PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + A^T P + Q = 0
$$

This is the **Continuous Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE)**:

$$\boxed{A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0}$$

**Step 5: Optimal Feedback Law**

Since $\lambda = 2Px$ and $u = -\frac{1}{2}R^{-1}B^T \lambda$:

$$\boxed{u^*(t) = -K x(t), \quad \text{where } K = R^{-1} B^T P}$$

**Step 6: Existence Conditions (KTU Exam Favorite):**

- $(A, B)$ stabilizable,
- $(A, \sqrt{Q})$ detectable,
- $R \succ 0$, $Q \succeq 0$.

Then the unique positive semi-definite solution $P \succeq 0$ of CARE exists, and $A - BK$ is Hurwitz.

**Step 7: Closed-Loop Optimal Cost**

The minimum cost is $J^* = x(0)^T P x(0)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of **2** in the costate ansatz $\lambda = 2Px$, leading to an ARE with wrong coefficients. The factor **2** cancels in the stationarity equation, but its correct inclusion is required for a rigorous derivation. Marks deducted: **2/7** for this slip.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU 2024 ESE for Feedback Questions:**
> 1. **Controllability not verified** before applying Ackermann — losing **2 marks** (Step 1 is mandatory).
> 2. **Confusion between observer gain $L$ and controller gain $K$** when transposing from dual Ackermann — verify $L \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the reference pre-filter $N$** in tracking problems — the closed-loop system will have non-zero steady-state error for step references without $N = -(C(A-BK)^{-1}B)^{-1}$.
> 4. **Mixing up observability $\mathcal{O}$ vs controllability $\mathcal{C}$ formulas** — observability is the **transpose** of the dual controllability matrix.
> 5. **Not stating the Separation Principle explicitly** in observer design — the KTU rubric allocates **2 marks** for stating "Controller and observer dynamics are decoupled" verbatim.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State Feedback Law:** $u = -Kx + Nr$; closed-loop matrix $A - BK$.
- **Ackermann's Formula (SISO):** $K = e_n^T \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_c(A)$; requires $(A,B)$ controllable.
- **Luenberger Observer:** $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$; error dynamics $A - LC$.
- **Separation Principle:** $\sigma(A_{cl}) = \sigma(A-BK) \cup \sigma(A-LC)$ — independent $K$, $L$ design.
- **Lyapunov Test:** $(A-BK)^T P + P(A-BK) = -Q$, $P \succ 0$, $Q \succ 0$ ⇔ closed-loop stability.
- **LQR Optimal Gain:** $K = R^{-1} B^T P$ where $P$ solves the ARE: $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$.
- **Observer Pole Placement Rule:** Place observer poles **3–5× faster** than controller poles.
- **Static Output Feedback:** Generally **not sufficient** for arbitrary pole-placement in MIMO (BMI problem).
- **Internal Model Principle:** For zero steady-state error to step/sinusoidal references, embed the exosystem model in the controller.
- **PBH Test (Must Memorize):** Controllability ⇔ $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \; B] = n$ for all $\lambda \in \sigma(A)$.
- **Lyapunov Function Form:** Always positive-definite, e.g., $V(x) = x^T P x$ with $P \succ 0$.
- **Reference Tracking:** Use pre-filter $N = -[C(A-BK)^{-1}B]^{-1}$ for unit DC gain.
- **Real-World Systems:** Aircraft autopilots, robotic arms, distillation columns, power grids — all use multivariable feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"For state feedback, check controllability first. For observer design, check observability first. For closed-loop stability, apply the Separation Principle and Lyapunov test together. Never skip rank verification."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Multivariable System:** A closed-loop MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) control system is one in which the plant $G(s)$ and the controller $K(s)$ are interconnected such that the outputs $y(s)$ track the references $r(s)$ through feedback, while accounting for the inherent cross-couplings between multiple input and output channels. The closed-loop transfer function matrix from $r$ to $y$ is given by the **MIMO Sensitivity Equation**.

For a standard unity feedback structure with plant $G(s) \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times m}$ and controller $K(s) \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times m}$, the **complementary sensitivity function** $T(s)$ and **sensitivity function** $S(s)$ are:

$$T(s) = [I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1} G(s) K(s)$$

$$S(s) = [I + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$

These two satisfy the **fundamental MIMO identity**:

$$S(s) + T(s) = I_m$$

where $I_m$ is the $m \times m$ identity matrix.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor:**
> Imagine an orchestra with $m$ musicians (outputs) and $m$ conductors (inputs). In an **open-loop** setup, each conductor follows a fixed score without listening to the music — if one musician lags, the harmony breaks. In a **closed-loop** multivariable system, the lead conductor (the controller $K$) listens to the entire orchestra's output (feedback) and adjusts *every* musician's cues in real time, **accounting for the fact that telling the violinist to play louder affects the flutist too** (cross-coupling). The "error signal" is the difference between the perfect score (reference $r$) and the actual music (output $y$), distributed across all instruments. The conductor must be smart enough to handle these couplings, which is what $G(s)K(s)$ matrix algebra captures.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standards

- **Loop Gain Matrix:** $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$ — a square matrix capturing all input–output channel interactions.
- **Return Difference Matrix:** $I + L(s)$ — governs stability via the **Generalized Nyquist Criterion**.
- **Characteristic Loci:** Eigenvalues $\lambda_i(s)$ of $L(s)$ — form $m$ separate Nyquist plots for MIMO analysis.
- **Closed-Loop Bandwidth:** Frequency $\omega_b$ where $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (the **-3 dB** crossover).
- **Disk Margin / Multiplicative Uncertainty Bound:** $l_m(\omega) = \bar{\sigma}[\Delta(j\omega)]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, closed-loop analysis for multivariable systems extends single-variable sensitivity/robustness concepts using **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** and the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** at the bandwidth frequency. These are high-yield topics for both ESE and module tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Closed-Loop MIMO System

Consider the standard **one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) unity-feedback** MIMO control structure:

1. **Reference vector** $r(s) \in \mathbb{C}^m$ enters the comparator.
2. **Tracking error** $e(s) = r(s) - y(s)$.
3. **Control action** $u(s) = K(s)e(s)$, where $K(s) \in \mathbb{RH}_\infty^{m \times m}$.
4. **Plant output** $y(s) = G(s)u(s) + d(s)$ (with disturbance $d$).
5. **Feedback path** sends $y$ back to the comparator (unity, sensor assumed ideal).

The closed-loop relationship $y(s) = T(s)r(s) + S(s)d(s)$ shows:
- $T(s)$: how well the system **tracks** references.
- $S(s)$: how well the system **rejects** disturbances and is **sensitive** to plant variations.

## 2.2 Why and How: The Rationale for Each Step

- **Why $S + T = I$?** Because every reference signal is either transmitted to the output (via $T$) or absorbed by the sensitivity function — a conservation law in linear systems.
- **Why use SVD?** Single-input single-output (SISO) Bode/Nyquist tools don't directly apply because $G(s)K(s)$ is a matrix, not a scalar. SVD provides direction-dependent gains (max/min) which bound performance.
- **How to check closed-loop stability?** The characteristic equation becomes $\det[I_m + G(s)K(s)] = 0$. The number of closed-loop RHP poles equals the number of encirclements of $-1$ by the **union of characteristic loci** of $L(s)$ — this is the **Generalized Nyquist Theorem**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Loop gain matrix | $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$ | $m \times m$ transfer function matrix |
| Sensitivity function | $S(s) = [I + L(s)]^{-1}$ | Disturbance rejection, sensitivity to plant variation |
| Complementary sensitivity | $T(s) = L(s)[I + L(s)]^{-1}$ | Reference tracking, complementary to $S$ |
| Identity | $S(s) + T(s) = I_m$ | Always holds in 1-DOF unity feedback |
| Load disturbance transfer | $y_d(s) = S(s)d(s)$ | $d$ is disturbance at plant output |
| Noise transfer | $y_n(s) = T(s)n(s)$ | $n$ is sensor noise entering at output |
| Control effort | $u(s) = K(s)S(s)r(s) - K(s)S(s)d(s)$ | Captures both tracking and regulation |
| Closed-loop poles | Roots of $\det[I + G(s)K(s)] = 0$ | Characteristic equation for MIMO |
| Generalized Nyquist (GNC) | $N = \sum_i N_i$, $Z = N + P_o$ | $N_i$: encirclements of locus $i$; $P_o$: open-loop RHP poles |
| Input multiplicative uncertainty | $G_p(s) = [I + \Delta_i(s)]G(s),\ \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_i) \leq l_{mi}$ | Robust stability condition |
| Output multiplicative uncertainty | $G_p(s) = G(s)[I + \Delta_o(s)],\ \bar{\sigma}(\Delta_o) \leq l_{mo}$ | Robust stability condition |
| RS condition (output mult.) | $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] < 1 / l_{mo}(\omega)\ \forall\ \omega$ | Sufficient (not necessary) |
| RS condition (input mult.) | $\bar{\sigma}[K(s)S(s)] < 1 / l_{mi}(\omega)\ \forall\ \omega$ | Sufficient condition |
| Nominal performance (NP) | $\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)] < 1 / w_p(\omega)$ | Performance weight $w_p$ |
| Robust performance (RP) | $\mu_{\Delta}[\ T\ \ \ w_p S\ ] < 1$ | Structured singular value $\mu$ test |
| RGA | $\Lambda(G) = G \otimes (G^{-1})^T$ | Elementwise (Hadamard) product |
| Closed-loop RGA property | $\Lambda(G) = \Lambda(GK) = \Lambda(T)\ [I - T]^{-1}$ for diagonal $K$ | Constant under diagonal loop shaping |
| Condition number | $\kappa(G) = \bar{\sigma}(G) / \underline{\sigma}(G)$ | $\kappa \geq 1$, ideal = 1 (no directionality) |
| Integral controllability | $G(0)$ nonsingular | Necessary for offset-free tracking of step references |
| Decentralized PI stabilizability (sufficient) | $g_{ii}(s)$ have no RHP zeros; off-diagonals satisfy bound | Morari–Zafiriou criterion |
| Closed-loop bandwidth | $\omega_{BW}:\ \bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega_{BW})] = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | 0 dB crossover of complementary sensitivity |
| Peak of $S$ (resonance) | $M_S = \max_\omega \bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)]$ | Typically $M_S \leq 2$ (1.0–1.5 for good performance) |
| Peak of $T$ | $M_T = \max_\omega \bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)]$ | Typically $M_T \leq 1.25$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units:** All singular values are dimensionless ratios of output to input. Weights $w_p, w_m$ are dimensionless frequency-dependent bounds.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Process Control:** Distillation columns have $2 \times 2$ MIMO dynamics with strong interaction. Closed-loop designs use decentralized PI loops informed by the RGA to minimize coupling.
- **Aircraft Flight Control:** Roll/pitch/yaw axes are cross-coupled. Modern fly-by-wire systems use MIMO $H_\infty$ controllers designed via closed-loop SVD shaping.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Joint dynamics couple through inertia matrix $M(q)$. Closed-loop computed-torque control uses feedback linearization to decouple, then SISO loops on each joint.
- **Power Systems:** Multi-machine stability uses closed-loop MIMO analysis for damping controllers (PODs, PSSs) across multiple generators.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function Matrix $T(s)$

Starting from the unity-feedback MIMO system:

$$u(s) = K(s) e(s)$$
$$y(s) = G(s) u(s)$$
$$e(s) = r(s) - y(s)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the expression for $e$ into the control law.

$$u(s) = K(s)[r(s) - y(s)]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u$ into the plant equation.

$$y(s) = G(s) K(s) [r(s) - y(s)]$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the matrix product $G(s)K(s)$ over the difference.

$$y(s) = G(s)K(s) r(s) - G(s)K(s) y(s)$$

**Step 4:** Collect terms with $y(s)$ on the left-hand side by adding $G(s)K(s)y(s)$ to both sides.

$$y(s) + G(s)K(s) y(s) = G(s)K(s) r(s)$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $y(s)$ on the left using the identity matrix $I_m$.

$$[I_m + G(s)K(s)]\, y(s) = G(s)K(s)\, r(s)$$

**Step 6:** Pre-multiply both sides by the inverse of $[I_m + G(s)K(s)]$ (assumed nonsingular).

$$y(s) = [I_m + G(s)K(s)]^{-1} G(s)K(s)\, r(s)$$

**Step 7:** Identify the closed-loop transfer function matrix $T(s)$ from the structure.

$$\boxed{T(s) = [I_m + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}\, G(s)K(s)}$$

**Step 8 (alternate form):** Right-multiply numerator and denominator by $K(s)^{-1}$ to obtain the equivalent form (valid when $K$ is square and invertible).

$$T(s) = G(s)K(s)\,[I_m + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the two forms?** When $L$ commutes with itself trivially (it always does with itself), $L(I + L)^{-1} = (I + L)^{-1}L$, so both expressions are equivalent for scalar $L$ and remain valid for matrices because $L$ commutes with $(I+L)^{-1}$ only if $L$ and $(I+L)^{-1}$ share eigenvectors. The two forms give the **same** $T$ because the closed-loop poles and the input/output mapping are unique; however, in numerical implementations, the first form $[I+L]^{-1}L$ is preferred when $\|L\| \gg 1$ (high loop gain) and the second when $\|L\| \ll 1$ (low loop gain), to avoid ill-conditioning.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Identity $S + T = I_m$

**Step 1:** Start with the definitions.

$$S = (I + L)^{-1},\quad T = (I + L)^{-1} L$$

**Step 2:** Add $S$ and $T$ using the common factor $(I + L)^{-1}$.

$$S + T = (I + L)^{-1} + (I + L)^{-1} L$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $(I + L)^{-1}$ on the right.

$$S + T = (I + L)^{-1}\,[I_m + L]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $(I + L)^{-1}(I + L) = I_m$.

$$\boxed{S(s) + T(s) = I_m}$$

**Engineering meaning:** At any frequency, the fraction of reference "leaking" past the controller plus the fraction "transmitted" through the closed loop equals one — an exact conservation law.

## 3.3 Derivation: Generalized Nyquist Stability Criterion

**Setup:** Let $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$ be open-loop stable with $m$ characteristic loci $\lambda_i(s)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, m$. Each locus is the eigenvalue of $L(s)$ traced as $s$ traverses the standard Nyquist $D$-contour in the $s$-plane.

**Statement:** The closed-loop system is **internally stable** if and only if the **net number of counter-clockwise encirclements** of the critical point $-1 + j0$ by the **union of all $m$ characteristic loci** equals the number of open-loop RHP poles $P_o$.

$$N = \sum_{i=1}^{m} N_i = -P_o$$

where $N_i$ is the encirclement count of locus $i$ (signed: positive CCW, negative CW), and we adopt the convention $N = -P_o$ for the standard Nyquist contour.

**Derivation Sketch:**

**Step 1:** The closed-loop characteristic polynomial is $\det[I + L(s)]$. The number of closed-loop RHP zeros of $\det[I+L]$ equals the number of closed-loop unstable poles.

**Step 2:** Apply the **argument principle** to $\det[I + L(s)]$ on the Nyquist $D$-contour:

$$N_{\det[I+L]} = Z - P_o$$

where $Z$ is the number of closed-loop RHP poles and $P_o$ is the number of open-loop RHP poles of $L(s)$.

**Step 3:** For a square matrix $L$, the eigenvalues of $I + L$ are $1 + \lambda_i$. Therefore the encirclements of $-1$ by the union of $\lambda_i(s)$ loci equals $N_{\det[I+L]}$.

**Step 4:** Equating:

$$Z = N_{\det[I+L]} + P_o = \sum_{i=1}^m N_i + P_o$$

**Step 5:** For **closed-loop stability** we require $Z = 0$, hence:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{m} N_i = -P_o}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Example: $2 \times 2$ Closed-Loop Pole Calculation

Consider:

$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{0.5}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{0.3}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+3} \end{bmatrix},\quad K = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & 0 \\ 0 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the loop gain $L = GK$ (diagonal $K$ scales columns).

$$L(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{k_1}{s+1} & \dfrac{0.5 k_2}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{0.3 k_1}{s+1} & \dfrac{k_2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $I + L(s)$:

$$I + L(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{k_1}{s+1} & \dfrac{0.5 k_2}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{0.3 k_1}{s+1} & 1 + \dfrac{k_2}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Characteristic equation is $\det[I + L(s)] = 0$.

**Step 4:** Compute the determinant as $\text{ad} - \text{bc}$ with common denominator $(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$:

$$\det[I+L] = \frac{\Big[(s+1) + k_1\Big]\Big[(s+2)(s+3) + 0.5 k_2(s+2)\Big] - (0.5 k_2)(s+3)(0.3 k_1)}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**Step 5:** Setting the numerator to zero yields the closed-loop characteristic polynomial. Substituting numerical values $k_1 = 2, k_2 = 1$ and expanding gives a 4th-order polynomial whose roots can be checked numerically (all in LHP ⇒ stable).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the **sensitivity function** $S(s)$ and the **complementary sensitivity function** $T(s)$ for a MIMO closed-loop system. State the fundamental identity that relates them.

**Model Answer:**

For a unity-feedback MIMO system with plant $G(s)$ and controller $K(s)$:

$$S(s) = [I_m + G(s)K(s)]^{-1}$$

$$T(s) = [I_m + G(s)K(s)]^{-1} G(s)K(s)$$

The fundamental identity is:

$$S(s) + T(s) = I_m$$

$S(s)$ governs **disturbance rejection** and sensitivity to plant variations, while $T(s)$ governs **reference tracking** and **sensor noise transmission**. **(3 marks — 1 mark each definition + 1 mark for identity with explanation.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is the **Generalized Nyquist Stability Criterion** for closed-loop MIMO systems? How does it differ from the classical SISO Nyquist criterion?

**Model Answer:**

The **Generalized Nyquist Criterion (GNC)** states that the closed-loop system is internally stable if and only if the **net sum of encirclements** of the critical point $-1 + j0$ by the union of all $m$ characteristic loci $\lambda_i(s)$ (eigenvalues of $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$) equals $-P_o$, where $P_o$ is the total number of open-loop RHP poles.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} N_i = -P_o$$

**Differences from SISO Nyquist:**
- SISO has **one** Nyquist plot of the scalar loop gain; MIMO has $m$ **characteristic loci** plotted simultaneously.
- The encirclements of $-1$ are counted as a **sum** across all loci.
- The GNC requires the **Gershgorin-like band check** to ensure encirclements are not cancelled by eigenvalue interaction in the vicinity of $-1$.

**(3 marks — 2 marks for statement + 1 mark for difference.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider a $2 \times 2$ MIMO plant with transfer function matrix:**

$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{2}{s+1} & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{s+1} & \dfrac{3}{s+3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**A decentralized PI controller** $K(s) = \text{diag}\left[k_1 + \dfrac{k_{i1}}{s},\ k_2 + \dfrac{k_{i2}}{s}\right]$ is implemented.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the closed-loop transfer function matrix $T(s)$ and write the closed-loop characteristic equation. Show the determinant explicitly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The loop gain matrix is $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$. Since $K$ is diagonal, the columns of $G$ are scaled by $k_j + k_{ij}/s$:

$$L(s) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{2(s + k_{i1}/k_1)}{s(s+1)} & \dfrac{(s + k_{i2}/k_2)}{s(s+2)} \\[6pt] \dfrac{(s + k_{i1}/k_1)}{s(s+1)} & \dfrac{3(s + k_{i2}/k_2)}{s(s+3)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Construct $I + L(s)$:

$$I + L(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{2(s + a_1)}{s(s+1)} & \dfrac{(s + a_2)}{s(s+2)} \\[6pt] \dfrac{(s + a_1)}{s(s+1)} & 1 + \dfrac{3(s + a_2)}{s(s+3)} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $a_j = k_{ij}/k_j$ (controller zero locations).

**Step 3:** The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = [I + L(s)]^{-1} L(s)$$

**Step 4:** The characteristic equation is $\det[I + L(s)] = 0$. Computing the determinant:

$$\det[I+L] = \left[1 + \frac{2(s+a_1)}{s(s+1)}\right]\left[1 + \frac{3(s+a_2)}{s(s+3)}\right] - \frac{(s+a_1)(s+a_2)}{s^2(s+1)(s+3)}$$

**Step 5:** Put over common denominator $s^2(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$:

$$\det[I+L] = \frac{\big[s(s+1) + 2(s+a_1)\big]\big[s(s+3) + 3(s+a_2)\big](s+2) - (s+a_1)(s+a_2)(s+2)}{(s+2)\,s^2(s+1)(s+3)}$$

Wait — redoing carefully. Each diagonal entry's bracket and off-diagonal have denominators $s(s+1)$, $s(s+2)$, $s(s+3)$. Common denominator of $\det[I+L]$ when written as a single fraction is $s^2(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$. 

Multiplying the first bracket: $\dfrac{s^2 + 3s + 2a_1}{s(s+1)} \cdot \dfrac{s^2 + 6s + 3a_2}{s(s+3)}$

Multiplying the second: $\dfrac{(s+a_1)(s+a_2)}{s^2(s+1)(s+3)}$

**Step 6:** Common denominator $s^2(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$:

$$\det[I+L] = \frac{(s^2 + 3s + 2a_1)(s^2 + 6s + 3a_2)(s+2) - (s+a_1)(s+a_2)(s+2)}{s^2(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$$

Setting numerator to zero yields the characteristic polynomial. **(7 marks — 2 marks for L(s) and I+L(s), 2 marks for T(s) expression, 3 marks for determinant expansion.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Using the RGA, determine whether decentralized control is suitable. Compute $\Lambda(G)$ at $s = 0$ and comment on the pairing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $G(0)$ by setting $s=0$:

$$G(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $G(0)^{-1}$. The determinant is $2 \cdot 1 - 0.5 \cdot 1 = 1.5$.

$$G(0)^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.5}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 & -1/3 \\ -2/3 & 4/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the RGA using $\Lambda = G \circ (G^{-1})^T$ (Hadamard product):

$$(G^{-1})^T = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 & -2/3 \\ -1/3 & 4/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Hadamard product $G \circ (G^{-1})^T$:

$$\Lambda(G(0)) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \cdot (2/3) & 0.5 \cdot (-2/3) \\ 1 \cdot (-1/3) & 1 \cdot (4/3) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & 4/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Verify rows sum to 1: $(4/3 - 1/3) = 1$ and $(-1/3 + 4/3) = 1$. ✓

**Step 6:** **Pairing recommendation:**
- $\lambda_{11} = 4/3 \approx 1.33$ — large positive, suitable for pairing $u_1 \to y_1$.
- $\lambda_{22} = 4/3$ — also large positive, suitable for $u_2 \to y_2$.
- Off-diagonal $\lambda_{12} = \lambda_{21} = -1/3$ — small in magnitude ⇒ weak cross-coupling under decentralized control.

**Conclusion:** Decentralized diagonal control **is suitable**, and the natural pairing is $(u_1, y_1)$ and $(u_2, y_2)$. However, since $\lambda_{11} > 1$, the loop interaction makes individual loops *more* sensitive to gain — caution is required with controller tuning. **(7 marks — 2 marks for G(0), 2 marks for inverse, 2 marks for RGA, 1 mark for pairing conclusion.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A multivariable plant has loop transfer function matrix $L(s) = G(s)K(s)$ with two characteristic loci $\lambda_1(s)$ and $\lambda_2(s)$. The open-loop system has $P_o = 0$ open-loop RHP poles.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch the characteristic loci $\lambda_1(j\omega)$ and $\lambda_2(j\omega)$ for $\omega \in [-\infty, +\infty]$ given the following data, and apply the Generalized Nyquist Criterion to determine closed-loop stability.**

- Locus 1: starts at $0.5 - j0.2$ at $\omega = 0^+$, crosses the real axis at $-1.5$ at $\omega = \omega_1$, returns to origin at $\omega = \infty$.
- Locus 2: starts at $0.3 + j0.1$ at $\omega = 0^+$, encircles $-1$ once in the **counter-clockwise** direction as $\omega$ goes from $0^+$ to $\infty$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For $P_o = 0$, the GNC requires $\sum N_i = 0$ for stability.

**Step 2:** Locus 1: It crosses the negative real axis at $-1.5$, which is to the **left** of $-1$. As $\omega$ goes from $0^+$ to $+\infty$, locus 1 goes from $0.5 - j0.2$ to $-1.5$ (real axis crossing) and then to $0$. The encirclement count $N_1$ of $-1$:

- The locus passes **to the left of $-1$** on the real axis. Considering the standard Nyquist contour, locus 1 contributes **$N_1 = 0$** encirclements of $-1$ (it does not encircle $-1$; it merely passes to the left).

**Step 3:** Locus 2: Encroaches near $-1$ and makes **one counter-clockwise** encirclement, so $N_2 = +1$.

**Step 4:** Total encirclements: $\sum N_i = 0 + 1 = 1$.

**Step 5:** Since $P_o = 0$, the closed-loop system is stable **if and only if** $\sum N_i = -P_o = 0$. We have $\sum N_i = 1 \neq 0$.

**Step 6:** **Conclusion:** Closed-loop system is **UNSTABLE** with $Z = N + P_o = 1 + 0 = 1$ closed-loop RHP pole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering remedy:** Either reduce the controller gain (pull locus 2 inward, away from $-1$) or add a **pre-compensator** $K_p$ to decouple/rotate the characteristic loci so that neither encircles $-1$.

**(7 marks — 2 marks for locus 1 analysis, 2 marks for locus 2 analysis, 2 marks for GNC computation, 1 mark for conclusion.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For the same plant, the closed-loop complementary sensitivity $T(s)$ has maximum singular value peak $M_T = 2.5$ at $\omega = 10$ rad/s. The multiplicative output uncertainty satisfies $l_{mo}(\omega) = 0.5$ at $\omega = 10$ rad/s. Determine whether the system is robustly stable. If not, what design modification would you recommend?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Robust stability condition for output multiplicative uncertainty:

$$\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] < \frac{1}{l_{mo}(\omega)}\quad \forall\omega$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the bound at $\omega = 10$ rad/s:

$$\frac{1}{l_{mo}(10)} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the left-hand side at $\omega = 10$ rad/s:

$$\bar{\sigma}[T(j10)] \approx M_T = 2.5$$

(Note: $M_T$ is defined as the peak over all frequencies, so $\bar{\sigma}[T(j10)] \leq 2.5$.)

**Step 4:** Compare:

$$\bar{\sigma}[T(j10)] = 2.5 > 2.0 = \frac{1}{l_{mo}(10)}$$

**Step 5:** **Conclusion:** The **Robust Stability condition is VIOLATED** at $\omega = 10$ rad/s. The system is **NOT robustly stable**.

**Step 6:** **Design recommendations:**
- **Reduce loop gain** at $\omega = 10$ rad/s (roll off $K$ faster) to lower $M_T$ below 2.0.
- **Add a lead-lag compensator** to reshape $L(s)$ so the peak of $\bar{\sigma}[T]$ is reduced.
- **Apply $H_\infty$ mixed-sensitivity synthesis** to explicitly trade off tracking vs. robustness.
- **Verify using $\mu$-analysis** for necessary and sufficient robust stability check (the SVD bound is sufficient but conservative).

**(7 marks — 2 marks for RS condition statement, 2 marks for numerical evaluation, 1 mark for violation conclusion, 2 marks for design modifications.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Closed-Loop MIMO Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $S$ and $T$ definitions:** Students often write $S = (I+GK)^{-1}GK$ — this is **wrong**; $T$ has this form, $S$ does not. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $I_m$ in $\det[I+L]$:** Writing $\det[GK]$ instead of $\det[I+GK]$ for the characteristic equation. The $I$ is the critical identity matrix. Loss: 2 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention in GNC:** Some texts use $N = -P_o$ and others $N = P_o$. Stick with the convention **$N = -P_o$** as stated in the derivation above. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 4. **RGA computation errors:** Forgetting to take the **transpose** of $G^{-1}$ before the Hadamard product. Loss: 2 marks.
> 5. **Robust stability check using the wrong inequality direction:** The condition is $\bar{\sigma}[T] < 1/l_{mo}$, not $>$. Loss: 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the encirclements of characteristic loci that *pass through* $-1$:** A locus touching $-1$ counts as a marginal stability case (closed-loop pole on imaginary axis). Loss: 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Closed-Loop Multivariable Systems:**

- **Two transfer matrices govern everything:** $S = (I + L)^{-1}$ (sensitivity) and $T = L(I + L)^{-1}$ (complementary sensitivity), with **$S + T = I_m$**.
- **Closed-loop poles** are the roots of $\det[I + G(s)K(s)] = 0$.
- **Stability check:** Generalized Nyquist Criterion — sum of encirclements of $-1$ by all characteristic loci of $L(s)$ must equal $-P_o$.
- **Performance:** $\bar{\sigma}[S(j\omega)] < 1/w_p(\omega)$ for nominal performance.
- **Robust Stability (output mult. unc.):** $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] < 1/l_{mo}(\omega)$.
- **Robust Stability (input mult. unc.):** $\bar{\sigma}[KS(j\omega)] < 1/l_{mi}(\omega)$.
- **Robust Performance:** $\mu$-test on the upper LFT structure with both performance weights and uncertainty blocks.
- **RGA** = $G \circ (G^{-1})^T$ (Hadamard / elementwise product). $\Lambda$ is **invariant** under constant diagonal loop shaping: $\Lambda(G) = \Lambda(GK) = \Lambda(T[I-T]^{-1})$.
- **Decentralized pairing rule:** Pair inputs and outputs corresponding to RGA elements closest to 1 (positive) and avoid pairings with negative RGA elements near the bandwidth.
- **Integral controllability:** $G(0)$ must be nonsingular for offset-free step tracking; sufficient condition is $\text{Re}[\Lambda(G(0))] > 0$ elementwise (Skogestad).
- **Condition number** $\kappa(G) = \bar\sigma/\underline\sigma$: high $\kappa$ means strong directionality, which is hard to control.
- **Bandwidth:** $\omega_{BW}$ is the frequency where $\bar{\sigma}[T(j\omega)] = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Design targets:** $M_S \leq 1.5$–$2$ and $M_T \leq 1.25$ give a good balance of speed, robustness, and noise rejection.
- **Singular Value Loop Shaping:** Make $\underline{\sigma}[L]$ large for performance and small $\bar{\sigma}[T]$ for robustness.
- **SISO extensions of Bode/Nyquist fail** for MIMO — always use SVD-based or eigenvalue-based analysis.
- **Closed-loop simulation** must include **all** channels with proper cross-coupling; testing only diagonal channels is a common student error.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT746 - MULTIVARIABLE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Interaction in MIMO Systems

In Multivariable Control Theory, **Interaction** is a fundamental phenomenon observed in Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems wherein a manipulated input variable affects more than one controlled output variable simultaneously. In an ideal decoupled (or non-interacting) system, each input would exclusively control only one designated output. The degree to which a system deviates from this ideal behavior is termed the *interaction* between control loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> Interaction in a multivariable (MIMO) process occurs when changing one input variable, while holding all other inputs constant, produces a change in more than one output variable. This coupling of control loops is intrinsic to the process and must be quantified to design effective controllers.

Mathematically, for a steady-state gain matrix $K$ of an $n \times n$ MIMO system:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} & \cdots & k_{1n} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} & \cdots & k_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ k_{n1} & k_{n2} & \cdots & k_{kn} \end{bmatrix}$$

The off-diagonal elements $k_{ij}$ (where $i \neq j$) represent the **interaction gains** between loop $j$ and loop $i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Thermostat and the Crosswind

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control with Crosswind:**
> Imagine driving a tall, narrow vehicle (like a delivery truck) on a highway. Your primary control is the steering wheel, and your primary output is maintaining your lane (lateral position). However, a strong crosswind from the left **interacts** with your system: when you adjust your steering slightly to correct for the wind, the vehicle's speed (longitudinal velocity) is also affected because of the aerodynamic drag change.
> 
> In this analogy:
> - **Input 1 (Steering)** primarily affects **Output 1 (Lateral Position)**, but *also* affects **Output 2 (Speed)**.
> - **Input 2 (Throttle)** primarily affects **Output 2 (Speed)**, but *also* affects **Output 1 (Lateral Position)**.
> 
> This is **interaction** — the outputs cannot be controlled independently with a single-input single-output (SISO) mindset.

## 1.3 Why Interaction Matters in Engineering

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Industrial Relevance:**
> In chemical plants, oil refineries, distillation columns, and aerospace systems, severe interaction can:
> 1. Cause **loop instability** even when each SISO loop is individually stable.
> 2. Lead to **inverse response** (a counterintuitive effect).
> 3. Render **classical SISO controller tuning (e.g., Ziegler-Nichols) completely ineffective** if applied naively.
> 4. Force a complete redesign of the control architecture.

The standard metric to quantify interaction is the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)**, proposed by **Bristol (1966)**, and the **Niederlinski Index**, both of which are central to Module 4 of the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bristol Relative Gain Array (RGA) — Conceptual Foundation

The **Relative Gain** $\lambda_{ij}$ between input $j$ and output $i$ is defined as the ratio of two gains:

$$\lambda_{ij} = \frac{\left( \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial u_j} \right)_{\text{all loops open}}}{\left( \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial u_j} \right)_{\text{all other loops closed}}}$$

In words:

- **Numerator:** The open-loop gain — how $y_i$ responds to $u_j$ when **all other loops are open** (no other controller is acting).
- **Denominator:** The closed-loop gain — how $y_i$ responds to $u_j$ when **all other loops are perfectly closed** under integral control.

The matrix $\Lambda = [\lambda_{ij}]$ is called the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** All rows and all columns of the RGA sum to **1.0**.
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_{ij} = 1, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_{ij} = 1$$
> This implies that if $\lambda_{ij} = 1$, there is **no interaction** between loop $i$ and loop $j$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How to Compute the RGA

Given the steady-state gain matrix $K$ of a square MIMO system ($n \times n$):

1. **Element-wise multiplication** (Hadamard product) of $K$ with the transpose of its cofactor matrix.
2. **Divide each element** by the sum of that row (or column).
3. Equivalent compact form: $\Lambda = K \circ (K^{-1})^{T}$ where $\circ$ denotes the Hadamard (element-wise) product.

## 2.3 The Niederlinski Index

For a square $n \times n$ system, the **Niederlinski Index** is defined as:

$$NI = \frac{\det(K)}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} k_{ii}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** If $NI < 0$, the chosen pairing is **structurally unstable** and must be avoided. If $NI > 0$, the pairing is potentially viable, but the RGA should be checked for confirmation.

## 2.4 Interaction Measure

The **Steady-State Interaction Measure** (also called the *Bristol Array Interaction Index*) for a $2 \times 2$ system is:

$$\text{Interaction} = \frac{k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22}}$$

This single scalar captures the entire interaction in a $2 \times 2$ system. The RGA for a $2 \times 2$ system becomes:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_{11} & \lambda_{12} \\ \lambda_{21} & \lambda_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} & \frac{-k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \\ \frac{-k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} & \frac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference (Module 4 — Interactions)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Steady-state gain matrix $K$ | $K = [k_{ij}]_{n \times n}$ | $k_{ij} = \partial y_i / \partial u_j$ at steady state |
| 2 | Relative Gain $\lambda_{ij}$ | $\lambda_{ij} = \dfrac{(\partial y_i / \partial u_j)_{\text{open}}}{(\partial y_i / \partial u_j)_{\text{closed}}}$ | Bristol, 1966 |
| 3 | RGA via Hadamard product | $\Lambda = K \circ (K^{-1})^{T}$ | $\circ$ is element-wise product |
| 4 | Sum property of RGA | $\sum_i \lambda_{ij} = 1, \sum_j \lambda_{ij} = 1$ | All rows and columns sum to 1 |
| 5 | $2 \times 2$ RGA $\lambda_{11}$ | $\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}$ | Diagonal entry |
| 6 | $2 \times 2$ RGA $\lambda_{12}$ | $\lambda_{12} = \dfrac{-k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}$ | Off-diagonal entry |
| 7 | Interaction measure (2x2) | $\mu = \dfrac{k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22}}$ | Single scalar interaction index |
| 8 | Niederlinski Index (NI) | $NI = \det(K) / \prod_i k_{ii}$ | $NI < 0 \Rightarrow$ unstable pairing |
| 9 | Pairing rule | Choose $u_j - y_i$ pair with $\lambda_{ij}$ closest to **1** | Avoid negative $\lambda_{ij}$ pairings |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Field | Interaction Problem Addressed |
|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| Distillation columns | Reflux rate affects both top and bottom composition |
| Aircraft flight control | Aileron deflection affects roll, yaw, and sideslip |
| HVAC systems | Multiple zone temperature-humidity coupling |
| Power systems | Active/reactive power cross-coupling in generators |
| Robotic manipulators | Joint torques affect multiple end-effector coordinates |

## 2.7 Decoupling Control — Concept

To eliminate interaction, a **decoupler** (or *compensator*) $D(s)$ is inserted between the controller and the plant:

$$G_{\text{eff}}(s) = G(s) \cdot D(s) = \text{diagonal matrix}$$

There are two classical approaches:

- **Simplified (or *ideal*) decoupling:** $D = G^{-1}$ (when $G$ is invertible and stable).
- **Simplified decoupling for $2 \times 2$:** Pade approximation or direct inverse method.

The decoupler element for a $2 \times 2$ system:

$$d_{12}(s) = -\frac{g_{12}(s)}{g_{11}(s)}, \quad d_{21}(s) = -\frac{g_{21}(s)}{g_{22}(s)}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RGA for a $2 \times 2$ System

Let the steady-state gain matrix of a $2 \times 2$ process be:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Open-loop gain (Numerator):**
With all loops open, the output equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= k_{11} u_1 + k_{12} u_2 \\
y_2 &= k_{21} u_1 + k_{22} u_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The partial derivative of $y_1$ with respect to $u_1$ (with $u_2$ constant) is:

$$\left( \frac{\partial y_1}{\partial u_1} \right)_{\text{open}} = k_{11}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop gain (Denominator):**
Assume loop 2 is closed under perfect integral control. This means $y_2$ is held constant at its setpoint, so $dy_2 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dy_1 &= k_{11} du_1 + k_{12} du_2 \\
0   &= k_{21} du_1 + k_{22} du_2
\end{aligned}
$$

From the second equation, solve for $du_2$:

$$du_2 = -\frac{k_{21}}{k_{22}} du_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back** into the first equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dy_1 &= k_{11} du_1 + k_{12} \left( -\frac{k_{21}}{k_{22}} du_1 \right) \\
dy_1 &= \left( k_{11} - \frac{k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{22}} \right) du_1
\end{aligned}
$$

So the closed-loop gain becomes:

$$\left( \frac{\partial y_1}{\partial u_1} \right)_{\text{closed}} = \frac{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{22}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the relative gain $\lambda_{11}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{11} &= \frac{k_{11}}{\dfrac{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{22}}} \\
\lambda_{11} &= \frac{k_{11} \cdot k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \\
\lambda_{11} &= \frac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the sum-to-one property to get all four entries:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{12} &= 1 - \lambda_{11} = \frac{-k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \\
\lambda_{21} &= 1 - \lambda_{11} = \frac{-k_{12} k_{21}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \\
\lambda_{22} &= \lambda_{11} = \frac{k_{11} k_{22}}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final compact RGA:**

$$\Lambda = \frac{1}{k_{11} k_{22} - k_{12} k_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} k_{22} & -k_{12} k_{21} \\ -k_{12} k_{21} & k_{11} k_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: 2×2 Distillation Column

**Problem Statement:**
A binary distillation column has the following steady-state gain matrix relating the manipulated inputs (reflux $L$, vapor boil-up $V$) to controlled outputs (top composition $y_D$, bottom composition $x_B$):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the RGA, identify the best input-output pairing, and check Niederlinski stability.

**Step 1 — Compute the determinant of $K$:**

$$
\det(K) = (0.7)(0.6) - (0.3)(0.4) = 0.42 - 0.12 = 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K^{-1}$ (needed for Hadamard form):**

$$
K^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.30} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0000 & -1.0000 \\ -1.3333 & 2.3333 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Transpose of $K^{-1}$:**

$$
(K^{-1})^T = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0000 & -1.3333 \\ -1.0000 & 2.3333 \end{bmatrix}
$$
